It was in the high, clear light of an October afternoon, a light that, filtrating through the thin London haze as through some temper of smoked crystal, contrived to be at once tender and explicit, that Mrs. Verrier paused, in the very movement of placing her hand upon the bell of the great house in Lowndes Square, to wonder, not uniquely for the first time, whether she had come too soon or had already waited too long. The house, which, on other days, had seemed to present to the street an appearance almost aggressively mute—like the reserved face of some personage shrewdly conscious that his very silence might be misconstrued as arrogance—showed, this afternoon, an expression that to her nervous sense made it almost garrulous. The drawn blinds at the lower windows, revealing rather than concealing the glow of firelight within, the half-opened transom above the door, the trail of a carriage just vanishing at the corner, left upon the air that slight disturbance by which city life proclaims some recent arrival or impending departure. 

She had, in fact, the odd impression that she surprised the house in the attitude of being taken, as it were, into the confidence of events with which she herself, by a mere turn of the wrist upon the bell, must flagrantly interfere. It detained her for the appreciable instant—this flutter of consciousness—because, in a career that had not been without its occasions of minor audacity, she had seldom felt so much like one about to cross a threshold where every object would be obliged, by its very familiarity, to judge her.

Her hesitation, which was after all brief enough to have escaped any eye less finely constituted than her own, contained, however, a whole little drama of reference and recollection. If she was too early she must share, beyond the claims either of delicacy or of prudence, in preliminaries that, in this house, would be sure to have their delicate side; if she was too late, the event—if event there were—might have already assumed, in the intimacy of its first expression, some shape from which any later approximation of hers must seem coarse or irrelevant. She needed no reminder that the very quality of the place lay in its aptitude for making everything that there occurred appear intenser than elsewhere, more complete in its felicity or its failure, more charged with the suggestion that a step once taken could never, in this particular circle, be either avowed as tentative or confessed as mistaken.

Yet the hand, which had lingered on the polished plate, did, inevitably, at last perform its office, and the faint, far, domestic tinkle, multiplied within into a soft cascade of sound, assured her that, whatever she had anticipated by her arrival, she was now definitely committed to meet it. The door was opened with that immediate, unhurried exactness which had always seemed to her one of the finer moral notes of the establishment; no vulgar surprise, no flurry of inquiry, but only the large, neutral acceptance of the properly expected. The butler, whose age, like the house’s, might have been any one of several distinguished numbers, and whose acquaintance with her had progressed by imperceptible shades from the formality of “Madam” to the tempered intimacy of “Ma’am,” allowed his small, discreet recognition to flicker, as it were, behind the mask of his impassivity.

“Her ladyship is in the drawing-room, ma’am,” he said, as if this were somehow less a piece of information than a kind of consecration; and then, when Mrs. Verrier had divested herself of the very slight wrappings which the mildness of the day permitted, he preceded her, with the unrelaxed urbanity of his office, up the broad, still staircase, on which the muffled fall of their steps seemed another form of the slow ascent of events to consciousness.

It was on one of those afternoons of premature dusk, when the streets of New York turn to inland canals of shadow and the lamps, lit before their time, hang in the thick air like ship-lights lost in fog, that Clement Alder first became conscious of the change in his house.

The change—he was to remember afterward—had preceded his consciousness of it by several days; by as many, indeed, as had elapsed since Mrs. Bragdon’s last Thursday dinner, at which he had seen Isabel Fenno for the first time in ten years. But at the moment it did not present itself to him as the outcome of that encounter. It had, on the contrary, the air of something self-generated, of a phenomenon proper to the place, like the brownish exhalation that the old mahogany perpetually distilled under the gas-flare, or the faint medicinal odour which betrayed, in spite of the most scrupulous concealment, the fact that his Aunt Sophronia’s “room” gave directly on the back parlour.

He had lived in the Alder house for forty-five years, and for thirty of these he had lived in it alone with Aunt Sophronia. The house—one of a stiff black-brown row in the West Nineteenth Street region which his childhood had been taught to regard as the ultimate western march of civilization—had long since suffered the indignity of being called “downtown” by such of his contemporaries as had pushed their Penates northward beyond the Park. It had, however, remained to him, not only by the operation of his father’s will, but by the more intimate process of habit and association, the inevitable setting of his days. He had never thought of leaving it, any more than he had thought of changing his tailor or his club; and in return for his fidelity the house had repaid him with that air of unalterable acquiescence which certain places know how to assume toward those who ask nothing of them but that they should go on being themselves.

That, precisely, was why the change troubled him. It was not that anything had been done—the Alder house was constitutionally incapable of “improvement.” The same green rep curtains, bluely filmed by time and gas-smoke; the same Turkey carpet, whose barbaric pattern had been subdued to a kind of melancholy docility; the same albumen prints of ruined abbeys and living bishops—all these retained their habitual outlines, as the features of an old servant retain, under a succession of troubles, the carved expectant look acquired in the days when the troubles were still ahead. Yet, as he stood in the front drawing-room, his hand on the crinkled plush of the portière, Clement Alder had the sense that if he were to drop his hand and cross the threshold, he should find, not the back drawing-room he had known since childhood, but a room subtly other, charged with some meaning which had not been there yesterday.

He stood thus, arrested, his mild near-sighted gaze travelling from the convex gilt mirror above the mantel to the fluted legs of the marble-topped table, as if in search of some vulgar explanation—a moved chair, a mislaid book, Aunt Sophronia’s work-basket displaced from its canonical angle with the sofa-arm. But everything was in its place; it was only the place that had altered. The silence, usually thickened by the ticking of innumerable clocks, seemed now to listen rather than to speak; the very ticking had withdrawn into the background, like servants conscious of a presence in the room.  

It occurred to him, with a faint movement of annoyance at his own impressionability, that the change dated, absurdly enough, from the hour when, on Mrs. Bragdon’s hearth-rug, Isabel Fenno had said: “So you have never left the old house in Nineteenth Street?”

Now this is the story of Little Thom, who was not little in the matter of pride, nor in the matter of ears, but was little in the years of his age and the inches of his height. He came out to India in the cold weather with his mother and a box of tin soldiers, and he found that the tin soldiers stood up better under the sun than he did.

They lived in a long, low, white bungalow beyond the cantonments, with a compound too big for a boy and not half big enough for the sun. There were three mango trees that gave no mangoes, two plantains that gave no plantains, and a well that gave frogs. The frogs croaked in the hot evenings, and the punkah in the dining-room went creak—sigh—creak, and the chuprassies outside the verandah said, “Ji—ji—ji,” to one another all through dinner-time, as if they too were punkahs on a string.

Little Thom did not love his breakfast, dinner, and tea as much as English boys are supposed to do, for everything tasted faintly of dust and kerosene, and the milk-smell in the early mornings made him think of the cow with the wicked blue horns in the village, and that was not an improving thought. But he loved the hours between four and six o’clock in the afternoon, when his lessons were done and the world belonged to him, and the sun went down behind the line of peepul-trees like a tired ayah, very cross and very grand.

His mother said: “You must not go beyond the big gate, Thom. There are snakes, and tanks, and bullock-carts, and Heaven knows what besides.” Mothers in India say “Heaven knows what besides” so often that the phrase crawls about the verandahs like a lizard, and the children catch it as they catch prickly-heat.

So Little Thom promised very faithfully—for he was English—to stay within the compound and to make no acquaintances except the bearer, who washed him; the ayah, who scolded him; and the syce, who laughed at him. The bearer said, “Sahib is very small,” as if he blamed Thom for it. The ayah said, “Memsahib, he is not at all good child.” The syce only grinned and touched his forehead and said, “Chhota Sahib—bahut zor ka,” which means “The little master—of great strength,” and is the sort of lie that makes men faithful.

In this manner Thom lived his first hot month—dazed with light, freckled with mosquito-bites, and full of new words he was not allowed to use at tiffin. He drew pictures in the dust with a broken riding-cane; he drilled his tin soldiers under the shadow of the useless mango trees; and he watched the great kite-hawks swinging in circles so high that it hurt his eyes to follow them. Once he saw a jackal slink across the back of the compound at noon, thin as a lath and gray as gunmetal. He would have whistled to it, only that his mouth was full of bread-and-treacle, and jackals do not like treacle.

On the thirty-first evening—as nearly as can be reckoned in a land where the days melt together like ghee in a pot—something happened which the ayah afterwards called “shaitan ka khel”—the Devil’s play. But it was not the Devil’s play. It was only India, which is older than Devils or boys, and does not care very much for the one or the other.

gpt_opening:
It has been laid upon me, whether by fate or by that whimsical concatenation of small accidents which men dignify by the name of destiny, to pass my days in perpetual proximity to the odd and the significant. From the quiet rectory of my boyhood, where the ticking of the clock and the droning of the bees were the chief disturbances, to the thronged streets and smoky rafters of London, where a man may jostle a murderer at a crossing and never know it, my path has brought me into contact with persons and events which, though in themselves perhaps no more extraordinary than the common affairs of life, have gained a certain colour by the eye that observed them and the pen that must now record them.

In setting down these recollections, I am conscious of two opposing dangers. On the one hand, there is the temptation to place myself too often in the foreground of the canvas; on the other, to retire so completely to the wings that the reader is left with a mere procession of puppets without any visible wire. Middle age—if, indeed, I can make so optimistic a claim—has a way of sobering a man’s estimate of his own importance, and I will endeavour, therefore, to appear in these pages only so far as is necessary to explain how I came to be a spectator of such scenes as I shall describe.

It is, in any case, no heroic chronicle that I offer. The reader must not look here for the clang of sabres or the roll of drums, though I have heard both; nor yet for the intrigues of princes and ministers, though I have seen some of them at close quarters. Life, as it presents itself to my recollection, is made up of smaller things: a gesture at a fireside, a word spoken in anger and regretted in solitude, the closing of a door behind a departing figure. These are the true hinges upon which our destinies turn, though the historian, intent upon his treaties and campaigns, may never notice that they have moved at all.

My earliest memory is of a long, low room, ill-lit by two narrow windows, in which the panes—clouded by age and perpetually misted by the vapours of our damp climate—quivered faintly whenever the wind swept in from the moor. Upon a deal table lay a number of objects: a carpenter’s rule, a small brass telescope sadly out of repair, and, most impressive of all to my childish fancy, a human skull so discoloured by smoke that it might have been charred in some forgotten conflagration. This grim relic, which had once sat more decorously upon the shoulders of a Highland sergeant, was the cherished possession of my father, and it formed, as I now perceive, the first of a series of curious companions which have attended me through life.

My father himself was a tall, spare man, with the stoop of those who habitually bend over books, and the far-away glance of one who sees, beyond the commonplace furniture of the world, certain landscapes and architectures invisible to coarser eyes. He had, in youth, aspired to the Church, in middle life had been diverted into the quieter but not less anxious waters of schoolmastering, and in old age took refuge in a mild antiquarianism which expressed itself in the accumulation of cracked coins, broken weapons, and such bony testimonials to mortality as I have already mentioned. He would sit of an evening with the skull before him, his long fingers steepled under his chin, and discourse upon the vanity of human wishes with a particularity which, while it failed to depress my spirits, did much to stimulate my imagination.

It was in that room, with the wind rattling at the casement and the peat-smoke curling in fantastic shapes along the beams, that I first learned there is more in the world than meets the eye, and that behind every quiet object there may lurk a story, waiting only for the interrogatory of a sympathetic mind to step forth into the light.

gpt_opening:
It is a singular thing that I, who have never seen a ghost with my own eyes, should be obliged to set down the story of one that has ruined three men and driven a fourth into the arms of a Mission chaplain. I make no pretence of understanding the business. Certain wise persons in the Station say it was all drink; others, who are of a chilly temper and delight in horrors, declare that it was Judgment; and a third party, who have read books on the nerves, attribute everything to "reaction"—whatever that may mean—after the fatigues of a hot-weather campaign. 

My own belief is that it was only India—a brown, baked India, with her smells and her mirages, her vile dust and viler devils—playing the fool with a man who had outstared the sun and forgotten that he was but one poor European among many millions of heathen souls. However, I am not writing to convince. I am writing because I gave a promise to a dying man in the verandah of the Sudder Hospital, and dead men, even in this country where they are plentiful, have their rights.

The tale begins, as tales in this province so often do, with the railway; for the line runs like a steel whip across our District, and there is no knowing whom it will scourge into madness before it is done. If you follow it down from the north, past the salt-lakes and the ragged little villages that squat by their wells, you will come, after sundown, to a place called Chanchra Junction. It is not much to look at by daylight—a scorched platform, a red-brick station-house, a string of neem-trees faint-heartedly pretending to be a garden, and beyond all, the great, flat, empty country running away to nowhere. 

But at night—especially in the Rains—Chanchra is a different matter. Men who are accustomed to the solitude of the dak-bungalows will tell you that there is something wrong with the place. They complain of noises in the booking-office long after the clerks have turned in, of cold winds that creep under the doors when the air outside is like a wet cloth, and of a certain signal-lamp which burns green when it has no right to burn at all. The natives, who are not troubled with explanations, merely say: "There is a Presence by the east siding. It is an Angrèz ghost. Do not sleep there."

In the hot weather of ’87, before the big siding was laid down, there came to Chanchra one of those restless men from the Staff Corps, whose names are always on transfer-lists and whose duties nobody is ever able to define. His name was Lieutenant Charles Everard, and he had been sent, so the papers said, to "inspect and report upon certain temporary arrangements in connection with the transport of commissariat stores." What that meant in plain English was that he had been buried alive in the Junction for three months, with a clerk, a telegraphist, and such society as could be found among the truck-drivers and the bazar-people. 

I first met him there, one evening in June, when the sky was piled up, like a native bazar, with black clouds that smelt of rain and were full of bad tempers. He was sitting in a long cane chair at the far end of the platform, smoking as if he hated his pipe, and staring down the empty line towards the east, where nothing was to be seen but a smudge of heat and the trembling rails running into it. He did not hear me till my boots rang on the flagged floor; then he started like a man who has been shaken out of sleep, and I saw that his face was too white for one who had been three seasons in the country.

title: A Night in Acadie

The cypress trees had been standing there longer than any one could tell, their great gray arms lifted as if in perpetual surprise at the sky’s blueness. They kept a still, watchful look upon the bayou that slipped between them without a murmur, carrying brown leaves and stray feathers and sometimes a rotting log that turned slowly, as if reluctant to go where the current led.

On that afternoon, the light fell slantwise through the branches and made a trembling gold upon the water. A boat pushed its way softly along the shore, the oars dipping with the mildest splash, for the hand that held them was used to the work, and the mind that guided the hand was somewhere else.

Lucie Guidry was not thinking of the cypress trees, nor of the blue sky, nor even of the bayou that had been her playmate since she could walk. She was thinking of a little scrap of torn paper pinned that morning above the kitchen mantel: a notice, written in the curving hand of Père Michel, announcing in his careful French that on Sunday next there would be a benediction for the feast of Sainte Claire, patronne des jeunes filles, at the chapel of St. Joseph.

It was Thérèse who had brought the paper home, her cheeks red from the heat and the importance of her mission. “Pour toi, Lucie,” she had announced, pinning it there with more ceremony than was needed. “Sainte Claire is going to open your eyes, ma chère. She will show you your good husband.”

“A paper cannot show one anything,” Lucie had answered, turning the corn cakes with more attention than they called for. But all the same, she had read the lines more than once, feeling the words like a small, soft weight in her chest.

Now, in the boat, she let the oars rest and drifted where the water chose to take her. A dragonfly, slim and green as a new reed, balanced an instant on the gunwale and went its way. Farther up, where the bayou widened, she could see the broad, low roof of the Leblanc house glimmering between the trees, and it was there her thoughts went unwillingly, as a child goes to catechism.

The Leblanc place had once seemed to Lucie like something from the old tales her grandmother used to tell at night when the wind was high and the lamp burned low. It was the largest house on that stretch of water, with a deep gallery running the length of it, and white columns that caught the moonlight so that they looked almost fragile. When she was little she used to believe that the people who lived inside must speak in whispers and walk without letting their heels touch the floor.

But she had grown older, and the mystery had fallen away, as it does from most things. She had been there often now—too often, her mother said—helping Madame Leblanc with the sewing, sitting in the wide, cool hall to shell peas into a great blue bowl, listening to the soft murmur of women’s voices and the sharper intrusion of a man’s laugh from outside. Lucie knew the sound of that laugh as she knew the sound of her own name; she could have picked it out from a hundred others.

“Tiens, Lucie!” It was that same laugh now, breaking suddenly across the stillness and making a little echo move along the water. “You pass without even looking. Are you turning sauvage on us down there?”

She started, flushed, and took up the oars again; but the boat had already swung gently toward the Leblanc landing, and Jean-Baptiste Leblanc was standing there with his hands in his pockets, smiling as if the bayou itself belonged to him and she had been adrift on his indulgence.

gpt_opening:
DRAMATIS PERSONAE

SPIRIT OF THE YEARS  
SPIRIT OF THE PITIES  
SPIRIT IRONICAL  
SHADE OF A STATESMAN  
SHADE OF A SOLDIER  
VOICE OF THE MILLIONS  
CHORUS OF THE UNBORN  
CHORUS OF THE DISPOSSESSED  

SCENE I  
The dim interspace betwixt the Visible and the Conceived.  
A grey vacuity, unbounded, yet bearing within it the hint of
planetary orbits, of pale revolving discs, and of faintly
luminous threads that cross and recross like a world’s forgotten
spider-web.

[A low sound, as of the slow turning of a monstrous wheel; then
a throbbing, distant as of innumerable muffled drums.]

SPIRIT OF THE YEARS  
The wheel recommences. Its spokes renew  
Their ancient, unvaried round.  
Old shapes of grief,  
New-masked, arise from the reuse of dust;  
And still the Rulers, lifted on shoulders of dream,  
Lead forth their myriads to the misty verge  
Where Being thins to Nought.

SPIRIT OF THE PITIES  
And thou, that wheelest them—call’st thou this wise?  
Behold, the orb they tenant labours sore;  
Her soils are scarred, her waters inward shrink;  
And yet thy impetus, dumb, undiverted,  
Urges the footfall of nations to the brink  
Of gulfs unguessed, yet certain.

SPIRIT IRONICAL  
Nay, sweet sighing one,  
Let be thy litanies! The old jest stirs anew.  
Mark how they marshal—these ephemeral kings—  
With tapestries of words, and banners dyed  
In credos, codes, and colours of an hour,  
To claim as purpose what is but pretext  
Hung o’er the visage of Necessity.

SPIRIT OF THE YEARS  
It is my wont. Though I devise them not,  
I bear them; and the mass of marching fates  
Is but the push of all that has preceded—  
A blind bequest of unremembered wills.  
I waste no wonder that the play repeats;  
I count but sequences.

[The grey vacuity condenses faintly, as through a deep haze.  
A STRAGGLING CONTINENT is dimly descried, its outline tremulous,  
its seas low-lustering as in a dream before dawn.]

VOICE OF THE MILLIONS (as from beneath)  
Who set us here between the sky and clay,  
With hunger twinned to hope, and both unfed?  
We plough, we drill, we delve, we breed, we bleed,  
And know not why the tale returns to pain.

CHORUS OF THE DISPOSSESSED  
We built the towers where the Masters sit,  
We paved the roads where their chariots glide,  
Yet our portion is fog in the lungs,  
And an unmarked mound by a hedge-side.

SPIRIT OF THE PITIES  
The cry climbs ever, and is ever spurned.  
What gain, that knowledge widening like a dawn  
Steals round their ridges, if the ancient wrong  
Outpaces it, and darkens in the van?

SPIRIT IRONICAL  
Knowledge?—they use it but to barb old spears,  
To speed old envies with an engine’s haste,  
To shrill old hatreds through a wire’s thin nerve.  
They burn new oils beneath the same stale idols,  
And kneel more quickly, that they rise to kill.

[From the dim CONTINENT a FLARE of brightness spurts and fails,  
as of a distant city signalling with fire. Murmurs, then clearer  
voices, rise: the confused sound of parliamentary strife,  
street-cries, foundry-hammers, church-bells, and the whir of  
unseen machines.]

SHADE OF A STATESMAN (half-shaped in the gloom)  
Time tightens. Hear you how the ratchet clicks,  
Notched by accords and secret memorandum,  
By feints of peace that mask commercial wars,  
By ink that serves as prelude to the steel?  
They talk of balance, meaning but their share;  
They name it honour when they hoard their fear.

SHADE OF A SOLDIER (appearing beside him)  
And I, that thought myself their chosen arm,  
Glimpse now, through this thin death, my littleness.  
Observe them, Pities, as they pawn my clay:  
These ministering lips that shape the doom-word,  
And sign away, with silken pen and smile,  
A million hearths to frost.

SPIRIT OF THE YEARS  
So must it be, till spent potential cools,  
And the last heat of Will lies under ash.  
Events but rise from roots beyond themselves;  
The moment’s actors mouth ancestral scripts  
Penned in the primal dark.

CHORUS OF THE UNBORN (faintly, from far forward)  
Yet we attend,  
Suspended in the tissues of To-come,  
Unbreathed, unbaptized into their despair,  
Listening if any gentler writ be read  
Ere we are launched to suffer.

SPIRIT OF THE PITIES  
Let such writ rise!  
O veiled Intending, hush the engines now,  
Ere from these murmurs break the larger roar  
Whose echo shakes the centuries in their shell.

SPIRIT IRONICAL  
Hark!—even now the fuse is to the powder.

[Far off, as from multiple horizons, there steals the low rumble  
of MASSING ARMIES, mingled with martial airs ill-heard, and the  
rattling of trains heavy with unknown freight.]

SPIRIT OF THE YEARS  
We watch. The play proceeds, self-urged, self-wound.  
Soon shall that rumble bruise the conscious ear,  
And men shall name it by their several hates,  
Each calling “Destiny” what he has sown.

[Curtain of dusk thickens over the CONTINENT,  
leaving but a few pale stars burning in the waste.]

gpt_opening:
When Dinanath Babu retired from the Service—three months before his proper time, because of the lungs that had been spoilt in the Terai and the temper that had been spoilt in the Secretariat—his household, which lay four miles beyond Howrah, put on the outward signs of mourning and the inward habits of festival.

For thirty years he had departed each morning in a black _phaton_ hired by the month, with a bundle of Government papers under his arm, and returned, just as the jackals began to speak across the paddy, with the same bundle—slightly more rumpled and much less important. All the village knew that he was “Office-sahib,” which is to say, one who signs papers that other men obey. No one, not even Dinanath, quite understood what those papers accomplished. They were written in English, and sometimes, when the cough was bad in the cold weather, he would sit with a lamp and translate a paragraph or two for the edification of his eldest son. Then Suren would say: “But what comes of it at the last, Baba?” and Dinanath would answer, as all who serve a Government answer: “Another paper.”

He had not been a great man in the Service. There are men whose very boots creak with authority, whose pens cut like tulwars, and whose signatures leave marks deeper than their ink. Dinanath was not of these. He was exact, punctual, frugal of speech, and wonderfully patient with fools in power. This last quality brought him promotion more slowly than any other; for the English overlook a good many virtues in a subordinate, but they have a faint distrust of a patience that is more enduring than their own.

So, when the cough grew troublesome and the English doctor at the Eden Hospital said: “Better get to a warm place, my friend, and don’t come back in April,” Dinanath read in that advice the last marginal note on his own career. He applied for leave preparatory to retirement, and, to his deep amazement, it was granted without fuss. Government can be swift when it has no further use for a man.

The black _phaton_ ceased to come. The peon with the silver badge on his belt no longer loitered under the pipal-tree, nor shouted “_Rasta! Rasta!_” to clear way for the babu who had once been so confident of his rung on the invisible ladder. The brass office-box, with its faded red paint and its hasp worn bright by thirty years of locking and unlocking, lay under Dinanath’s cot collecting a new sort of dust.

It is a dangerous thing for an Indian, who has been half an Englishman all his working life, suddenly to become wholly Indian again. He is like a man turned out of an old, tight-fitting coat into a garment that he wore as a child and has outgrown unawares. There are unexpected constrictions under the arm-pits and draughts in the back; besides certain forgotten pockets where curious things may have been left.

Dinanath’s forgotten pocket was his caste.

title: No Name
prompt: Write the first page of a book in the style of Wilkie Collins's No Name. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first page is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first page yourself.
gpt_opening:
The history of the Verinder family (for so the neighbors persisted in calling them, although a legal-minded man might have objected to the accuracy of that title) begins, for our present purpose, on a certain dull November afternoon, in the drawing-room of a small house at Brompton.

The house stood in a row of other houses—each so like its fellow in brick, in stucco, and in that peculiar air of dingy gentility which seems inseparable from a London suburb, that you could only distinguish Number Twenty from Number Nineteen by counting the doors. Within, the resemblance to similar habitations ended abruptly. There are rooms which bear witness to the character of their owners as plainly as if the chairs had tongues and the tables kept diaries. The Verinders’ drawing-room was one of these rooms; and the testimony it offered was of the least agreeable kind.

It had the aspect of a place in which people had lived long together who were thoroughly tired of one another, and had not the means of separate existence. The chairs placed themselves in prim, uncomfortable attitudes, like old-maid aunts at a marriage which does not concern them; the sofa stretched its faded chintz with a martyr’s resignation; the curtains hung limply as if weary of looking out on the same blank opposite wall year after year. The carpet had been new in a vanished age of prosperity; it retained, in patches, a melancholy recollection of flowers and devices, much as the master of the house retained a melancholy recollection of earlier hopes and vanished guineas. 

Mr. Julian Verinder sat at the table nearest the fire, and pretended to read a newspaper which he steadily perused upside down. In his youth—for he had once possessed that fleeting advantage which Time bestows only that he may the more cruelly withdraw it—he might have passed for a handsome man. The wreck of that pretension still floated about him in the shape of fine eyes, now grown listless, and of regular features, now crossed and re-crossed by those small fretful lines which disappointment graves on the face more surely than sorrow. His thin, well-formed hands were restlessly employed in folding and refolding the corner of the journal. There are men who can mislay a fortune with resignation, but who cannot mislay the smallest fragment of their self-importance without betraying the loss in every movement. Mr. Verinder belonged indisputably to the latter and more numerous class.

At the farther end of the room, near the window, Miss Isabel Verinder sat at work—professedly at work, I should say. The object in her hand bore the outward semblance of embroidery; the object in her mind—judging by the idle dropping of the needle and the frequent upward glance at the leaden sky—was evidently of a more speculative nature.

Isabel was in that dangerous interval of a woman’s life which lies between nineteen and twenty-three years of age, when she is old enough to perceive her bondage and young enough to be indignant at it. Her dress was of the simplest kind, but was worn with a certain decisive arrangement not commonly acquired in a house where the bills are paid with difficulty, and the milliner is admitted by the back door. Her eyes were too steady for prettiness and too honest for beauty of the fashionable order; the mouth, restless even when silent, betrayed a temper more ready to speak than to submit. The hair—which a romantic novelist would, I suppose, describe as raven-black or golden; but which I, being a plain man, am content to declare as dark and abundant—refused to keep the position prescribed by the comb, and thereby offered the first intimation of the character which the features afterwards confirmed. 

It was Isabel who broke the silence.

“Papa,” she said, letting the coloured silk slip unheeded from her fingers, “how much longer are we to go on in this way?”

Mr. Verinder started, turned his newspaper right side upwards, and confronted his daughter with that expression of mild irritation which a domestic question invariably produced in him, as being a species of inquiry to which his whole life had unfitted him to give an answer.

gpt_opening:
In a green, low-lying meadow, where the river curled itself like a silver ribbon upon a lady’s gown, there lived a very small and not-at-all remarkable field mouse whose name was Minna. The daisies did not bow to her when she passed, the clover did not whisper her secrets, and even the tall spear-grasses, who had the kindest manners in all the meadow, scarcely noticed when she brushed by their ankles on her way to market.

Now this might not have mattered to some mice, who are content if their cupboards are full and their whiskers kept properly in order; but it mattered very much indeed to Minna. For Minna had once overheard a lark remark (and larks, you know, are great authorities in matters of importance, because they go very high and see very far):

“There goes the little brown nobody,” the lark had sung to his wife, as Minna trotted beneath.

The words fell from the sky like thin, sharp icicles and pierced Minna’s small, soft heart. Ever since that morning she had gone about with a prickled feeling inside, as if some invisible thistle had taken root where her courage ought to have grown.

“It is a dreadful thing,” she said to herself one dewy dawn, as she polished her whiskers on a clean blade of grass, “to be a little brown nobody. I am as brown as the earth and as small as a sigh. If I were as white as the daisies, or as red as the berries, or as golden as the buttercups, the lark would never have said such a cruel, high-up thing.”

She peeped over the rim of her doorway—a neat hole beneath a flat stone—and watched the day waking itself. The sun was just pushing aside a cloud, very politely asking its pardon, and touching everything with his long yellow fingers. The buttercups shone as if they had been newly polished, the forget-me-nots put on their bluest eyes, and even the nettles held their stings rather proudly in the light.

Minna looked at herself instead. She was brown from nose to tail-tip, a modest, dusty sort of brown, like an old acorn that had missed its chance to become a tree. Her ears were small and her eyes sensible and dark, though no one had ever told her that they were bright, because no one had ever troubled to look closely enough to see.

“It is settled,” she whispered—for when one is about to do something very foolish or very brave, it is best to whisper rather than shout—“I shall make myself noticeable. I shall become as pretty as the meadow itself, and then the lark shall be obliged to sing of me as ‘somebody’ from the top of the sky.”

Having made this very serious promise to her own heart, Minna tied up her provisions for the day—a crumb of yesterday’s bread and three and a half seeds (for she had been too anxious to finish the fourth)—and set forth along the narrow path which all the small folk of the meadow used when they wished to become more than they were. It led, as such paths generally do, straight into trouble.

title: The Confidence-Man: His Masquerade

gpt_opening:
At dawn of a certain year, which, not being signalized in the annals of nations, may, for our purpose, remain as nameless as some poor foundling left at the door of Time, a steamboat, gaudily christened *The Reputable*, lay moored at the levee of a city that did not always deserve its river. It was a city large enough to cherish illusions and yet small enough to recognize, without confessing, its dupes; a capital of compromise, where merchants quoted probabilities as others do prices, and where Truth herself, if she ever passed that way, would have done well to travel under an alias.

It was early—so early that the gas-lamps along the wharf, conscious of their own untimeliness, still burned with that dim, embarrassed glow which lingers about all things detected in the act of overstaying. The river, unconsulted, moved on with its customary composure, bearing upon its brown, indifferent back those ingenious devices by which men propose to amend the sluggishness of Providence. Rafts, barges, and boats of newer show and older debt rocked against their moorings like dreamers disturbed by the uneasy conscience of the tide.

Among these, *The Reputable* made peculiar claims upon the eye. Painted with that cheap luxuriance which is the poor man’s impression of the rich man’s taste, she presented, upon nearer inspection, something of that same contrast which has been found between professions and performances, between the prospectus and the dividend, between the front of a show-man’s wagon and the tent behind it. Her paddle-boxes, ornamented with gilded wreaths which a single hard season had sufficed to tarnish, bore in large, defiant letters the legend: “SAFETY, CERTAINTY, DISPATCH”—a trinity which the river, with its mutinous sand-bars and covert snags, has long since learned to regard with the pious incredulity it accords to sermons preached on stormy Sundays.

To-day, the first of April, *The Reputable* was to depart upon one of those excursions, half commercial, half convivial, by which the West essays to blend business with pleasure and commonly comes short in both. Already a motley queue of passengers—citizens and strangers, veterans of travel and raw adventurers—had begun to string itself along the gangway, each bearing, beside the inevitable carpet-bag or trunk, a private bundle of hopes, apprehensions, and small speculative schemes; those invisible impedimenta of the spirit by which men are more truly encumbered than by any quantity of tangible leather or brass.

It has often been observed—chiefly by those who have never left home—that a man on the eve of a journey is a sort of amateur philosopher. So it might have been remarked here, had any one been in the mood to observe closely, that the expressions upon these river-bound faces ranged all the way from the sanguine vacancy of him who expects everything because he has done nothing to deserve it, down to the guarded shrewdness of him who, having once bought experience at retail, now retails it, with profit, to the unsuspecting. Meanwhile, invisible to all these self-centered solitaries, Fate—who is not above traveling second-class when she has business on the boat—had already secured her passage.

author: Thomas Hardy
title: A pair of blue eyes
prompt: Write the first page of a book in the style of Thomas Hardy's A pair of blue eyes. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first page is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first page yourself.
gpt_opening:
A light wind, as irresolute as a thought half-formed, came and went along the western cliff, bringing with it the tang of salt and a suspicion of rain that had not yet made up its mind to fall. The sea, far below, was a sheet of dim steel, unpolished at the edges and blurred here and there, as if some giant hand had pressed its fingers upon the surface and left faint circles widening to nothing. Morning had broken an hour ago, but the sun remained a promise rather than a performance; he was still negotiating with a bank of clouds that had risen early to dispute his rights.

Upon the utmost verge of this cliff a narrow footpath had been worn rather by obstinacy than by traffic. The ledge, too slender to be called a field and too wide to be dignified as a wall, ran between a stone fence on the landward side and a drop on the other that discouraged curiosity. Here and there the cliff-face broke into ragged teeth, and tufts of small, persistent plants clung in the crannies as if they had been blown there and afterward had thought better than to remove.

Along this path walked a girl who appeared to trust both the path and the precipice more than she did the day. She held her skirts close with one hand, a futile defence against the wind that had no manners in the presence of a petticoat; in the other she carried a book, the covers of which were bent back upon themselves, not by long reading, but because she had lately discovered earnestness and supposed that earnestness required such treatment of literature.

Her name in the parish register was Anne Vale, though the parish, fond of improvement in its own rustic way, had compressed her to “Nan” whenever it spoke of her at all. She was about nineteen in years and forty in opinions—those opinions, it may be added, having been lately supplied to her from the outside world, and therefore being received by her as a personal discovery. Her eyes were of that undecided colour which, in the absence of poetry, would be called grey, but which a charitable observer might liken to the sea under a cloud; they had the same habit of deepening without warning and brightening without cause.

She paused where the path widened a little, as if the earth here had taken breath after the long strain of keeping itself out of the Atlantic. From this point the coast stretched north and south in a series of retreats and advances that looked less like the work of the tides than of some huge animal’s experimental bites at the land. Boats crawled in the distance—black ants on a strip of pewter—and a single gull moved steadily against the wind with the air of having business elsewhere.

Anne opened her book and tried to read, for her present philosophy assured her that feeling ought always to be accompanied by study. But the letters danced obligingly out of their rows and formed themselves into small white cottages, brown hedges, and the familiar bend of a lane. She shut the volume with a soft impatience.

“I wish,” she said to the cliff, which had more patience for wishes than the people inland, “that something would happen that has never happened before.”

title: Three Ghost Stories
prompt: Write the first page of a book in the style of Charles Dickens's Three Ghost Stories. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first page is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first page yourself.
gpt_opening:
It has sometimes appeared to me, in the quieter intervals of life, when the fire burns low and steadily and the clock’s ticking grows so distinct that it seems to be measuring one’s thoughts as well as one’s time, that certain occurrences which I once accounted exceptional might, with a less instructed judgment, be dismissed as the mere trickery of nerves. Yet I am not so young now as to be easily persuaded that what I have beheld with my own eyes, heard with my own ears, and suffered in my own mind, was no more than a vagary of the blood or a confusion of the brain. The reader is at liberty to form his own conclusion. I have none to force upon him. I only ask that, while I relate with as much plainness and exactness as my memory will allow, he will be pleased to believe that I am neither romancing for his diversion, nor magnifying a small and natural incident into something monstrous and supernatural.

The house in which the events I am about to set down took place, stood, and I am told still stands, at the extremity of a short and rather ill-favoured street, which seems always to have been struggling to conceive a better condition for itself, and always to have languished and fallen back again into its first dispirited intention. There was a melancholy in its very paving-stones. The narrow pavement halted abruptly at intervals, as if discouraged from going on; the lamps—two only between the corner public-house and my aunt’s door—shed a feeble and yellow light, which suggested more darkness than it dispersed; and the houses, though not old enough to be venerable, were too much sunk and stained and out of repair to be considered merely modern. They had already entered upon that middle age of houses in which they look as if they had seen nothing very remarkable themselves, but could testify to a long course of dingy and disappointed living.

My aunt’s dwelling closed the prospect with a dull obstinacy. It had no bow-window, no cheerful show of curtains, no flower-pots, no bright knockers to redeem its front. Two lean stone steps—no doubt once white—led up to a blistered door, on which the paint had broken out into an eruption of little bubbles, as though the wood beneath were suffering from some internal malady. Over the door was a fanlight of so begrimed a character that it might have been mistaken at dusk for a blind eye, permanently turned upwards to the scowling sky. A narrow slit of a window to the right, sunk lower than the pavement, served the kitchen. Two more windows, one above the other, made all the front could boast in the way of features; yet, in spite of this meagreness, there was in the house a certain reserved air, a kind of decayed propriety, which seemed to say that, if it was poor, it had not always been so, and that, whatever else might be questioned, its respectability never could.

Into this retreat I was received, on the death of my parents, by my aunt, Miss Lydia Rook, whose name I never write or utter without recalling the sharp outline of her spare figure against the blackness of that house, and the strange association which connected her, in my mind, with the first terror of my life. For it was in that house, under her roof and within her knowledge—though not, as I shall take pains to show, with her consent—that I came, for the first and, I hope, the last time, into immediate and conscious communication with what I must, in default of a more reasonable term, continue to call a ghost.

title: A Journey to the Centre of the Earth

gpt_opening:
On the 4th of May, 18—, a singular incident took place in the quiet little house which my uncle, Professor Theophilus Hartmann, inhabited in the Albrechtstrasse, at the extremity of the old quarter of Hamburg. I record this date with scrupulous fidelity, for it marks the precise instant when the whole course of my life was violently deflected from the modest, tranquil orbit I had traced for it, and flung—without consultation or consent—into the most prodigious adventure of our age.

Until that memorable afternoon, nothing could have been more regular than my existence. I was then twenty-three years of age, of a sufficiently serious disposition, and entirely resigned to the humble rôle of amanuensis and assistant to my illustrious uncle, whose name was already pronounced, not without respect and sometimes with a certain terror, in the learned societies of Germany and Scandinavia. His lectures on comparative mineralogy filled the halls of the Johanneum; his dissertations on volcanic phenomena left no repose to the subterranean fires of Iceland; his temperament, volcanic in its turn, admitted of neither contradiction nor delay.

This extraordinary man, small in stature, spare of frame, with irregular features seamed by innumerable wrinkles, and keen gray eyes that flashed behind his steel spectacles like sparks struck from a flint, was the very embodiment of restless intelligence. He spoke abruptly, walked rapidly, thought with precipitation, and, like certain chemical substances which explode on the slightest contact, passed in an instant from calm to fury. To live near him was to inhabit the vicinity of a powder magazine, where the least imprudence might give rise to a formidable detonation.

On that day, the professor had shut himself up in his study from an early hour. I heard him opening and shutting drawers, dragging chairs, moving piles of books, and uttering from time to time those inarticulate exclamations which, in him, might indicate either the highest satisfaction or the most bitter disappointment. I was prudently occupied in the library, classifying a new series of fossils just arrived from Bohemia, when a cry, half triumphant, half menacing, rent the air and made me start so violently that a venerable ammonite escaped my fingers and broke upon the floor.

“Axel!” howled my uncle, with the formidable accent which caused the bravest of his students to tremble in their benches.

I did not dare to let him repeat the summons. Leaving the scattered fragments of the fossil to their irreparable fate, I hastened to the door of the study and entered, not without a certain inward agitation.

The room, encumbered as usual with heaps of pamphlets, specimens, and instruments, had that air of sublime confusion by which true genius loves to manifest its sovereignty over material things. Yet, despite the apparent disorder, I perceived at once that something extraordinary had occurred. The professor stood in the middle of the apartment, his hair more disheveled than usual, his spectacles pushed up upon his forehead, his cheeks flushed with excitement. In his hand he brandished, like a banner wrested from the enemy, an old volume, bound in worm-eaten calf, whose yellowed pages seemed to protest feebly against such violent treatment.

“Axel,” he repeated, in a voice that strove to be calm and succeeded only in becoming more formidable, “do you see this book?”

“I see it, uncle,” I replied, submitting with resignation to the fatal necessity of seeing whatever he wished me to see.

“You do not yet know,” he resumed, bringing his face so close to mine that his spectacles nearly touched my nose, “into what abysses these pages are about to plunge us.”

author: George Eliot
title: George Eliot's Life, as Related in Her Letters and Journals. Vol. 1 (of 3)
prompt: Write the first page of a book in the style of George Eliot's George Eliot's Life, as Related in Her Letters and Journals. Vol. 1 (of 3). Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first page is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first page yourself.
gpt_opening:
In attempting to set before the reader the inward history of a life which, in its outward circumstances, was almost destitute of striking incident, I have been guided less by any hope of satisfying a general curiosity than by the conviction that those who care to know something of the growth of a mind must be content to follow it patiently through its doubtful, often circuitous, course. The materials here employed—letters, fragments of diaries, and such recollections as have been entrusted to me by early companions—are of that homely and familiar kind which, to an inattentive observer, might wear the aspect of triviality. Yet it is in these very trifles that the character habitually unveils itself; the chosen phrases of affection, the recorded scruples, the half-humorous, half-mournful comments upon the day’s small failures, tell us more than many stately professions.

The subject of this memoir, whom I shall, for convenience, designate by the initials A. M., was not, during her lifetime, the object of wide-spread notice. Those who knew her best would have been the last to expect that her story should hereafter be thought worthy of extended narration. She herself, if some prophetic glimpse had disclosed to her that these private utterances would one day be submitted to public inspection, would, I believe, have shrunk with a peculiar pain from such a prospect. It will therefore be understood that this volume is not the fruit of any wish of her own, expressed or implied, but of the affectionate desire, on the part of those most nearly concerned in her memory, that the spiritual experience which found but a broken and reluctant utterance while she lived should not utterly pass into oblivion with her death.

My task has been rendered at once more easy and more difficult by the very nature of the documents placed at my disposal. A. M. wrote much, but she wrote without the smallest care for the preservation of her writings. Her letters were composed hastily, often under a pressure of bodily discomfort and mental depression, and, once dispatched, seem to have vanished completely from her thoughts. In several instances, as the reader will see, she repeats, in different language, the same confession of error, the same trembling aspiration after a nobler life, evidently forgetful that she had already committed the like sentiments to paper. This spontaneous reiteration, however, is in itself significant; it indicates not poverty of feeling, but rather the recurrence of a burdened conscience to the same points of self-accusation and of hope.

Of the journals, a still more fragmentary account must be given. They consist, for the most part, of small, disjointed entries—dates, brief summaries of books read, scanty notes of visits paid or received, here and there a more extended comment on some moral perplexity or religious impression. The very brevity of these passages, their frequent abrupt beginning and ending, has imposed upon me the necessity of inserting connective remarks, where, without such aid, the sense would have remained obscure. I have, however, been scrupulous to mark the limits of my own intervention, and to distinguish, by typographical and other obvious means, between the words of A. M. and any explanatory bridge which I have ventured to throw over the intervals of silence in her record.

If the picture thus obtained is in many respects incomplete, it is nevertheless, I trust, faithful so far as it goes. We must be content to know a life as we know a landscape, through those broken vistas which accident or choice permits; only remembering that the hidden portions are of the same nature as the seen, and that the character which reveals itself in one sincere line of self-disclosure is not likely to be contradicted by the unwritten remainder.

author: Louisa May Alcott
title: An Old-Fashioned Girl
prompt: Write the first page of a book in the style of Louisa May Alcott's An Old-Fashioned Girl. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first page is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first page yourself.
gpt_opening:
Miss Charity Gray first came to the city on a blustering afternoon in March, when the wind seemed to have taken it upon itself to sweep winter and all its shabby remnants out of the streets with one great housekeeping. It roared round corners, rattled loose shutters, whisked old papers up to the very chimneys, and set every flag and bonnet-string a-flutter, as if determined that no one should go about that day with dignity or composure.

Charity sat very straight in the corner of the great railway carriage, with both hands folded upon a small basket that had evidently seen better days. The basket contained her worldly treasures—as much of them, at least, as she thought proper to bring with her to Aunt Honoria’s; a clean apron or two, a worn but much-cherished book, a little roll of knitting, and a modest parcel tied in brown paper, which had been charged with so many messages and good wishes that it seemed heavier than its small weight could account for.

She was not pretty in the way the city understands prettiness, having neither the delicate pallor nor the elaborate curls which fashion then considered the only proper setting for a girl’s face. But the clear color that came and went in her cheeks, the bright, honest eyes which saw everything and forgot nothing, and the firm little mouth that looked as if it had learned early to do hard things without much complaining, made certain people turn for a second look; and if those certain people happened to be of the sensible kind, they generally said to themselves, “There is a girl one could trust.”

Charity, however, thought very little about her own appearance, being fully occupied in trying to look as if she were quite used to coming to the city alone, and did it every day of her life. Inside, her heart beat rather fast, and a voice which was very like her mother’s kept saying, in a trembling sort of way, “Oh, my dear, I do hope they will be kind to you!” Whenever that inward voice grew too anxious, Charity remembered the last words spoken at the little gate, where the snow still lingered, and a flock of hens had huddled disconsolately to see the carriage off.

“You’ll find temptation there, child,” her father had said, looking down at her over his spectacles in the grave way that made his smile, when it came, the more comfortable. “All sorts of gimcracks and follies, and fine folks that think more of the fashion of their hats than of the state of their hearts. But mind you keep your Sunday face on week-days, and wear your conscience as some people wear their watches—where you can get at it easily.”

Then her mother, with eyes that were dry for fear of making Charity’s wet, had tied the faded blue ribbon closer round the girl’s throat, and whispered, “Remember whose child you are, Charry. You needn’t be ashamed of your home-made frock if you keep a home-kept heart.”

Thinking of these things, Charity sat straighter than ever, though the carriage gave a lurch that sent a whole row of bonnets nodding like dingy flowers in a gale. A stout lady opposite, who had watched the girl’s serious little face with some interest, ventured a remark upon the weather; but Charity answered so briefly, and colored so much at the sound of her own voice, that the good soul supposed her shy, and inwardly resolved that city manners were a great deal more distant than she had been wont to believe.

Presently there was a shrill whistle, a great outcry of “Tickets! tickets!” everybody woke up at once, and, with much bustling, the train came to a stop in the very heart of the great, noisy, unknown place that was henceforth to have so much to do with Miss Charity Gray’s fortunes.

author: Anthony Trollope
title: The Way We Live Now
prompt: Write the first page of a book in the style of Anthony Trollope's The Way We Live Now. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first page is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first page yourself.
gpt_opening:
Whether the reader will think that the story here to be told be worth his trouble must depend very much upon the reader’s own taste for contemplating the confused and uneasy prosperity of our present years. There are, I am aware, those good people—ladies chiefly, and perhaps some clergymen—who declare, with much soft vehemence, that they are weary of hearing how the world runs to its ruin through joint-stock companies and railway debentures; that they would be better pleased to have their pages filled with moonlight and fidelity, or with the sorrows of governesses who never balance an account more difficult than that between hope and resignation. But the world in which such readers would fain sojourney has retired from the field, leaving in its stead a new and bustling arena, in which shares and schedules contend more loudly than ever love and virtue contended in those elder days.

No man who lives with his eyes open can deny that we have created, by means of ink, paper, and a certain elastic facility in the use of figures, a nobility more immediate in power than any that grew out of Norman lances or Tudor benefices. It is not the Duke now who shakes the country with his caprices, unless indeed he be a director; it is not the old territorial gentleman, ensconced amidst timbered avenues, whose habits determine the comfort of small people living twenty miles around him; but Mr. This or Mr. That, whose name, written in a bold round hand at the foot of a prospectus, may to-morrow lay low the prudent savings of a county town, and next week set afloat a yacht on the Mediterranean worthy of the old Venetian days. 

In such a world as this our friend Mr. Septimus Blake flourished; or, to speak more properly, appeared to flourish, which, to the eye of London, is nearly as good. Flourishing, in these times, consists less in the possession of solid things than in that assured air by which a gentleman proclaims that if such things be not his to-day they unquestionably will be his on the next settlement. Mr. Blake understood this art to perfection. He was a man of forty-five—or of thirty-eight, or of fifty-two, as the conjecturer might prefer—of a tall person which he carried with a negligent bravery very effective in the lobbies of clubs and the ante-rooms of directors. He wore his hair a little longer than fashion strictly demanded, and had a habit of leaning upon the backs of chairs with a familiarity that persuaded weaker men they must have known him intimately for years.

It is possible that some severe moralist, if any such shall be at the pains to follow our history, may declare that Mr. Blake had risen too far in the world without the purchase-money of labour. This is a hard saying, and requires that we should first define labour. Is it nothing to sit for six hours in a dingy board-room, listening with decorous gravity while an industrious secretary reads aloud columns of figures which no man present intends to believe? Is it no exertion to dine nightly at late hours in suffocating rooms, smiling resolutely the while upon underdone salmon and overdone compliments? They who think such toil easy should make trial of it before they condemn the class of men with whom Mr. Blake had thrown in his fortunes.

On a certain gloomy evening in November, when the lamps in Piccadilly had been lighted early, that the city might the better see its own splendour through the drizzle, Mr. Septimus Blake turned out of the Athenaeum into the street, and paused for a moment under the portico, as though considering upon which portion of the wealth and respectability of London he would first bestow the favour of his presence.

title: The Deerslayer
