gpt_opening:
It was towards the decline of a sultry afternoon, in the latter part of the last century, when two men might have been seen slowly pursuing a narrow trace that wound, with many a turn and uncertain inclination, through one of those vast American forests which, at that distant day, so overspread the interior as to leave the frequent traveller in doubt whether the dominion of man would ever wholly dislodge the older and more stubborn empire of the wilderness. The sun, though still high above the dark and waving line of the western hills, sent his rays aslant among the heavy foliage, where they glimmered in broken shafts of golden light, touching here a trembling leaf, there a swelling bole, and anon dying away in the almost midnight gloom of the deeper glens, like the flickering of some inconstant beacon seen across a troubled waste of waters.

The path followed a ridge of elevated land that stretched, in a long, undulating back, between two basins of swamp and thicket; and though it bore those faint markings of human passage which the experienced woodsman seizes with the accuracy of an instinct rather than the labor of an observation, it was yet so faint and erratic as to be little more than the vestige of some wild creature’s accustomed run. The soil was strown with the brown and matted relics of many seasons; and the feet of the travellers, sinking noiselessly among the decayed leaves and mouldering twigs, gave forth no sound more distinct than that soft, smothered rustle which the ear, long schooled to the language of the forest, learns to interpret with the certainty of plainer speech.

Of the two, he who trod foremost was a man in the flower of a robust and sinewy manhood; one on whom hard service and a life passed chiefly in the open air had wrought their usual effects of bronzing the cheek, sharpening the outline of the features, and imparting to the whole frame that mixture of flexibility and strength which so well fits the human form for toil and endurance, when these are to be borne in scenes that hold as many dangers as they do privations. His dress, though of materials that had once been fashioned according to the habits of the settlements, had long since taken on that wild and mongrel character which marks the borderer, and which may be said to form a visible link between civilised and savage life. The colour of his hunting-shirt had once been that of some coarse and serviceable cloth; but time, exposure, and certain rude efforts at repair, made with the materials which chance threw in his way, had reduced it to a tint that might, at need, have rivalled the fading hues of the forest itself, when the frosts of autumn begin to work their silent transformations.

His companion walked a pace or two behind, less from any deference that one might be supposed to yield to the other, than from that habit of caution which, in those unsettled regions, prompted the wary to leave even these trifling advantages to such chances as might arise from the disposition of the ground. He was the elder by many years; the silver already showing itself in locks that had, doubtless, been once as dark as the wing of the raven, and the lines, traced by care rather than time, having begun to furrow a brow that yet retained the calm, unyielding cast which so often marks the man born for command, whether his authority be exercised amid the trim fields of a peaceful husbandry, or along the uncertain line where the clearings of the pioneer contend inch by inch with the sullen grandeur of the wild.

For some minutes neither spoke. Their faculties, indeed, appeared too much engaged in that silent communing with all around them which becomes, in such scenes, not merely a habit but a species of necessity. At length, the younger of the two suffered his keen, gray eye to turn from its jealous scrutiny of the obscure depths on either hand, and, casting a glance over his shoulder, he suffered a low remark to escape him, modulated rather to suit the jealous ear of the forest than to meet the grosser demands of human hearing.

author: Henry James
title: The Jolly Corner
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Henry James's The Jolly Corner. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
It was characteristic of him that, after an absence which his oldest acquaintance in the town would, on looking back, have found it difficult to measure save by the loss of his own hair and the gain of his grandchildren, he should return at an hour and in a manner that contrived, almost perversely, to elude remark. The steamer had come up the harbor in a cold, clean light of late afternoon, that washed the facades and cornices and thin chimneys of the city with a distinctness which, to his sharpened perception, gave them the air of having been freshly put there in his honor. Yet he disembarked with an attentive quiet, stepped into the first conveyance presented to him with the indifference of a man long practised in crossings, and permitted himself no gesture of recognition, no audible exclamation, as the vehicle conveyed him, over the clatter of stones and the more muffled tumult of wheels, into that quarter which had once been his own.

He had, at fifty-two, the look of a person whose life has been largely a matter of choosing not to be looked at. There was in his lean, well-preserved face a conscious absence of appeal; one guessed the discipline with which he had disaccustomed his features from advertising their contents. He had gone away young, with a certain quick, apprehensive sensibility blinking in his eyes, and had returned with that same sensibility narrowed and sharpened, like the blade of a knife too long and too carefully ground to waste itself on the common loaf. His name—though this plays, for the moment, a part almost indecently subordinate to his consciousness of having a name—was Bernard Latham; and the oddity of his case, as he himself would have been the first to admit, had always lain less in what he possessed than in what he had, almost with an amateurish zeal, renounced.

The carriage had turned now into a side-street which, even before his departure, had been giving evidence of its intention to become something other than merely a side-street. He had known it once as a place where the twilight collected early in winter, where the lamplighter’s passage constituted an event, and where the little houses, with their shy, self-contained fronts, suggested families perpetually on the verge of going away for a holiday they would never quite afford. It was not that the street had been poor—poverty, like opulence, had in those days sought elsewhere its more extravagant occasion—but that it had maintained, through some happy conspiracy of scale and accident, the look of a life that neither aspired nor confessed.

Now the very air of it — to his purposely detached apprehension — had been altered by a brightness which was not wholly a matter of gas or glass. The modest houses had suffered strange aggrandizements; cornices had sprung, as if overnight, into the field of vision; balconies dripped, with an almost exotic assurance, from upper windows; and one became aware, through the sudden show of plate-glass where once there had been discreet curtains, of arrangements and occupations that would have startled the old shy domestic twilight. He had been prepared, of course; no man remains for thirty years absent from a city like his and seriously expects to find the very cracks in the pavement consenting to wait his pleasure. But it was one of his habits — and no mere traveller’s trick, but, rather, the fruit of a life spent in slow, exacting observation — to measure outward change less by its gross facts than by the fine readjustments it obliged him to make in his own inward posture. What surprised him, therefore, as the carriage stopped before a house which, in its newly elaborated dignity, yet preserved for him the recognizable gesture of a former doorway, was less the face the street turned to him than the fact that some portion of his mind had clearly, obstinately, expected no change at all.

He dismissed the coachman with an absent-minded liberality, and, standing for a moment on the pavement, let his glance travel up the façade that had, by a curious legal and sentimental economy, waited for him as a dog waits, thin but faithful, beside the packed trunks of a family that has forgotten it.

author: Nathaniel Hawthorne
title: True Stories of History and Biography
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Nathaniel Hawthorne's True Stories of History and Biography. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
In a little New England town, which has now grown old and somewhat solemn with the lapse of many years, there was, not long after the Revolution, a certain low, brown house, with a sloping roof and small square windows, that looked shyly out upon the road, as if half-afraid of the great world passing by. A tall elm tree stood sentinel before the door, and drooped its branches, as though it would shelter the inmates from sun and storm, and from whatever other trouble might chance to visit them.

It was in this humble dwelling that there lived a widow, Mistress Allen by name, with her only son, a bright-eyed lad of twelve summers, whom the neighbors, in their kindly fashion, called Little Stephen, though he would stoutly affirm that he was grown enough to cast aside such diminutives, and to be Stephen Allen, as stout and proper a gentleman as any that marched away in the late King George’s war. But mothers and neighbors, and the good parson himself, still pleased themselves with “Little Stephen,” and perhaps you and I, kind reader, shall do the same.

Mistress Allen, though poor in the world’s account, was rich in those treasures of memory which time cannot wholly steal, and which death itself sometimes seems rather to brighten than to darken. Her husband had fallen many years before, in a skirmish on the frontier, where the rude forest, as if in league with savage men, stretched its arms between the English settlements and the clear fields of peace. He had gone forth a plain farmer, with an old musket and a steadfast heart, and he never came again to sit by his own hearthstone. Yet the boy’s eyes would sparkle, and his cheek flush, when his mother spoke of those days of alarm and courage, when the roll of the drum was heard at sunrise, and strong men left the plough in the furrow, that they might stand between their homes and the shadow of destruction.

It was the good woman’s custom, on long winter evenings, when the wind went crying about the corners of the house, or in the quiet of a summer Sabbath afternoon, to draw her little chair near the kitchen fire, and, with her knitting in hand, tell Stephen such portions of the past as were suited to his young understanding. Sometimes she would speak of their own town, in the days when the Indians still roamed in the neighboring woods, and the meeting-house had loopholes in its walls, that the godly people might worship in safety; sometimes of the great city of Boston, where she had once seen a royal governor ride in state, with a scarlet coat and a glittering sword. And oftenest of all, when the boy’s questions came thick and fast, she would unfold, as one opens an old, well-worn volume, the story of our forefathers’ trials and steadfastness, from the landing of the Pilgrims to the stern triumph of the Revolution.

“Mother,” Stephen said to her, on a certain evening with which my little history begins, “you have told me many a tale of brave men, who fought and bled that we might dwell quietly under our own vine and fig-tree. But you have never yet related all from the beginning. I would fain know how this land, which you say was once a wilderness of savages and wild beasts, became our own country, and why the king beyond the sea claimed it, and how our people found courage to say that they would be free.”

Mistress Allen paused in her knitting, and looked thoughtfully into the red embers, where many an old-time image seemed to live and move for her alone.

“It is a long story, child,” she replied; “longer than one winter’s night, or even many, can well contain. Yet, if you have patience to listen, and a mind to remember, I will tell you, so far as an ignorant woman may, how the Lord led our fathers into this goodly heritage, through storms upon the ocean, and yet rougher storms among men; and perhaps, while you listen, you will learn that Heaven writes history, even more than kings and captains do.”

And thus, while the fire burned low, and the elm tree outside drew strange figures upon the window with its waving boughs, the mother began to rehearse to her child—and to you, gentle reader, if you will accompany him—the true stories of our country’s earlier days.

author: Mark Twain
title: The Innocents Abroad
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Mark Twain's The Innocents Abroad. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
We were to sail on Tuesday, and on Monday the whole town discovered that it had pressing business with the sea. It is astonishing how many persons can suddenly remember that they have always longed for foreign travel, the instant somebody else has actually purchased a ticket.

The ship lay in the harbor like a stout elderly landlady who has been corseted for company; everything bulged where it had no right to bulge, and was drawn in where nature plainly never intended any drawing. She wore a new coat of paint, which in ships, as in men, is accepted as proof of perfect integrity of character. Her name—The Celestial—was lettered on her stern in gold so thick that, had the vessel gone to the bottom, a capable diver might have set up a small jewelry business upon her remains.

A deputation of our acquaintances escorted us to the wharf, ostensibly to bid us Godspeed, but in reality to make sure we really went. They had a singular faith that the civilized world could endure our absence, but were not so confident of our resolution to absent ourselves from it. A man will promise almost anything on dry land.

The passengers gathered in such knots and tangles upon the deck as would have puzzled a mathematician. Every group contained: one old traveler who had “been over before” and had brought back nothing but opinions; one young lady who was certain she should die the first day out, and equally certain it would be romantic; one citizen who had read a handbook of Europe and consequently felt qualified to remodel it; and several persons who plainly believed that the Almighty had reserved the best views on the continent with special reference to their opera-glasses.

They examined the lifeboats with great diligence, not as intending to use them, but as finding a subdued comfort in the thought that the shipowners had, at least, heard of drowning. They inspected the crew, and decided—upon no evidence whatever—that the men were sober and the officers experienced. And then each one, having satisfied himself that he was in the hands of Providence, proceeded to give minute directions about his trunks, as if the loss of his shirts would be too severe a test for even an all-wise government of the universe.

Presently the bell clanged, in that authoritative tone which implies that people who have lingered fondly on the shore for twenty or thirty years must now tear themselves away in two minutes. We embraced such of our friends as were idle enough to allow it; others we shook by the hand with that earnest vigor which is understood to signify that we shall never meet again, though we fully expect to be drinking coffee with them in October. The band struck up an anthem which attempted to combine patriotism with cheerfulness, and succeeded in being too solemn for one and too wheezy for the other.

At last the dock slipped from us—or we from it; there was some misunderstanding between us on that point—and the town retreated with great dignity, exactly as if it had long intended to go backwards into a gray mist and had been waiting only for us to lead the way. The little flags on the pier fluttered us a farewell, the larger ones ignored us; and so we set out, a select company of pilgrims, to improve our minds at foreign expense.

author: Jack London
title: Love of Life, and Other Stories
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Jack London's Love of Life, and Other Stories. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
The man came over the divide at noon. He did not hurry. There was no place to hurry to. The sky was a white glare and the snow took the glare and flung it back into his eyes, so that they were red-rimmed and half-closed and the lids were swollen as though beaten with many little fists. His beard held frost, though the day was not cold, and the frost melted and ran in dirty rills down the stiff hair and into the corners of his mouth.

He stopped on the crest and stood, swaying slightly, and the pack upon his back swung with him. It was not a heavy pack, for he had grown wise to the ways of weight and distance, but it dragged on him as a child drags on a tired hand. He let it slip to the snow and stood free of it, his shoulders aching sharply with the sudden lack of pull. His knees bent once, of their own will, as if they would fold altogether and let him go down.

Below him the land spread wide and empty. Ridges ran like frozen waves, long-backed and smooth, with no trees upon them and no sign of man’s meddling. Between the ridges the valleys lay in blue shadow, and in the bottoms of the valleys black threads of water cut through the white. The sun, high and pitiless, made no warmth. It looked down as a thing that has no concern with men.

He saw no game trails, no smoke, no broken crust where a sled might have passed. He had not expected to see such things, yet his eyes sought them stubbornly, as though sight itself could make them real. There was nothing but the land and the sky and the faint wind that crawled along the surface of the snow, shifting dry crystals from one place to another with a sound like distant whispering.

He drew the mitten off his right hand with his teeth and fumbled at the breast of his coat. The hand was raw and cracked where the mitten had rubbed, and the nails were broken to the quick. He pulled out a strip of bacon wrapped in a scrap of flour sack and looked at it a long time before putting it back. Then he licked the salt from his fingers carefully, as a dog licks the last grease from a bone.

There was no need to look again into the pack. He knew to the ounce what was in it. He knew the last of the flour by the way the sack hung slack against his hip, the last of the tea by the bare cluck it made in the tin. There were six cartridges in his belt and a seventh in the chamber of the rifle, and he knew them as a man knows the money in his pocket when there will be no more.

He put the mitten back on and swung the pack to his shoulders. The straps bit cruelly at the hollows beside his neck. He hitched them to a better place and turned down the slope, breaking trail through the crust that had formed in the night. The snow broke with a dry snap at each step, and the sound went ahead of him and came back from the silent hills, as though the land were mocking the small noise of his going.

He did not think of turning back. There was no back. Behind him lay the weeks of travel, the frozen river, the spruce forest, the abandoned cabin with its door smashed in by winter storms. Before him lay only what he could not see. It was enough. He went on.

author: Leo Tolstoy
title: What Is Art?
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Leo Tolstoy's What Is Art?. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
Of all the questions which, from ancient times, have disturbed the human mind and now, more than ever, demand an answer, none appears so simple in appearance and so confused in reality as the question: What is culture? At every turn we hear this word. We are told that culture is progressing, that nations perish when deprived of culture, that the task of schools, universities, newspapers, museums, even of governments and churches, consists in the protection and advancement of culture. Yet if one were to ask those who speak so confidently what they mean by this term, it would soon become evident that each understands it in his own way, and that these understandings, far from coinciding, often contradict one another.

Some, chiefly among the learned and the wealthy, call culture the sum of all scientific discoveries, of all artistic productions, of all technical conveniences which distinguish our age from those preceding it. Others, especially among the political, see culture in the organization of the state, in the extension of rights, in the multiplication of institutions: parliaments, factories, railroads, journals, laboratories. Others again, more modest but not less convinced, imagine culture to be the refinement of manners, the softening of customs, the diffusion of certain habits of cleanliness, of reading, of conversation, of dress. Finally, there are those who, perhaps not expressing it openly, nevertheless act as if culture consisted in that peculiar way of living which has established itself among the upper classes of the great cities: a way of living which includes concerts, theaters, illustrated books, seasonal travel, society gossip, and a constant concern with “being up-to-date.”

All these definitions, although differing in content, share one common feature: each one, without noticing it, places under the word “culture” precisely that collection of conditions by which the speaker himself sets value on his own life. In this way a scholar, who has spent thirty years bent over books, naturally finds the meaning of culture in the accumulation of knowledge; an engineer, proud of his bridges and machines, sees culture in mechanical progress; a lady of society, to whom an evening without the theater seems lost, finds culture in the arts, by which she understands certain performances in carefully lit rooms, with an orchestra and a high price for admission. 

Yet if we are to speak of culture not as each one of us pleases, but as the peasant in the field would speak of grain, or the carpenter of his wood—that is, if we wish to speak of something real, and not of our private fancies—we must seek a definition that does not depend on our habits, our profession, or our vanity. We must ask: in what does culture consist for the human being as such, not for the German professor, or the French deputy, or the Russian landowner, but for that being who is born naked, who suffers, rejoices, loves, fears death, and inevitably dies?

Until we answer this question simply and honestly, all our talk of “serving culture,” of “developing culture,” will be not only confused but harmful; for a man who does not know what he is doing, yet continues to do it with increasing zeal, can scarcely escape doing evil.

author: Alexandre Dumas
title: Camille (La Dame aux Camilias)
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Alexandre Dumas's Camille (La Dame aux Camilias). Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
All those who have passed a winter in Paris between the years 18— and 18— will remember a certain hôtel in the Rue de Provence, whose green blinds, forever half-lowered, suggested at once secrecy and luxury. It was there that, one morning in January, I followed an auctioneer’s clerk up a carpetless staircase, the velvet having been, so to speak, knocked down with the rest, and found myself in a drawing-room where the last fragments of an existence of pleasure lay waiting the hammer, like the bones of a feast.

I had not known the woman whose property was to be sold. I had only seen, a few days before, her name printed in the small black characters of the obituary notices that flutter, each morning, along with tradesmen’s bills and love-letters, into every Parisian apartment. Under this name, once so celebrated that it had no need of a surname, there were four words: “Died of a chest ailment.” A week later the announcement of the sale appeared in the newspapers; curiosity, I confess it, and something more than curiosity, led me there.

Those who have frequented auctions know that every sale has its own physiognomy. The one to which I had come had the melancholy gravity with which a curtain falls for the last time on a play that one liked perhaps too much. Men came in singly, or in groups of two or three, walking softly as though afraid to wake a sick person, then, reassured by the silence, began talking in undertones, casting quick, appraising glances upon the objects arranged along the walls and on the tables, just as once they had, doubtless, cast glances—of a different kind—upon the mistress of these objects.

The air kept still held a vague perfume, a tired fragrance that seemed to drift from the blue satin of a sofa whose cushions were already hollowed by absent forms. On the mantelpiece, between two girandoles bereft of their candles, a clock under a glass shade, representing Time surprising Love, ticked obstinately; its monotonous sound had something ironical about it in that place where Time had indeed surprised Love, and killed it.

A young man, pale, carefully but negligently dressed, stood at the window, turning his back to the room, and tracing, with the point of his cane, invisible figures on the bare floor. I noticed him because he did not look at anything; and in an auction-room, whoever does not look at the objects must be thinking of the person who possessed them.

“That is Monsieur de Lancy,” whispered at my ear a stout man with red hands whom I had already seen in similar gatherings, and who knew, as he boasted, “all the scandals of the right bank.” “He is the one who loved her best—and the one who ruined her the least.”

At that moment the auctioneer struck his little bell; the murmur ceased. One lifted the cover from a great armchair of white satin, embroidered with silver; a few stains marred the dazzling purity of the seat.

“Gentlemen,” said the auctioneer with that dry liveliness which tries to jest with mourning, “an armchair in which many reputations have reposed; who will make me an offer?”

author: E. M. Forster
title: Alexandria: A History and a Guide
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of E. M. Forster's Alexandria: A History and a Guide. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
The traveller who approaches this city does well to disbelieve at first sight. He may arrive, as most do, by the road from the airport, where the desert has been persuaded—never quite convincingly—to give way to villas and petrol stations, and where the sea, which is the true explanation of everything that has ever happened here, remains obstinately invisible. Or he may descend from a train into a station that smells of dust and diesel, and whose iron arches admit only a scrap of sky. In either case the first impression is one of disappointment, and this is fortunate, for Alexandria has always thrived upon the refutation of expectations.

It is not merely that the city looks smaller than its name. The imagination, furnished with minarets and marble colonnades, with philosophers arguing in the shade of libraries, has been misled by the rhetoric of centuries. Alexandria has been praised or cursed so often that, by the time one encounters her, anything short of apocalypse seems tame. Instead of apocalypse the traveller finds traffic. Horns protest where the orators used to. A man selling plastic toys occupies the place of a Ptolemaic monument. Over everything hangs that peculiar haze which is neither cloud nor sea mist, but a reminder that men have been burning fuel of various kinds on this strip of shore for a very long time.

To understand her, one must begin not with what is visible, but with what has disappeared. Few cities have been so careless with their possessions. The King’s tomb, the world’s first research library, the dining halls of queens, the palaces where Caesar hesitated and Antony did not—each has gone, and gone recurrently, for Alexandria has had more chances than most to lose herself. Fire, flood, earthquake, and the indifference of later centuries have operated with an impartiality that would be admirable if it were not so inconvenient to the historian.

Yet this very absence is useful. It compels us to resist the easy comfort of monuments and to attend instead to lines and tendencies. A column that survives may dominate a guidebook; a vanished street, following the curve of a shoreline that has altered, is often more important. The ancient city, rising by degrees from the marshes that fringed the Mediterranean, followed an idea, and that idea proved more durable than stone. It was the conviction—rash, perhaps, but glorious—that a diagram traced upon a map could reorder the human race. Alexander, that restless surveyor, drew such a diagram here, and the generations that followed him tried, with varying success, to live inside it.

Modern Alexandria has spread far beyond his lines, and one must not force her, like an ill-fitting garment, back into them. She is no longer a Macedonian experiment nor a Roman possession, nor yet the cosmopolitan playground that Europeans invented in the nineteenth century, and then remembered with excessive tenderness when they lost it. She is, above all, Egyptian, and it is one of the purposes of this book to explain how that came to be, without surrendering the older Alexandrocentric illusion that all roads, when properly inspected, lead to this particular corner of the sea.

The reader is advised, therefore, to proceed without romance, but also without malice. We shall attempt no defence of the city—for she has needed none and would be embarrassed by it—but only a description of her, as she was, as she is, and as she persists in the minds of those who never quite leave her, even when, unlike Alexander, they manage to die elsewhere.

author: Joseph Conrad
title: The Secret Sharer
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Joseph Conrad's The Secret Sharer. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
On my first evening as master of the Dido, when the last of the shore lights had fallen astern and the river had closed about us like a slow brown curtain, I came to stand alone upon her quarter-deck. The pilot had gone, the tug’s wake had long since lost itself in the sullen folds of the estuary, and the land, withdrawn into a shapeless murk, seemed already of another existence. A faint, damp breath, exhaled by those distant marshes I had crossed so often in my boyhood, crept aboard and passed me by, as if reluctant to attend us any farther seaward. 

The ship herself, though new to me, moved under my feet with the grave composure of an old, self-contained creature. She answered the wheel with an unhurried swing, acknowledging each degree of helm as one might concede some reasonable demand. A residuum of sunset glowed faintly in the west, where a dull smear of crimson threatened and then resigned itself to extinction behind ragged banks of cloud. Above the low rims of the banks there lingered one long, greenish smear of pallor, which was not light so much as the memory of it. There, in that failing remnant, the spars and rigging traced their intricate and motionless design, black and rigid, like the framework of some slender and elaborate instrument left hanging in a deserted chamber.

Voices had ceased along the decks. The men, wearied by the last fretful labors of departure—hauling on damp hawsers, stowing the final muddle of stores, securing the boats in their chocks—had drifted forward, swallowed one by one by that dim region about the forecastle. Somewhere there a handspike fell, with a hollow knock upon the planks, and for a time the ship’s life seemed contained in that solitary, unclaimed sound. 

I had the sense, standing there, that my figure was the only one remaining between the waning sky and the dark reach of the wake spreading astern—an upright, inconsequential shadow, set like a thin marker at the beginning of some long unwritten record. For it is to be understood that this was my first command; and though the papers lay secure in my cabin, countersigned and proper, and though the Dido had this very afternoon, in a world of inquisitive tide-surveyors and indifferent clerks, been formally made over to my charge, I experienced then—not so much doubt of my authority—as a curious detachment from it, as though the fact which now bound me to the ship with an iron finality were in league, for a little yet, with the intangible.

The mate’s step sounded briefly on the planking behind me—measured and cautious, with that air of considerate reserve by which certain old seamen seem to protest, even in the smallest movements, against the unseemly impetuosity of youth. He paused a pace or two away.

“Sou’west by south, sir,” he said, in a voice that contrived to be respectful without surrendering its habitual distrust of all novel arrangements. Then, after an instant, he added, “Tide making still. We’ll have the bar before midnight.”

I assented with a word which seemed hardly mine, and he withdrew again, leaving me with the stars just beginning to declare themselves overhead, like far-off witnesses assembling to observe, without comment, what might befall between that shadow of land and the open sea.

author: Oscar Wilde
title: De Profundis
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Oscar Wilde's De Profundis. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
It is only now, when the world which once applauded me has grown tired of its own echo, that I begin to comprehend how frivolously I squandered the grandeur of my ruin. I mistook the flare of my fall for a species of triumph, and applauded myself, with the vanity of a bad actor, for the very gestures that betrayed me. I had imagined that tragedy was a purple garment which one might wear with deliberate grace; I discover it to be instead a slow undressing of the soul, performed in the dimmest chamber of the heart, where there is no spectator but remorse.

In the seasons when my name passed pleasantly from mouth to mouth, like a sweetmeat too delicate to be kept, I believed that I possessed myself. In truth I was the last person to whom I belonged. Society is an exacting creditor: it lends its smiles at a rate of interest so usurious that to repay them is to be stripped of every honest possession. I had given myself over to its flattery as some men surrender their estates at the gaming-table, intoxicated less by gain than by the glitter of the lamps above the green baize. Only now, among the stark furnishings of disgrace, do I begin to perceive the extent of the mortgage.

They say a man’s character is revealed by adversity, as if misfortune were a species of moral lantern. They are wrong. Adversity does not reveal; it compels. Under praise, one may drift indefinitely in pleasant self-deception; under censure, one must learn at least the art of self-invention. I do not know whether what has awakened in me is conscience or merely a more fastidious form of vanity; but I feel that I must construct some meaning out of the debris of my days, lest I be forced to admit that I suffered merely, and did not even learn to suffer well.

It would be convenient to ascribe all that befell me to others: to the perfidy of friends, to the dull vindictiveness of institutions, to the crude appetite of the crowd for the spectacle of a fallen idol. Society loves to stone its prophets, especially when they have never uttered a prophecy more alarming than a clever epigram. Yet, however justified my bitterness, I find that each stone, once hurled, lies finally at my own feet. When I stoop to gather them, I discover that their weight has always been mine.

There was a time when I imagined that the highest form of wisdom was to remain on the surface of things, to skate elegantly along the polished ice of experience, and never inquire how deep the waters ran beneath. I adorned my shallowness with paradox, as one might deck a mannequin with jewels and mistake it for a living prince. Now that the ice has at last cracked, I must learn either to swim or to drown with some semblance of dignity. I am trying, therefore, to speak plainly—not because plainness becomes me, but because extravagance has exhausted itself.

If style was once my refuge from sincerity, let this attempted honesty be my final affectation. I cannot cease to be theatrical; I can only change the scenery. Here, then, upon the narrow stage of this page, I shall rehearse my confession, not in the hope of acquittal—for I no longer believe in the justice of the world—but in the dimmer, more private hope that I might yet become intelligible to myself.

title: Their Mariposa Legend: A Romance of Santa Catalina
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Charlotte Bronte's Their Mariposa Legend: A Romance of Santa Catalina. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
It was toward the soft decline of a Californian afternoon, when the sun, wearied with his riot upon the mainland, seemed to retreat with languid splendour across the sea, that I first beheld Santa Catalina. The island rose from the lap of the Pacific like some opal dream, half veiled in amethystine haze, yet pierced in places by sharp, luminous realities of rock and peak, which caught the light as the lance-heads of a phalanx catch it in the van of battle. Dim gulfs and hidden coves gaped, yet disclosed nothing; slopes of grey-green chaparral were ribbed with shadow; and, higher yet, rugged heights stood forth, touched with a faint rose as if remembering a bridal flush long since faded.

Our small steamer, that had plied with gruff pertinacity between the world we had left and the solitude to which we drew near, slackened its throbbing pulse as though the very mechanism felt the hush that overbreathed the scene. The passengers stood crowded upon the forward deck, some gay with that noisy rapture which spends itself upon the surface of things, others already subsiding into such silence as is born of a sudden consciousness that Beauty, once manifest, demands something more reverent than laughter. For myself, I leaned against the worn rail, my eyes fastened upon the widening curve of the little bay, and was sensible, without precisely knowing why, that I gazed upon a place whose story had begun long before my own, and would not end with it.

“Yonder is Avalon,” said a voice at my side.

The speaker was a gentleman of middle years, with a brow somewhat lined, less by time than by thought; his hair had not yet submitted to grey, yet one divined that youth had retreated from his spirit as from a citadel beleaguered, but not disgraced. He indicated with his cane a crescent of shore, already disclosing a few white façades clinging timidly to the foot of the mountain, as if the cliffs behind had but reluctantly consented to their intrusion.

“Avalon?” I repeated, pleased, perhaps foolishly, that so romantic a name should be gravely bestowed upon a port of such small commercial pretension. “Then we must expect enchantments.”

He smiled, though his eyes remained on the island. “Expect little, and you may find much. Avalon has its legends; but it is with legends as with treasures—those who come to dig for them make the most vulgar work of it. It is better, perhaps, when a story rises of its own will, like a spring from the hillside, and compels you to stoop and drink.”

“You speak as if you knew such a story here,” I said.

“I know too many,” he answered briefly; then, with a change of tone: “Do you see that cleft above the town, where the ravine opens like a dark letter in the very body of the hill? Remember it. The island’s heart lies there; and if it ever speaks to you, it will speak from that shadow.”

At that instant, the vessel swung, the water hissed along her flanks, and the clustered roofs of Avalon drew near with an air at once inviting and remote. Between the golden clouds above and the lucid depths below, Santa Catalina seemed poised in some rare balance of earth and dream; and I felt—as one feels sometimes, without logic—that I was passing, not into a mere holiday resort, but across a threshold where the commonplace life of yesterday must loosen its hold, to make room for some stranger guest.

author: H. G. Wells
title: The Red Room
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of H. G. Wells's The Red Room. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
"I am not here," I said, "to be tutored by superstition nor frightened by phantoms of your gossip. I have come to sleep the night in the Iron Corridor, and I mean to do so."

The words had upon the old woman a visible effect; she drew her shawl closer about her bent shoulders and peered up at me with an odd glitter in her eyes, as though something in my resolve pleased and angered her in the same instant. Her mouth, a pale and shrunken line, twitched as if the recollection of speech had grown unfamiliar. In the yellow tremor of the firelight her face seemed carved rather than living, with deep engravings of time and trouble.

"You’ll not say I didn’t warn you," she answered at last, her voice breaking strangely on the last word. "I’ve warned every gentleman that’s fancied himself equal to that place. They all come with brave words and smiling mouths. They don’t all go away so."

From the straight-backed chair between us the old man—the oldest man I have ever seen—lifted his head, which had been nodding towards the fire, and fixed on me two watery, accusing eyes. They were slow eyes, and yet they held a certain watchfulness, as of one who listens always for a particular sound. His face was all lines and shadows.

"The Iron Corridor," he croaked, lingering stiffly over the syllables, "is no fit lodging for the likes of you, sir. Nor for the likes of any living man."

"And yet it is but a passage," I replied, with an impatience I did not wholly feel, "a narrow way with walls of stone and doors of iron. No more, no less. The tale that it is haunted is an old wives’ tale. I shall go there presently with a lamp and a rug, and in the morning I shall return as sound in mind and limb as I go."

"Aye," he said, and shook his head slowly. "That’s what they said. That’s what they all said."

The third person in the room, a little man with a crooked back who sat far from the fire in the shadow of the great oak press, uttered a dry, mirthless chuckle. I had not heard him speak before, and his laughter was an offensive, rustling noise, like dry leaves dragged across stone.

"You’ll be wanting candles," he said. "Not just one lamp. Not if you’re wise."

The place in which we sat had that peculiar air of rooms long shut from daylight. The ceiling beams stooped low; the walls, panelled in dark wood, drank in the light and seemed to sweat age. The window-shutters were closed and fastened with an iron bar, though the evening outside was calm. The only light came from the fire and from two guttering candles on the mantle-shelf, whose feeble flames wavered whenever the old woman shifted or the little hunchback wheezed.

"I shall be sufficiently provided," I said. "If you have candles, I will take as many as you can spare. And the key."

At the mention of the key a silence fell among them, a silence so complete that the small hiss and crackle of the embers became suddenly loud. The old woman’s hands, which had been twisting together in her lap, grew still. The hunchback’s chuckle died in his throat. The ancient man stared not at me now, but somewhere beyond my shoulder, as if the mere idea of that piece of metal summoned up some private vision for him alone.

"It’s not the dark you’ll be needing protection from," the old woman muttered at last. "Nor the cold. But go, then. Go, if you must. You’re young and certain, and youth will always rather learn from its bruises than from another body’s scars."

"The key," I repeated.

She rose slowly, as though each joint argued against the motion, and shuffled towards the shadowed corner where the great clock stood ticking ponderously. As she passed me, she paused and lifted her face so near to mine that I could see the wet glint along the rim of her pale eye.

"When you hear it," she whispered, her breath unpleasantly warm against my cheek, "you must not answer. Whatever it says, you must not answer."

"I do not expect to hear anything," I answered, forcing a laugh that sounded thin to my own ears. "Except perhaps the wind."

"Aye," croaked the old man by the fire. "The wind."

author: Arthur Conan Doyle
title: The Valley of Fear
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Arthur Conan Doyle's The Valley of Fear. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
“‘The raven circles at dusk,’” read Sherlock Holmes slowly, holding the scrap of paper up to the lamp so that the crude pencilling stood out the more clearly. “‘The raven circles at dusk. The nest is empty. The chick is on the road.’ That is all, Watson.”

He let the paper flutter down upon the table among his tobacco-ash and chemical stains, then leaned back in his arm-chair with that peculiar expression of weary amusement with which he was wont to greet some fresh intrusion of mystery into the stagnant London afternoon.

It was in the late autumn of ’89, if my memory serves me aright, and the fog had lain upon Baker Street since morning, a yellow, greasy pall that turned the gas of the street-lamps into blurred orange smudges and sent a cold moisture trickling down the window-panes. Inside, however, our rooms were snug and warm, the coal fire burning cheerily, and Holmes’s long limbs stretched at loose ease upon the hearth-rug like some great cat awakened yet not roused to exertion.

“You see, of course, what it means,” he remarked.

“I am afraid that I do not,” I confessed. “Unless it be that some eccentric naturalist has mistaken you for a collector of birds.”

Holmes’s eyes twinkled.

“My dear fellow, if Nature had endowed you with the imagination of a poet instead of the courage of a soldier, you would long ago have rivalled my poor efforts. No, no; ‘raven,’ ‘nest,’ ‘chick’—these are not parts of an ornithological inventory. They are the lumber of the conspirator. You observe the paper?”

I took it up. It was of an ordinary bluish tint, torn roughly from a larger sheet, but bearing along one edge the trace of a watermark.

“Common enough,” said I. “Half the stationers in London—”

“Common to you, remarkable to me,” Holmes interrupted. “You will place it, if you please, upon the mantel, away from the draught. There are times when the most trivial clue, like the smallest vein in a marble block, may guide the sculptor’s hand. We shall examine it presently.”

“You speak,” I said, “as though this message had some importance. Who brought it?”

Holmes’s thin fingers strayed to the violin lying upon the chair-arm, but did not lift it.

“A boy from Charing Cross. He found it, so he states, slipped beneath the strap of his satchel as he stood upon the platform. No one near him; no one touched him. Only, when he opened the satchel in the train, the paper lay upon his books. He read it, found nothing in it but nonsense, and would doubtless have flung it away had not his eye caught the first two words of the address: ‘To Mr. Sherlock Holmes…’”

“Extraordinary!”

“On the contrary, quite characteristic. It is the fashion just now among certain gentlemen of the underworld to take such elaborate precautions that they defeat their own object. Here is an instance in which a conspiracy throws a bottle into the sea and addresses it before casting it from the rocks. There is at least the comfort that the sea has washed it up at the right door.”

“And you infer a conspiracy?”

Holmes’s gaze wandered past me to where the fog pressed thick and yellow against the panes.

“My dear Watson,” said he, “a solitary crime is the outburst of passion or greed; a message in cipher, thrust upon an unwitting messenger, is the whisper of an organisation. Listen to the cadence of the thing: ‘The raven circles at dusk. The nest is empty. The chick is on the road.’ There is a mind behind that—an uneducated mind, perhaps, but a mind schooled in secrecy. We must find the road, we must discover the chick, and, if Providence smiles upon us, we shall see this raven of the night in the light of the dock at last.”

“You have some theory, then?”

“I have an impression,” he answered. “The theory will follow the facts, as dawn follows the darkness. For the moment, there is only this paper, the boy at Charing Cross, and a certain very grim possibility. You observe, Watson, that dusk is already upon us. It may be that by nightfall the road of this unknown ‘chick’ will have crossed our own.”

author: Stephen Crane
title: The Open Boat and Other Stories
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Stephen Crane's The Open Boat and Other Stories. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
None of them had thought much of the wind when they first came out upon the flats. It was merely the sort of thing that moves one’s hat a little to the back of the head and obliges a man to grip at his coat-front. They spoke of it, as they spoke of the road and of the distance, because it existed and was somewhat in the way. But it was not until the last low house, balanced on its four brick feet above the reeds, had diminished behind them, and the raw, level land had reached over the horizon to touch itself, that the wind began to mean something.

The three of them—Hale, the boy Tom, and the man with the parcel—went in single file on the narrow causeway. On either side lay the marsh, brown and sickly-green, sliced here and there by black cuts of water which, even in that grey hour, reflected a hard little edge of day. The sky itself was the color of dirty tin. It was a sky that had been handled roughly.

The man with the parcel walked first because he had said he knew the way, and the other two had accepted this like a tax. He was a short fellow, thick in the shoulders, carrying his package under his arm with a kind of offended loyalty, as if the thing had insulted him and he was escorting it to court. The boy came next, stooped already by no weight at all, and Hale was last, partly through habit and partly because, in a thin uncertain company, he preferred there should be someone ahead to meet the world.

The wind came lengthwise over the flats, having sharpened itself in secret places. It struck at their ears and crawled down inside their collars. Often it took the words straight out of their mouths and flung them aside upon the marsh where they lay at once as useless as dead birds. Conversation became a matter of shouting brief, economical declarations that were not worth the effort.

"Long yet?" the boy cried once.

The man with the parcel turned his head a little, his cap-bill cutting a piece out of the sky. He considered the far-drawn line where earth and heaven clumsily met. The line had not altered for an hour.

"Not so long," he shouted back, and at the same time knew he lied. The knowledge gave him no great pain. Here, a truth about distances would be a poor, thin thing; a lie had more flesh on it.

Hale heard and smiled in his coat-collar. For him, the deceit was not in the words but in the idea that any man could calculate length in a place that did not admit to edges. The flats went on and on, much as regret does, and it was quite possible they would walk forever and still be approaching.

Beneath them, the causeway was a long, white nerve laid bare across the marsh. It was narrow enough that they did not willingly look down from it. Where there were ditches and pools, the water was unexpectedly lustrous, made cruelly distinct by the poor light. It had a look of waiting that irritated Hale. He disliked a thing that behaved as if time were its ally.

Around them the reeds rattled and shook themselves, but produced nothing—no bird, no animal, not even an honest sound. The only live noise was the wind’s steady outrage and the monotonous slap of their boots on the chalky path. The parcel, bound in brown paper and string, knocked now and then against the man’s ribs with the dull, regular complaint of a thing that does not choose its own journey.

After a while the boy began to limp on his right foot. He said nothing of it. His face, raw from the wind, had taken on that patient, injured look which belongs to people who believe pain to be a private indecency. Hale watched the rhythm of the limp assert itself in the line of footsteps, an extra beat, a small confession, and was perversely relieved. It made the procession more human. Without it, they might have been three purposeful shadows blown over a dead map.

Far ahead there appeared—so faintly that it might at first have been an error in the eye—a little black interruption of the horizon. It did not grow quickly, but it persisted. Each time they raised their heads from the tyranny of the path, it was there, the same, like a thought that will not be dismissed.

"There," the man with the parcel said at last, and his voice had the hollow ring of someone announcing land to a ship that has not yet decided whether it wishes to arrive.

author: Mark Twain
title: The Gilded Age: A Tale of Today
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Mark Twain's The Gilded Age: A Tale of Today. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
In a bend of the river which nature had formed with an absent-minded elbow, and then apparently forgot to straighten, there lay the town of Pecan Bluff. It had been laid out once by a surveyor with a square jaw and a compass that would point north when it was not otherwise engaged. The surveyor departed long ago, but his lines remained, cutting the wilderness into tidy parallelograms of expectation. Most of those expectations were still unsatisfied.

The town, as seen from the water on a spring morning, had an air of having been dropped there by accident and then of making the best of it rather than suffer the humiliation of being picked up again. A scatter of roofs, the steeple of a church that prayed louder than its congregation, two brick warehouses struggling to look citified among a democracy of plank structures—all were strung along a muddy street that began devoutly at the river and lost its religion in the woods.

To enter Pecan Bluff from the land side was to approach civilization cautiously, as one might approach a sleeping dog of uncertain disposition. The road came wavering through fields and gullies, fenced in places and ambitious in others, and at last staggered into the town itself, quite spent and no wiser than when it set out. It brought with it dust in summer, mud in winter, and hope in all seasons. Hope was Pecan Bluff’s principal article of traffic. It had as many varieties as a dry goods store and sold on as easy credit.

The reader must not suppose, however, that Pecan Bluff was an unimportant place. There was no such word in the town’s vocabulary. Its inhabitants were too patriotic to admit that the census returns were correct, and it was the settled conviction of the community that if the government had placed the capital there in the beginning, as it plainly ought to have done, the nation would have been spared a vast amount of traveling.

There was, to begin with, Colonel Jasper T. Marlowe, who had never been in the army but had fought so many battles in conversation that it was held a cruel economy on the government’s part not to have furnished him with a uniform. The Colonel owned the largest house in town, a square, pretentious edifice with a cupola and an opinion of itself. In front of it stood two sickly locust trees, which, being Southern, declined to thrive under Northern paint. The Colonel had inherited the house, the cupola, and the opinion; and if he had not inherited the locust trees also, they had at least taken root in his circumstances.

The Colonel had, besides these possessions, a daughter named Augusta and a plan. Of the two, the plan gave him the greater anxiety. Daughters have a way of going off without leaving any particular evidence of having been managed, but plans remain at home and reproach a man with their failure. The Colonel’s plan contemplated nothing less than the elevation of Pecan Bluff from a town of promise to a city of performance. There were details, of course, such as railroads, factories, colleges, a music hall, gas works, and a cemetery suitable for a metropolis; but these were subordinate fireworks, meant to play about the grand rocket of his design and astonish mankind when it burst in the legislative sky.

Augusta was the instrument by which all this was to be accomplished. She did not know it, which made her all the more valuable as an instrument; for a conscious violin is the most unmanageable of musical contrivances. She was a tall girl, with the same resolute chin that had set the surveyor’s lines and the same habit of looking past a thing as if she saw something beyond it that might be improved. In a town where everybody knew everybody’s business and improved it in the telling, such a disposition was either a providence or an impertinence, according to the beholder’s tax receipts.

Between the Colonel’s plan and Augusta’s ignorance of it there stretched a future broad enough for a whole generation of mistakes. It is with the making of those mistakes, and the fortune of Pecan Bluff under their guidance, that this faithful history is concerned.

author: George Eliot
title: The Lifted Veil
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of George Eliot's The Lifted Veil. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
I have always held, with a firmness that has been the single inflexible thing in a life otherwise wavering and indeterminate, that to understand one’s own doom in advance of its fulfilment is no blessing from any benign power. A man may be endowed, if you will, with a faculty that lays bare the secret threads of other lives; he may read their contrition before they have sinned, their weariness while they are yet exultant in hope, and the stale taste of pleasures still untasted: but the prevision of his own closing scene, the foreknown boundary at which his story must break off—this is a gift against nature, and must be paid for as we pay for the knowledge of evil.

I write now, while the faint heat of an autumn noon rests upon the stone parapet beneath my window, and the shadow of the lime-tree draws itself, long and rigid, across the lawn, like the dial-hand that points to the last of my numbered hours. It is not that any physician, with wise head shaking over my wrist or my lungs, has pronounced my term approaching; they have been uniformly encouraging, with that officious courage which shrinks from recognising the limit of its own art. They speak of change of air, of cheerful company, of diversion from my solitary habits, as if the demons that beset me were of the vulgar order that may be exorcised by a journey or a jest. Their optimism might have been of some poor value had my disease lain in the flesh; but I have long carried within me the sentence that no human speech can revoke.

You who read this—if indeed it ever pass beyond my own hands—may, being happily ignorant, turn from such morbid prolixity with impatience, and ask what right I have, whose days are no more singular in outward event than those of many obscure men, to demand your attention thus at the outset. My excuse, if excuse it be, lies precisely in that obscurity. Lives that the world calls remarkable leave a broad trail of print and echoed speech behind them; they are shouted over by critics and biographers, subjected to the coarse handlings of posterity, till whatever delicate turn of feeling or secret pressure of circumstance that first gave them tragic meaning is rubbed and blurred. It is the fate of the unnoticed, whose existence passes like the faint print of small creatures in the snow, to retain more faithfully the impress of that unseen foot which has crushed them.

Moreover, I am impelled—not by any conscious hope of moral edification, for I have long ceased to believe that men amend themselves by printed example—but by a dull necessity, as if some inward witness demanded that I set down the stages by which I have come to this foreknown end. If my words have any efficacy, it will not be to make virtue more attractive, nor to deter eager youth from its rash desires: rather, they may add one more testimony to the bitter truth that there are gifts no man should covet and revelations which, coming too soon, make every subsequent hour an echo of an event already suffered.

I was not always what I am now—a man to whom the future is but a twice-told tale, and whose chief surprise is that others can still be astonished. There was once a time when I lived within the common shelter of ignorance, when to-morrow had for me that vague, luminous vastness it presents to any child who has not yet learned that possibility is but a name we give to what will never be. To trace, even for my own understanding, how I passed from that dim, happy region into the cold clearness where I now abide, is to go back to the earliest movements of consciousness, to those remembered hours whose trifling incidents—an unkind word, a hand withdrawn, a door left ajar upon forbidden talk—may afterwards stand revealed as the first threads of a fatal web.

Let me begin, then, not where the catastrophe declared itself, nor where the strange faculty I bear was fully recognised, but at that earlier period when it was as yet no more than a vague distemper of the nerves, a haunting oppression, which made my childhood at once more vivid and more desolate than is usual with the young.

author: Anthony Trollope
title: Can You Forgive Her?
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Anthony Trollope's Can You Forgive Her?. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
There are ladies, young, fair, well-born, carefully nurtured, tenderly cherished, and endowed by fortune with more than a modest sufficiency, who yet contrive to be very miserable—as miserable, at any rate, as a lady who breakfasts at ten and has a carriage at call can decently presume to be. Whether they have a right to their misery is a question which, before we go very far with the story I am about to tell, you must assist me in answering. You, my reader, shall sit with me as one of a family council, and decide whether Miss Clara Ainsley was entitled to make wry faces at fate, or whether she should have eaten her partridges and oranges in thankful silence.

Clara was not plain. I may go further and say that she possessed, as some of her lovers asserted with more warmth than discretion, a beauty that was almost striking. She had that peculiar fairness of skin which, though it may imperil a lady’s tranquillity under a July sun, is held in London drawing-rooms to be of more value than a duchess’s coronet. Her eyes were blue,—not the bold, staring blue which seems to ask each passer-by what he is worth in the Funds, but soft, and, when she was pleased, affectionate. Her hair—every novelist must say something of his heroine’s hair, or the critics will swear that the woman is bald—was of that pale brown which poets call chestnut, and which no chestnut-tree ever yet produced. She was tall also, with shoulders that had never stooped to the darning of stockings, and hands which betrayed an intimacy with the piano rather than with the saucepan.

Nor was she poor. Her father, the late Mr. Josiah Ainsley, of Ainsley & Wragge, West India and Colonial Produce, had left her, after twenty-five years of very honest traffic in sugar, rum, and mahogany, some fifteen hundred a year,—money which had been made, not without groans, under a sun scarcely favourable to the complexion which his daughter afterwards so successfully displayed. He had further left her the small, respectable house in Harley Street, in which our story opens, and a widowed mother, who considered the preservation of Clara’s prospects to be the first duty imposed upon a Christian parent.

What, then, was lacking? you will say. Was it health? No doctor had yet earned a guinea by lamenting over Miss Ainsley’s nerves. Was it admiration? The young men who lounged against the door-jambs at evening parties, and the elder gentlemen who watched them from the card-table, could have set that doubt at rest. Was it occupation? She read poetry, wrote letters longer than many sermons, played the harp,—for in those days the harp had not yet yielded to the piano’s despotism,—and visited the poor, with much rustling of silk in very narrow alleys.

Nevertheless, when my story first takes her by the hand, Clara was not content. She had been asked a question, and she did not know whether she might answer it with a simple Yes. That a gentleman had asked it will surprise no one. That the gentleman was in all external matters unobjectionable,—nay, in some particulars positively desirable,—will, I think, also be granted; for he possessed five thousand a year, a county place, a town house of more pretension than the one in Harley Street, and a nose which, if not exactly Roman, suggested at least some acquaintance with the Caesars. Yet you shall hear how Miss Ainsley, after being duly solicited to share these advantages, sat for nearly a month considering whether she would be the most virtuous of women in accepting them, or the most heroic of martyrs in declining.

author: Louisa May Alcott
title: Louisa May Alcott : Her Life, Letters, and Journals
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Louisa May Alcott's Louisa May Alcott : Her Life, Letters, and Journals. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
When one seeks to follow the growth of a soul, it is needful to begin, not with any outward triumph, but with the small and quiet beginnings in which character first betrays itself. The woman whom the world afterwards knew as writer, reformer, and friend, came into life neither heralded by great expectations nor fenced about by fortune. She was a child of narrow means, of high hopes, and of that strenuous patience which poverty and principle so often breed together.

Her birthplace was one of those New England towns whose very spareness seems to train self-denial. The white, wooden house, standing a little back from the road, with lilac bushes at the gate and a long slope of garden ground behind, is remembered by all who knew it as a place of incessant endeavor. Books, babies, and bread‑making went on together beneath its roof; and if the oven was sometimes empty, the mother's hands idle of work, the father's purse bare of coins, no one could say that hearts or brains lay fallow there.

Of the childhood passed in this unpromising soil, our records are few, yet sufficiently characteristic. In a small, brown‑covered note‑book, stitched clumsily by her own fingers, the child wrote, in an uneven hand:

“March 12th.—I meant to be very good to‑day. I got up early and made Ma’s fire. Then I scolded Anna because she stayed in bed, and after that nothing went right. It is hard to be pious when the kettle will not boil and one’s stockings are full of holes. I think God knows I try, even when I fidget and fly out. I shall begin again to‑morrow.”

It is the accent of a nature at once impulsive and conscientious, too ardent to be decorous, too honest to be self‑satisfied. She fails, she records the failure with humorous indignation, and straightway sets herself a new task of amendment. These little entries, made in moments stolen from play or work, give more insight into the girl than pages of description. The very spelling, uncertain and wilful, has a flavor of protest in it.

Her mother, a woman of strong sense and tender feeling, read these confidences with anxious interest, yet forbore comment. “I would rather she should govern herself from within,” Mrs. M. once wrote to a friend, “than that my poor rules should be the only curb upon so fiery a temper. There is something noble under all this impetuosity, if only I can keep it from wasting itself in impatience and small storms.”

The father, more given to dreams than to discipline, watched his little philosopher with an admiration not always tempered by discretion. To him her quick indignations were signs of “righteous wrath,” her vehement denials “the protest of the individual soul against tyranny.” Of money, and the need to earn it, he spoke in high phrases; of labor, as of a sacrament. The children listened with the respectful wonder which children give to their elders’ abstractions, but it was the mother who contrived that there should be bread on the table and warm garments for winter.

Thus between ideal and necessity, sermon and sewing, the girl grew to that uneasy stature which is called youth. Of the outer events of these early years there is little to chronicle; of the inward conflicts, much that may help us to understand the woman she was to become.

title: Age of Innocence

gpt_opening:
It was universally conceded, that winter, that the new opera house had been built at least twenty years too soon. The old families, who had so patiently cultivated their prejudices in the stagnant light of the Academy, felt themselves rather indecently exposed in the harsh electric brilliance of the new auditorium; they had the look of people whose housekeeping has been suddenly invaded by professional decorators, and who sit conscious and uncomfortable among strange upholstery that has not yet grown shabby enough to be associated with their memories.

The house itself—florid, over-gilded, and already, in its first season, vaguely out of date—seemed to have sprung up from the pavement in a single night, like those illuminated palaces which form and fade on the white screens of magic-lanterns. In the older quarters of the city, where brick-fronted houses, their brownstone stoops worn hollow by half a century of visiting and being visited, confronted the street with the obstinate respectability of people who have “always lived there,” the notion that such a flamboyant structure should call itself an opera house, and attract there the same names that had once sat under dingy frescoes in the Academy, was felt to be a kind of architectural impertinence.

Yet, on that January evening when our narrative begins, no one would have supposed that the new opera house had imputed any discourtesy to its patrons. Every box and balcony had put on its best behaviour. The tiers of crimson and gold, stacked one above the other like the layers of some gigantic wedding-cake, were thronged with faces for which the florists and hair-dressers of Fifth Avenue had been industriously toiling since midday. Pearls, which looked as if they had come down unwillingly from old velvet cases still faintly smelling of camphor, shone with a quiet obstinacy that no new lighting could disconcert; tiaras that had once nodded over dowagers’ temples at embassies in Vienna and Rome reaffirmed, above the smooth hair of their American granddaughters, their right to interpret social precedence in an immigrant city.

The opera was advertised as “Tristan und Isolde,” a work which several of the older subscribers still refused to recognize as music, on the ground that one could not hum it, and that it went on a long while without seeming to arrive anywhere. That such a composition should be allowed to inaugurate the first season of the new house was another proof, to these critics, that the town was hurling itself down a declivity of foreign fashions; but they had not, on that account, relinquished their subscriptions. If New York insisted on going to perdition to the sound of German horns, they at least proposed to sit where they could see who went with it.

It was one of the compensations of the new house that, from any of the front boxes on the first tier, one could command an almost uninterrupted view of all the others. The architects, in a moment of unconscious veracity, had arranged the interior less like a temple of art than a kind of circular drawing-room, in which the occupants might, under pretext of listening to music, study each other’s clothes and companions with the scientific intensity which only leisure and good breeding can give. The stage, sunk at one end like an afterthought, presented its artificial storms and calamities less urgently to the eye than did the real, if more veiled, agitations that played over the faces in the horseshoe curve.

At eight-thirty precisely, when the chandeliers were turned up to their fullest incandescence, and the vast, polished shell of the house held, as in the cup of a lily, the murmurous perfume of hundreds of people scented for an occasion, Mrs. Aurelia Van Degen entered the left-hand box in the first tier, and by that simple act laid claim to the evening.

title: What Men Live By, and Other Tales

On the edge of a certain village, not far from the road that led to the town, there lived a widowed peasant, Ivan Petrovich, with his old mother and his young daughter. Their hut was low and smoke-blackened; in spring, when the snow melted, water ran under the sill, and in winter the wind rattled so in the thatch that the old mother would cross herself and whisper a prayer before lying down to sleep. 

Ivan was not counted among the rich. He had no horse, no cow of his own, and his only field, planted with rye, lay beyond the birches where the land began to rise toward the forest. Yet among the villagers Ivan was spoken of as an honest man and a quiet one. He did not drink away his wages at the tavern; he did not quarrel at the communal meetings; and when there was a sick neighbor or a roofing to be lifted, one could be sure that Ivan would come, with his worn cap in his hands and his bare, calloused feet treading softly over the earth.

His mother, Akulina, was a small, stooped woman, with a wrinkled face like dried apple peel. She remembered the old times—how it had been in her father’s day, and under the lord, and how the soldiers had come riding through the village when she was a girl. Her eyes were dim and watered from the smoke of the stove, but she kept the house in order, swept the earthen floor, lit the icon lamp on Saturdays, and taught her granddaughter to read the prayers, syllable by syllable.

The girl’s name was Dunya. She was thin, with long hands and serious gray eyes. At twelve years old she already rose before dawn in summer to run with a pail to the river and bring back water, to knead bread with her grandmother, and to weed the potato patch. When the work was done and Ivan had gone to the neighboring estate or to the forest to earn his bread by day-labor, Dunya would sit by the window, moving her lips over the crooked letters in the torn primer that had come to them from the priest, or she would go out to stand under the birch and listen to the cuckoo calling from beyond the marsh.

Thus they lived, not in plenty, but as God sent it, until one year misfortune came upon them.

That spring the thaw was early and hard. The river broke its banks and swept pieces of ice through the meadow; the road to town disappeared under muddy water. The villagers said: “This will not end well. The fields will rot.” But when the waters drew back and the sun had dried the furrows, the men took their ploughs as they had always done, trusting in God and the season.

Ivan, too, went to his field beyond the birches. Borrowing his neighbor’s horse, he set to work while the ground was still cool and fragrant. He had not gone three furrows’ length when the borrowed horse stumbled and fell, snorting and trembling, its breath coming in gasps. By evening the beast lay on its side, foam at its mouth, its large eye dulled; and before night it was dead.

The neighbor, seeing his loss, tore at his hair and shouted; and in his anger he fell upon Ivan, cursing him for a destroyer and a good-for-nothing. The other peasants gathered, and though some said the horse had been old and the pasture poor, others shook their heads and muttered that Ivan would never repay such a debt in all his days.

From that time misery entered Ivan’s house and sat by their hearth like a fourth person.

title: The Old Curiosity Shop
