Night—so absolute that the very stars appeared to shiver in their high places—was settling like a worn and dusky curtain over the great, begrimed city, whose multitudinous noises fell to a sort of hoarse whisper, as though the day’s riot had left them breathless. A thin rain, like a weary clerk patient in long addition, had been dropping, dropping, dropping since the afternoon; and now, gathered in dark and shining lines upon the gutters, it crept sluggishly along the crooked streets, pausing every now and then, as if uncertain whether to sink into some noisome grating or pursue its melancholy journey further on.

You might have walked for half a mile amid those lanes and alleys and never guessed, from any stirring at door or window, that Christian creatures lodged within. All was closed, barred, muffled up; and only here and there, where poverty is too poor for shutters, a rag, a board less rotten than its fellow, or a cracked pane supplied their office, there shone a whimpered light, faint as the hopes of those it served. And yet the city lived—oh, how intensely! Wretched men sat wakeful on heaps of rags, calculating with dull ferocity how long a certain crust might be shaven and pared, and soaked and stewed, to serve the hunger of to-morrow; children, whose whole inheritance was want, lay huddled together, dreaming of meats of which they had never tasted, with the fierce delight of young emperors feasting in marble halls.

It was in a quarter much given over to such sights, and much neglected by every species of improvement but that which gives new shapes to misery, that a narrow street, too proud to be a lane and too dismal to be anything like a street, thrust itself askew between two nobler thoroughfares, like a beggar elbowing into the society of gentlemen. Number Seven in this obscure assemblage of tottering tenements had a face so pinched and crooked, that if houses, like men, carried their histories in their countenances, you would have said it had outlived three honest generations and come at last to keep company with rogues.

Its door, being obstinately too small for its frame, left a permanent grimace of a crack above and below; its windows, being at war with the very idea of squareness, leaned inward and outward in such a staggered and irresolute way, that the whole front appeared to be listening perpetually to some tale of sorrow told in the street. Over the door there hung a board, once indicative of its business, now so peeled and weather-beaten that what had been a golden key might as well have been a withered parsnip, and the letters, once boasting the name of “P. GRIGSBY, DEALER IN—” came at last to a modest confession of “P. G, EA—,” leaving the curious to fill up the remainder as best their wits might allow.

Yet this same shop, so decayed in body and in reputation, held within it one treasure which it had not always possessed: to wit, a child. There are people who think children as common as sparrows, and no more to be wondered at than those small and humble birds; but in a place so worn by use, so eaten into by sorrow, so patched with patience and ragged with despair, a child’s face is a marvel. It is a white flower sprung between the stones of a prison-yard; a little strain of music heard through the clank of chains.

Her name—for we must take early acquaintance with her, since you and I will follow her steps through many weary places—was Kitty. How she came there, what strange winds of fortune had blown that slight figure into so close a corner of the world, you shall learn, if you have but patience to walk softly with me through the darkness of Number Seven, and listen, as the poor old house listens, for the low voices that speak of sorrow and of love behind its crooked walls.

title: The Aspern Papers

It was years ago, in that elastic season of youth when one’s designs have the merit of appearing adventurous because they are not yet ridiculous, that the scheme of which I am now to give an account first took hold of me. I cannot pretend that it was then accompanied by the innumerable hesitations and scruples which later came to hang upon it like barnacles on a ship’s keel; at the beginning it presented itself in an aspect of perfect candour, almost of piety. I believed, with a sincerity that I should be glad to recover in other matters, that I was engaged to rescue from imminent dissolution the fragments of a great man’s mind.

It was in Venice that my notion ripened, and it is Venice that must bear the moral responsibility, if there be any, of what I finally did. That city, which seems to float on its own past, has the effect of making one’s conscience also a little amphibious. Principles that elsewhere would stand firm on dry land acquire, amid so much reflected light and wavering water, something of the shimmer of the canals. I had gone there to pursue a shadow and I found a conspiracy of air and architecture in its favour.

The shadow was that of Edmund Valery, whose verse, you will remember, has never ceased to be discussed by persons of sensibility, though its author has been half a century in his grave. To say that I admired him would be paltry; I inhabited him. I had ransacked libraries, bullied heirs, courted librarians, submitted to old aunts and older solicitors, all with a zeal that left me, as I suppose zeal must always leave one, alternately elated and ashamed. Yet through all my rummaging there remained a set of things which continually eluded my hand, the rumour of which followed me from catalogue to catalogue like a tantalising misprint: the letters—perhaps even the journal—addressed, during his last Venetian sojourn, to the lady whom biographers have agreed, faute de mieux, to call “the foreign friend.”

Her name, if she had ever permitted it to be printed, escaped me; her identity, like the rustle of her gown on Valery’s staircase, had died with the last generation. Only a few scurrilous contemporaries hinted at a liaison, and these were precisely the persons whose testimony could not be decently invoked. Still, the letters must have existed; Valery was not a man to bear such feelings as he unmistakably wore in his Venetian odes without permitting them, at some hour of weak reason and strong ink, to betray him. To trace these letters, to bring them into the light, became at last, I confess it, not so much a literary enterprise as a personal passion.

It was the old Marchesa Loredan who, by an accident of conversation, first gave my passion a local habitation. We were seated in the dusky sala of her crumbling palazzo, that melancholy museum of the Loredan resignations, when she remarked—she had a genius for the irrelevant—that Englishmen were the only foreigners who still cared to inquire about “la povera Signora N—.” The name, though partly lost in her cough, struck my ear like the fall of a coin in an empty purse.

“Signora N—?” I repeated, as carelessly as the quickened beat of my heart would permit.

“Ma sì,” the Marchesa returned, with that little tired malice which made her seem to have grown old in order to enjoy her own anecdotes the longer. “The friend of your poeta, of that signore who wrote verses to my lagoon and quarrelled with my gondoliers. She had rooms in a house of my husband’s family, at San Barnaba. A strange person, very silent. They say she kept a box that nobody might touch, not even the priest when she thought herself dying.” 

“A box?” I ventured.

“A chest, perhaps. An old coffer. Dio mio, how you fasten upon the furniture of a dead woman! It is doubtless eaten by rats. Unless,” she added, turning her faded eyes upon me with a little more light than I liked, “unless your English curiosity has already bought it.”

author: Elizabeth Gaskell
title: Curious, if True: Strange Tales

gpt_opening:
It was, I think, in the autumn of the year ’57, when the leaves in our northern counties hang long upon the bough, as though reluctant to confess the summer past, that a little incident befell me, so trivial in its outer circumstances, so strange in its inward significance, that I have never yet succeeded in speaking of it without a shiver, half of mirth and half of awe. If I set it down now, after the lapse of near twenty years, it is less with the presumption of instructing any one wiser than myself, than with the humble hope that certain perplexities, long fostered in the silence of my own heart, may lighten and disperse when fairly looked in the face by candle-light, like perturbed spirits obliged to show their features when named.

At that time I was staying with my cousin, Mrs. Denbigh, at her house in the neighbourhood of Barford,—a grey, many-gabled place, with windows that blinked like half-shut eyes under their heavy brows of stone, and long passages, where one’s own footfall ran before one in a sort of terrified companionship. You will perhaps say that such an abode would of itself suffice to furnish forth any quantity of ghostly imaginings; and I, who went thither a very matter-of-fact spinster of eight-and-thirty, with more thought of the flannel for the schools than of the spirits of the air, came away with a different measure, I confess, of the unseen around us.

My cousin was a cheerful, brisk little woman, rather given to laughing at what she called “old wives’ tales,” and desirous, I suspect, to establish before me a character of particular enlightenment. She would ring the bell after dusk, and have every blind pulled carefully down, saying, with a significant look towards me, that she chose to keep the draughts out, and the superstition in; and then she would bid the maids bring in more candles “for Miss Margaret, who likes antiquities, and may chance to see one walking on the terrace, if we give her light enough.”

I bore these small sallies with the patience due to relationship, and perhaps with some of that self-complacency which creeps over us when we consider ourselves superior to the fear imputed to us. I had my work-basket, and my cousin her netting; the curtains were drawn, the fire burned clear, little jets of blue and red playing amongst the coals like imprisoned thoughts, and the wind went crying round the corners of the house, as children do when they are put to bed too early. We sat snug within; yet, in spite of the warmth and the homely odour of burning logs, there was that in the long moan of the air, and the occasional pattering of withered leaves against the panes, that made my needle go less steadily than was its wont.

“You look as solemn as if you expected an apparition,” cried my cousin, glancing up. “Take care, or you will have one. I am persuaded that half the ghosts in stories are only the offspring of serious faces and dim lights.”

“Then we are in no danger to-night,” I answered; “for the lights are good, and your face, at least, is not of the serious kind.”

“Ah, you do not know,” she replied, with a little toss. “They tell me that this house has its legend, like all respectable houses of its age; but I have never troubled myself to master the particulars. Old Dyson, the housekeeper, is the authorised chronicler; if you are bent upon being frightened, I will have her in with the tea.”

It was spoken heedlessly, but the word once uttered seemed to take to itself a sort of authority. The fire gave a louder crack just then, as though it applauded the proposal, and somewhere along the upper gallery I heard the dull, uncertain sound as of a door that had stood a long while unused swinging to upon its hinges. My cousin’s busy fingers paused an instant; then she laughed—rather more loudly than there was need—and pulled the bell.

“After all,” she said, “the evening is long; and if we must have a ghost, we may as well choose it for ourselves, and have it in the shape of a story.”

author: Arthur Conan Doyle

gpt_opening:
It is, I conceive, the peculiar misfortune of my life that I have never been taken with full seriousness at the very moments when I have been most serious. In affairs of the heart, in my humble essays at journalism, and most notably in that strange adventure which I am now compelled to set upon paper, I have found myself continually stumbling against that barrier of polite incredulity which passes with my friends as a sense of humour. 

I do not complain. There is no ink so corrosive as self-pity. I merely state the circumstance as a warning to those who may be inclined to toss aside this narrative before they have given it fair hearing. The things which I am about to tell are not merely out of the common; they are, I dare assert, beyond what even the most heated romancer of cheap periodicals would venture to submit to his long-suffering public. Nevertheless, I have seen what I have seen; I have handled what I have handled; and if this record fails to convince, it is not the fault of my facts but of my telling.

It was upon an October afternoon, grey and raw, with a thin drizzle so fine that it seemed less to fall from the sky than to be sifted from the very texture of the air, that I first encountered Professor Aldous Murgatroyd. The scene was the reporters’ room of the Gazette, a dingy apartment whose bare boards, frayed linoleum, and ink-stained desks disclosed with brutal frankness the economy of modern journalism. Four of us were there at the time, all of us young, none of us prosperous, and each gnawing his own particular bone of professional frustration. 

Williams of the Sporting column was cursing softly over a misprinted score. Jenkins, whose talents were purely imaginative and therefore wholly wasted upon the City page, sat biting his pen and staring at the rain. As for myself, I was endeavouring to coax some life into a singularly lifeless account of a municipal drainage committee, when the door was pushed violently open and our office boy, Thomas, shot into the room as if propelled by some explosive force from behind.

“There’s a cove downstairs askin’ for a scientific man,” he announced. “Editor says as how it’s you or nobody, Mr. Harrow.”

I have never pretended to science, though a misspent youth among microscopes and galvanic batteries has left me with a smattering of terms which I am sometimes rash enough to employ. Our editor, whose knowledge of his staff was limited to our surnames and our salaries, had, it appeared, credited me with an authority which I was painfully aware that I did not possess.

“A scientific man?” repeated Williams, looking up with a grin. “There you are, Harrow. They’ve found a new bacillus in the drains and want someone to christen it.”

“Show him in, Thomas,” I said, with more boldness than I felt.

The man who entered in response to this summons was one whom you would notice in any assembly. He was tall, though stooped, and spare almost to emaciation, but there was a nervous vitality in his movements and a fierce alertness in his deep-set grey eyes that gave an impression of forcefully compressed energy. A mane of iron-grey hair, untrimmed and rebellious, sprang from his high forehead and fell in disordered locks about his temples. His chin was long, clean-shaven, and decisive, his nose aquiline, and his mouth firm to the point of obstinacy. The first impression was of an eagle which had somehow been compelled to adopt human dress and manners, and resented the necessity.

“Which of you is Harrow?” he demanded, in a voice that crackled like burning thorns.

author: Stephen Crane
title: War is Kind
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Stephen Crane's War is Kind. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
The battalion went forward as if sleep-walking.

They were thin men and broad men and boys with scarcely any hair upon their faces, and they all moved with one motion, as though some invisible rope dragged at their chests. Their heavy shoes made soft, dull sounds in the dust of the road. The sun sat in the sky like a brass plate and burned upon the steel of their rifles until the barrels looked like little streaks of fire.

A drum somewhere beat a lazy tattoo, as if ashamed.

Beside the column rolled a crooked stream of wagons, jolting and creaking, the mules’ ears twitching at every shot that muttered on the horizon. The drivers cursed in colorless voices. Their oaths fell upon the road and broke into white dust.

From time to time the men looked up to the hills, which watched them like grave, green spectators. In the folds of the hills small puffs of smoke blossomed and faded, like unenthusiastic flowers, and then the delayed cry of the guns came down the air in long, wobbling moans.

The lieutenant in front wore a new sword and a very old face. His eyes had been young once. Now they were two little blue ashes. He rode a gaunt horse whose ribs showed plainly, as if some careful hand had drawn them with charcoal. The horse lifted his knees high, delicately, like a lady crossing a puddle, for the road was torn in places by old shells and scarred with the footprints of regiments that had gone this way and dissolved into the earth.

A little private named Dorrie walked near the rear ranks. His cap sat foolishly on the back of his head, and he had put a white handkerchief inside his collar so that it hung down like the beginning of wings.

He muttered prayers that had no endings.

Around him the men carried their fear like an additional piece of equipment which the quartermaster had issued without explanation. They shifted it from shoulder to shoulder, and none of them knew how to pack it comfortably.

On a small hill to the right, a farmhouse stood with its windows dark and square, like the eye-sockets of a skull. Once, there had been a cherry tree by the door. Now the cherry tree was a black stick. A child’s red shoe lay in the yard and shone in the sun with a ridiculous courage.

The army passed this house in silence, except for one man who laughed. It was not a merry laugh. It tumbled out of his mouth like a handful of broken glass. His comrades did not rebuke him, for each man had in his breast some wild sound which he was afraid to hear.

Foreign birds circled above the column and made little cries of astonishment.

Far ahead, where the road sank into a hollow, there was a commotion of flags, and officers rode back and forth like anxious insects. The men could see only the tops of the flags, waving and bowing with solemn politeness to the enemy.

The world had become very small—only road, and hills, and sky, and the place toward which they went and from which they would not return in any orderly fashion.

The chaplain rode along the flank. His thin black coat flapped about his legs like a desperate crow. He had a Book under his arm, fat with promises. When he opened his mouth, the dust entered first, and then the words came after, stumbling, uncertain.

He told them that God was watching.

The men thought, each in his mute fashion, that it is a curious thing to be watched at such a time.

author: Stephen Crane
title: The Monster and Other Stories

gpt_opening:
In the year when the railroad men first talked of putting a second track through the marshlands, the village of Harlowe appeared to the traveler as something that had yawned and forgotten to shut its mouth. It spread along the road in a crooked line of unpainted houses, sagging porches, and shops that looked as if they had been nailed together in a hurry and then neglected through a sort of fatigued contempt. At either end of the street a wide field began at once, as though the town had been abruptly abandoned in the middle of a thought.

On market days, when the farmers came in with squealing pigs or crates of hens taking their indignation loudly to the public air, the place was touched with an uneasy liveliness. But upon most afternoons, a man could walk from the blacksmith’s, where an anvil sounded with a sullen regularity, to the white church standing with stiff, offended dignity at the far end, and meet nothing on the road but a scavenger dog or a drifting sheet of newspaper that gave an air of shabby mystery to the dust.

The inhabitants had the settled appearance of persons who had accepted long ago that nothing final would happen to them. They went about their affairs with a manner of covert expectation, as if they half-believed the next wagon turning the corner might bring a singular, perhaps disagreeable, revelation, but they were far from wishing to hurry its approach. Their gossip, conducted in low voices on store steps and under the shade of reluctant trees, concerned chiefly the doings of their neighbors, the state of the crops, and those distant crimes and catastrophes which, read in the Weekly Banner, caused them to inflate the narrow walls of their own existence with a proud horror.

Into this community there had come, some years before, a man named Ezra Vole. He arrived on a drizzling autumn evening, seated on the back of a freight-wagon among a confusion of baskets and a clock with a cracked face. The circumstance of his entrance, being devoid of any theatrical quality, would have disappointed more ambitious towns; yet Harlowe, having no practice in such spectacles, took a grave interest in it. The children pursued the wagon with the unashamed curiosity of those who have nothing to conceal, and at the corner by Willet’s store, three elders lowered their pipes to note in silence the stranger’s thin, long body and the angular set of his shoulders.

Ezra Vole established a watchmaker’s shop in a low, brick building that had previously given a reluctant shelter to three unsuccessful trades—a cobbler, a picture-framer, and a woman who had undertaken to sell confections of such inhospitable aspect that the town had finally suspected them of a medicinal purpose. It was said that Vole repaired clocks that were not worth repairing and kept them ticking out of a private obstinacy, as if he had resolved that, in Harlowe at least, machinery should never admit defeat.

He lived alone above his shop in two rooms that looked down upon the main street with an air of dingy surprise. No wife, no relation, no dog, no yellow canary in a cage ever appeared to mitigate the rigid solitude of his windows. In a village where even bachelorhood was expected to be noisy and social, his taciturn isolation gave the women an anxiety which they disguised as a moral concern. They agreed that it was not natural for a man to sit by himself all evening listening to the slow heart-beats of crippled clocks, and they waited with a patient, almost kindly, assurance for the moment when such unnaturalness must declare itself in some proper scandal.

Up to the June morning when the story properly begins, they had been disappointed.

The wind, which had been boisterous all day among the chimneys, sank at the fall of evening to a kind of weary sighing, as though the city herself were out of breath with the labours of the light. From my window, which overlooked a crooked back lane off the Canongate, I could observe the last pallor of the western sky dying upon the tall house-tops, and the lamps, one by one, start into a dingy life along the thoroughfare. It was an aspect of things well fitted to sober a merrier spirit than mine; for, although still in the greener side of youth, I had come already to that pinch of circumstance when a man’s courage is his only patrimony, and his memories, such as they are, his chief society.

I was at this time lodged in a melancholy house of many stories, whose cracked gables and dejected chimneys leaned together like conspirators. The stair was narrow and black as a well; the bell, when it sounded, had the startled note of a creature long unused to be disturbed; and the landlady, a widow of some experience in adversity, went about the place with a candle in her hand and misfortune in her face. To her I paid my few shillings on the Saturday, and to my Creator such thanks as I could muster that there were any shillings left to pay.

It was on an evening in the dull close of November, the air thick with the smell of coal-smoke and the promise of rain, that the incident befell which I now set myself to relate. My mind had been that day employed upon the dreariest of reckonings. I had taken stock, not only of my wardrobe and my credit, but of my remaining expectations, and had found all three running very low. The fire in my small grate, like my spirits, burned but faintly; the candle guttered and spat as though it, too, had private grievances; and the only sound in the room was the scratch of my pen, wandering aimlessly among figures that would not be reconciled.

I had just arrived at a particularly dispiriting sum, which left me with no more than four pounds between myself and that last extremity of fortune where a man grows acquainted with pawnbrokers and the inside of cheap eating-houses, when a new sound stole into my hearing. At first I supposed it to be merely some trick of the wind in the chimney; but as I paused and listened, I made out quite clearly a footfall upon the stair—slow, heavy, and singularly resolved.

Now in that house visitors were rarities. The other lodgers, a shy congregation of needle-women and retired sergeants, were seldom honoured with company; as for myself, I had not so much as a dog that might claim me for its acquaintance. The step, therefore, assumed in my imagination an instant importance. I heard it mount from landing to landing with the same measured progress, pause a moment as though the climber took breath, and then advance again. Presently it came to a stop upon my own floor; there followed the faint jingle of metal, and after that, a knock—delivered softly, it is true, yet with a firmness that admitted of no mistake.

I am not ashamed to confess that I hesitated. In my short course through this world I had learned that ill-luck has a door-key to every man’s lodging, and often presents herself in the most respectable attire. However, it is not in my nature to play the coward to an unanswered summons. I rose, therefore, pushed back my chair (which protested upon the boards with a squeal very expressive of its antiquity), took my candle by the neck, and went out into the chill passage.

The knock was repeated as I stood with my hand upon the latch; and this time, though still decorous, it carried with it a sort of impatient authority, such as might belong rather to a creditor than a friend. With a heart that beat somewhat faster than ordinary, and with I know not what foolish expectation of change—whether for better or worse I could not have told—I turned the handle and opened wide the door.

Those who in after years learned to associate the name of Margaret Ellwood with a few small volumes, grave and tender in their tone, are apt to imagine for the writer a life of collected leisure, of chosen solitude, and of outward circumstances not wholly adverse to the cultivation of letters. They conceive a quiet room, somewhat old-fashioned, where the light falls gently upon ordered books; a woman of middle age, pale but composed, sitting by the window with her pen beside her Bible; and a circle, not broad indeed, yet considerate, of friends and kinsfolk, who have resigned themselves to the oddity of her pursuits, while half esteeming that which they cannot wholly understand. 

No image could be more unlike the actual beginning of her days.

Margaret was born in the autumn of 1817, in a small manufacturing town of the West Riding, at a season when the hills were growing dun and purple, and the short evenings sent the operatives home along the damp, ill-paved streets in a murky twilight that seemed rather to collect than to disperse the smoke. Her father, Mr. James Ellwood, was at that time book-keeper in a cotton-mill, and lived, with his wife and then three children, in a narrow house that formed part of a crooked court, entered under an arch, where rain-water lingered in stagnant pools, and the wind, when it chose to visit so obscure a place, came with a kind of angry fierceness, as if impatient of the constraint.

Of the interior of that house, early recollections, preserved by the youngest of the family, show us little beyond a certain painful cleanliness, and the ceaseless presence of figures passing to and fro with anxious faces, or sitting long in silence with the work fallen from their hands, in that sort of fixed brooding that betrays an habitual acquaintance with disappointment. There were solid, high-backed chairs; a table which, even on week-days, aspired to the dignity of a Sunday aspect; two little shelves of books, chiefly sermons, a “Pilgrim’s Progress,” and a large Bible, grown brown and loose in the binding. The windows, scrupulously polished, looked only on the dead wall of the adjoining house, where a patch of auburn moss, and once a solitary sunflower, were all that spoke of Nature’s insistence, even in the narrowest corners afforded to her.

Margaret’s mother, a grave, delicate woman, who had been the daughter of a dissenting minister in a remote dale among the northern fells, brought into this confined and smoky dwelling certain traditions of purer air and ampler horizons, which, though she seldom uttered them at length, made for her children a faint, yet constant, background of comparison. She had early learned the hard lesson of submission. A girlhood spent under the strict but affectionate rule of a father who believed that the soul’s progress was best forwarded by the denial of almost every temporal pleasure, had given to her manner something of that stillness which is not so much peace as the habit of expecting little. Yet there remained in her, as in most natures once touched by the beauty of loneliness and wide skies, a clinging memory of moor and stream, and of those mountain shadows that travel, in slow grandeur, over fields and hamlets which scarcely seem conscious of their visitation.

It would be inaccurate to say that Margaret, in her first years, listened with rapt attention to these rare reminiscences. Children are not chiefly impressed by description; it is the tone, the broken pause, the sudden brightening of the countenance, that fixes itself upon their memory. And so, long before she had shaped for herself any distinct notion of the place from which her mother came, she had gathered, from many slight indications, that somewhere beyond the walls of their town, beyond the mills, beyond the low, smoke-stained hills which bounded her visible world, there lay another region, where life moved, not to the clang of wheels and the hoarse cry of the over-driven, but to some gentler and more accordant sound.

It was in the latter end of September, of a year now too long gone by for me to name without a certain shock of age, that I found myself once more in the old town of Claverick, and, standing by the bridge parapet, looked down upon the brown and troubled river of my boyhood. The leaves were already spun with yellow, and went in hand-breadth fleets upon the water, turning and wheeling like defeated armies upon the tide; the air had that sober brightness which belongs to autumn rather than to spring, and the very stone of the houses, long weathered by sea-fogs and the continual drift of rain, took a mellower colour under the declining sun. A bell, somewhere within the town, was tolling the hour, slow and solitary, and the sound mixed itself with the hollow murmur of the weir and the more distant, sea-like note of the wind upon the moors.

I had come, as men will come, being no wiser than my kind, to view again those places where the foundation of my life had been so rudely and yet so firmly laid. I cannot say what spirit moved me; whether it was penitence, or some vain curiosity to measure the man against the boy, or that tenderness, half sweet and half unmanly, which comes to us with the grey hairs. But I know that for a little while, leaning upon the warm stone and watching the leaves whirl to their dissolution, I was whole years younger in my own thought; and there rose up so clear before me the image of my earliest friend, and with him the beginning of all my fortunes, that I may be said to have seen my very childhood as a picture in a frame of sunlight.

This friend of whom I speak, and whose name I shall give as Francis Denholm, was at that time the only son of the richest merchant in Claverick; and I, who narrate these passages, was the orphan nephew of old Mr. Carvel, the schoolmaster. His father’s house, with a high-walled garden running down almost to the river’s lip, stood there upon the opposite bank, so that in those days, when I was a ragged laddie, more fit, as the phrase goes, to herd swine than to cons my Latin, I would look across from my uncle’s schoolroom and see the chimneys of Mr. Denholm smoking very comfortably against the morning sky. You will understand that to a boy bred up upon porridge, the glimpse of such prosperity had a flavour almost of romance; and yet, if I had been shown in a glass the manner in which that man’s wealth was to cast so dark a shadow on my subsequent life, I might well have envied the poorest fishwife that ever cried her wares beneath his windows.

It is needful that I should be particular, for these events, trivial in themselves as some may appear, are all of a piece with what followed, and I cannot tell where the insignificant ends and the important begins. Indeed, if I have learned anything in the school of experience, it is that the least of our actions goes out from us like a messenger, and, by roads undreamed of, returns again to sit at our own board.

At the time of which I write I was in my fifteenth year, an awkward, long-legged creature, with a great aptness for books, a quicker temper than became my station, and such a stock of pride as would have served a marquis. My uncle, who feared God, loved learning, and understood neither boys nor women, had marked me down in his own mind for the ministry, and spared no labour to make a scholar of me. Under that dry and crabbed exterior he carried a heart that would have melted like wax at a generous word; but the word was seldom spoken, and he passed in the town for a hard man because he was a silent one.

It was upon my return to Claverick, then, with the dust of many roads on my coat and the first silver in my hair, that I stood upon the bridge and thought of Francis Denholm. I had last seen him some twenty years before, upon a night so black with storm and human passion that it seems to me even now rather the invention of a feverish mind than any passage from a Christian life. The remembrance of that hour, long kept down by business and the bustle of foreign scenes, rose over me like a tide; and I knew, as surely as I heard the water snarling at the piers beneath me, that I was come there not by any choice of my own, but by the unrelaxing hand of destiny, to look once more upon the face of my first friend and my first enemy.

author: Mark Twain
title: Roughing It
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Mark Twain's Roughing It. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
When I was a boy, which is a misfortune I share with most of the human race, I had a habit of believing whatever printed matter fell under my nose. This was due less to my faith in the world’s honesty than to my ignorance of its printing presses. I supposed that if a man took the trouble to set up type and pay for paper and ink, he would naturally tell the truth, because lying is cheaper spoken than published. It took thirty years and a dozen newspapers to relieve me of that superstition.

It was in those raw, credulous days that I first made up my mind to go West and gather a fortune, an education, and perhaps a bullet, in about equal quantities. I had been nourished on emigrant guides, frontier handbooks, and those pious little almanacs that recommend, on the same page, a cure for colic and a verse from Deuteronomy. They spoke with one voice, and that voice assured me the West was a region where the soil yielded crops the very first time you asked it, gold lay about in such offensive profusion that a gentleman could scarcely walk without apologizing to his boots, and the inhabitants were a hardy but honest race who would cheerfully share their last crust with you and then borrow it back with interest.

Under such generous testimony, I began to feel that my native town was a sort of moral mistake. The streets were paved with mud instead of nuggets; our most savage animals were the church deacons; and if any man offered to share his last crust, it was only because he had a second last crust concealed in his pocket, intended for his own astonishment later. The situation was unbecoming for a youth of my presumed destiny. I grew restless, like a fly in a molasses barrel—very determined, and very slow.

My elder brother encouraged this migratory fever with the noblest philanthropy, for he proposed to stay at home and manage our affairs—which consisted chiefly of a joint proprietorship in two suits of clothes and one indifferent reputation. He said my talents lay in exploration, and that society could ill spare so promising an ornament to the wilderness. I thought this exceedingly handsome in him. It did not occur to me, at the time, that the wilderness had done nothing to deserve such a favor.

The decision being made, the town took the news as a personal compliment. It had believed, all along, that I would come to something if I only went somewhere else. The tavern-keeper shook my hand with an enthusiasm that would have done credit to a life-insurance agent, and the editor of the weekly paper remarked, in an item of seven lines, that “our young townsman contemplates a journey to the Far West, where, we sincerely trust, he may find ample scope for his peculiar abilities.” As no one had ever discovered what those abilities were, the West was clearly the proper place to look for them.

Thus commended to Providence and the stage-line, I packed my worldly goods—a shirt, a second-hand volume of Plutarch’s Lives, and a letter from my mother which I was expected to read whenever I felt wicked—and set about leaving civilization, to see if I could find any.

title: Lost Face

gpt_opening:
In the gray dawn the river smoked. Frost, keen as a flensed bone, bit into the fog and turned it to ragged strips that clung low along the water. Beyond, where the banks lifted and the black spruce showed their combs against the paling sky, the snow lay unbroken and very still. It had not yet taken the track of man that winter. The river was the only thing alive, crawling by under its own breath, dark and slow, with the thin ice forming on the edges and breaking, forming and breaking, in a tired, monotonous whisper.

The man watched it from the cut-bank. His name was Latham, and the name went poorly with the stained moose-hide parka and the ragged beaver hat that had lost its crown. His beard was hoared with rime where his breath had frozen on it. His eyelashes were white, and his eyes, set in the frost-mask of his face, were tired. They had the dull patience of a beaten dog and the restless flicker of calculation that comes of much thinking over small things—meat, a dry match, a willow twig that will burn even when green if you shave it close enough to the heart.

He stood without moving for a long time. To move was to spend warmth, and warmth was the one coin that had become very precious to him. The spruce behind did not stir, and from somewhere down-river a single crack, as of a rifle-shot, announced the freezing of a great cake of ice. The sound came thin and sharp through the cold, and then all was still again except for the low, troubled murmur at the edge of the open water.

“You’ll go out soon enough,” Latham muttered to the river. His voice was hoarse, not from shouting, for there had been no one to shout to these last many days, but from the constant steady breathing of frost that seemed to rasp the throat with fine ground glass. “Soon enough, and take ’em with you.”

He glanced over his shoulder. Twenty paces back from the bank the sled lay overturned where he had pulled it in the night. The dogs were a tangle of bodies and rawhide thongs. One lay quite still, a black-and-tan husky with the snow drifted in a thin, level sheet over its side. The drift was new; the dog was newly dead. The others were not dead, but the difference was not great. They lay on their bellies, heads flat on the snow, and their eyes followed him without the lifting of a lid. Their flanks moved little. In that land, a dog spends life like a gold miner spends dust—carefully, with a miser's hand. These had already overspent.

Farther yet, where a clump of dwarf birch broke the monotony of the spruce, a thing that might have been a sack or a log lay half buried. One mittened hand stuck straight out of the snow, fingers crooked as if reaching. The snow had not drifted evenly there. It had been brushed aside once or twice in the night. The brushing had been done by Latham. He had done it with the same detached thoroughness with which he had once sorted dry from wet in a pile of spruce-twigs.

“Daylight,” he said to the hand. “You held out for it, anyway.”

The hand did not answer. But Latham, who had been far too much alone of late, nodded as though a bargain had been made and he was its unwilling witness.

“I told you it would be cold,” he added, in the tone of a man making idle talk over something that is past mending. “You didn’t believe. Nobody ever does until the Yukon gets its teeth into their bones.”

He turned back to the river. A pale stripe was rising in the east, and the frost-smoke was thinning as the faint light stole into it. With that light came another day in which to travel or to die, and Latham knew he must choose between the two. It is no easy thing, there at sixty below, to be a man and still go on being one.

title: The Pioneers; Or, The Sources of the Susquehanna

The afternoon of a clear day in the early spring of the year 1793 was drawing toward its close, when a small wagon, roughly fashioned from the unseasoned timber of the surrounding forest, descended, by a winding and laborious path, toward the banks of a stream then known only to the hunters and a few adventurous settlers, but which has since, under another appellation, borne upon its bosom the products and the fortunes of a populous region. 

The road—if that name may be given to a track worn rather by the frequent passage of deer and the heavy sliding of uprooted pines than by the wheels of civilized men—clung fitfully to the side of a steep declivity. Above it rose a broken rampart of gray rock, from the clefts of which a few dwarfish pines and the hardier varieties of the beech cast their scant, leafless branches against the tender sky. Below, an abrupt descent, strewn with the ruins of many winters, fell away to the margin of the river, whose dark and still unmastered waters were here compressed between banks of singular boldness and elevation. Over these, the scanty sunshine of the season fell in slanting lines, touching, with a pale and cold lustre, the lingering wreaths of snow that yet harbored in the shaded hollows, and contending feebly with those long shadows that were stealing, with the advance of the day, from the eastern range of hills.

The wagon, whose slow and cautious progress we have thus far traced, was drawn by a pair of horses, whose rough coats and patient, self-possessed motion betrayed that they were long inured to the labor and privation of the wilderness. A youth, whose figure united the elasticity of early manhood with the broader and firmer outline that bespoke no recent escape from boyhood, walked at the heads of the animals, restraining, rather by the tone of an occasionally uttered word than by the cord he bore in his hand, their natural disposition to increase a pace which the difficulties of the descent rendered doubly hazardous. Upon the rude seat of the vehicle sat a man in the decline of life, whose person, though somewhat bowed by years and exercise, still presented an air of command and conscious superiority that no mean attire and no fellowship with the rougher elements of the scene could wholly conceal.

He was wrapped in a coarse surtout of blue cloth, faded less by time than by the exposure to sun and storm incident to the condition of the settler. A broad-brimmed hat, of the kind then much worn by persons of substance in the older states, shaded features which, though seamed by care and bronzed by the winds of many winters, retained a certain regularity and even delicacy of outline. His eye, of a grayish hue, wandered, as the horses advanced, from rock to river, and from the broken acclivities before him to the purple distance of the western hills, with a scrutiny that bespoke, at once, an habitual observation of nature and an interest more awakened than any novelty in the objects might of itself have excited.

By his side, perched somewhat insecurely upon the same narrow plank, was a female figure, slight, and wrapped from throat to foot in a cloak of dark camlet, from which the edge of a quilted petticoat, of richer materials and more delicate colors, occasionally ventured forth, as the motion of the wagon disturbed her guarded posture. The strings of a close bonnet concealed much of her countenance; but enough might be seen, when, in yielding to some sudden lurch of the vehicle, she lifted her face for an instant from the sheltering fold of the cloak, to betray the bloom and anxious curiosity of youth, united to an expression of gentle timidity that harmonized well with the wild and, to her at least, untried character of the scenes around. 

“Have a care, Henry,” said the elder of the party, in a voice that the habits of command had rendered more authoritative than the occasion, of itself, might have demanded; “keep the near horse well in hand, boy. These devil’s-stairs of the backwoods are enough to shake the bones out of a man that has sat in a Christian carriage on a king’s highway. Another season, and I will have a road hewn here that shall shame this Indian goat-path. Sirrah! do you hear me, Henry?”

“I hear you, sir,” returned the youth, without turning his head, though a smile, that struggled between filial reverence and a consciousness of some latent humor in the complaint, passed over his features. “The horses know the hill as well as I, and, may be, better; for they have climbed it often when I have been sleeping sound at home.” 

“Home!” repeated the elder, slowly, letting his eye rest for a moment upon the darkening line of the eastern horizon, over which a few slender wreaths of smoke betrayed the existence of other clearings hidden from their present view by intervening ridges; “ay, we shall see, in God’s good time, whether men will not learn to call this wilderness by that same honored name.”

author: Wilkie Collins
title: The Queen of Hearts

gpt_opening:
If I had been blessed with the sort of imagination that can build castles out of clouds, or people the empty corners of a room with ghosts at pleasure, I might, perhaps, have spared myself the trouble of writing these pages. Being only an obstinate old man, with a stubborn memory that will persist in setting itself against Time, and a trick of observing my fellow-creatures which I never have been able to shake off, I must e’en make the most of the few gifts that are left to me, and submit to tell my story in the plainest manner I can.

The winter of the year eighteen hundred and sixty—never mind the exact figure; it is enough for my purpose that the date belongs indubitably to this century, and not to the last—found me, at sixty-five years of age, in the quiet possession of my bachelor comforts, my bachelor prejudices, and my bachelor liberty, in a trim, square house of red brick, standing back modestly from one of those by-streets of London which the omnibus-conductor passes with contempt, and the tax-collector with familiarity.

My name is Dunstan. The Christian name I was cursed with at my baptism—bestowed on me from a family vanity which it is needless to perpetuate here—has been effectually extinguished under the weight of my surname. I am “Mr. Dunstan” to my landlady, my boot-maker, and my banker; I was “Dunstan” to my school-fellows and brother officers; and I am, I presume, “Old Dunstan” to such of my juniors as have met me in later life, and have an ill-regulated propensity to speak of their elders with irreverent abbreviation.

I had served my country for five-and-twenty years in that mildly heroic branch of the public service known as the Commissariat. The excitement of providing other men with shoes that pinched and rations that never were enough, had been the romance of my existence. When the authorities at last permitted me to retire, with a pension that alternately inflated and humiliated me, I selected London, that inexhaustible museum of oddities, as the safest place in which a lonely man could grow old, without absolutely mouldering away from inaction.

On the particular evening with which my narrative opens, the snow had begun to fall—not with the modest reticence of an English fall of snow, but with an immoderate profusion which reminded me of certain over-doings of Nature that I had once witnessed in Canada. It fluttered at my window; it hissed upon my fire; it lay, a white, insinuating finger, along the ledge of my book-shelves. To an unphilosophical mind, these little domestic invasions of the weather might have been provoking. To me, who had always found my only real society between the covers of books, they were merely additional reasons for not going out.

I had drawn my chair a little nearer to the fire, arranged my slippers with military precision on the fender, and taken up my old friend Clarendon, with the amiable intention of discovering in the miseries of the great Rebellion some consolation for the small vexations of daily life, when my house-bell pealed with such sudden violence and in such unconscionable repetition as at once to suggest either a conflagration in the street or an invasion of the premises.

I laid down Clarendon with a sigh—for I am of opinion that a man ought to be permitted to choose his own interruptions in this world—and listened. The bell rang again, with an urgency that shook the very respects of the neighbourhood. My landlady’s footsteps (she is stout, and does not disguise it) travelled along the passage with unwonted expedition; the door was opened; and the next sound that reached me was the recognizable voice of a woman, raised—not, I think, in indignation, and not, I am sure, in sorrow—but in that particular key of tremulous excitement which denotes a person who has something extraordinary to communicate, and is not quite certain whether the communication will ensure her a welcome or a rebuff.

“Tell Mr. Dunstan,” said the voice, “that I have brought back his past life to him—and that it is waiting at the door.”

author: H. Rider Haggard
title: Heart of the World

gpt_opening:
It is now more than twenty years since those events befell of which I am about to write—years that have dealt kindly enough with my body, though not so gently with my spirit. The hand that traces these lines is still firm, yet the hair above it is grizzled and the eyes have looked too often upon death and wonder to view the common sights of life as other men behold them. I set down this history, not because I seek belief—for I have learned that men credit only such marvels as fall within the narrow circle of their own experience—but because memory grows tyrannous with age and drives me to ease my mind by speech, though it be but to these dumb white pages.

Moreover, I have a reason beyond the quieting of my own heart. Somewhere in that great and secret land which lies far to the south of the settled republics—a land whose rivers flow nameless through forests that never saw a white man’s foot—there is a people who wait. For what they wait, and why they chose me, of all the wandering sons of Europe, to bear their strange commission, I shall presently set forth. Enough that I promised, if I lived, to tell their tale—yes, and to warn a world that would mock the warning—before my time should come to join those whose bones whiten in the lost valleys beyond the Cordillera.

In these latter days I dwell in a little house upon the outskirts of a New England town, where the strong sea-wind comes up over the marshes and rattles the casements in winter. My neighbours know me for a retired engineer who has seen service in many countries; occasionally, when the snow lies deep and the evenings are long, some young fellow will draw me to the tavern hearth with questions of the West and the South, of the great mines among the Andes, or the forgotten cities of Yucatan. Then, perhaps, I will tell him something of siege and fever, of brigands or of earthquakes; stories common enough in the rough chronicles of frontier life. But of the greater matter I say little or nothing. Even now, as I take up my pen, I hear the cheerful clatter of wheels in the village street and the shrill voices of children at play; and the thought comes to me how mad and monstrous my narrative must sound amid these homely noises.

Yet it is true—true as the cold light upon the snow yonder; as true as the pain that wakes me at night when the wind blows from the south and brings with it, over uncounted leagues, the phantom scent of orchids and wet earth steaming after rain.

Upon my table, beside this inkstand, lies a small object wrapped in a piece of worn native cloth. Often I catch myself staring at that bundle as though I expected it to stir and speak. Sometimes I lay my hand upon it, half fearing, half longing to feel again the thrill that once it gave me. When all is written, if I am spared to finish this work, I shall burn what it contains; for the thing has brought enough blood and sorrow into the world. Yet I cannot begin my account without first speaking of it, since from it, as from a seed, sprang the whole mad growth of adventure which was to tear my life from its quiet furrow and fling it, a broken stalk, upon strange and stormy seas of fate.

North Ashfield lay in a cup of the hills, so neatly contained that on clear mornings the sky seemed to have slipped down and settled in it. The white steeple of the meeting-house pricked the blue like a needle left standing upright in a pincushion; around it the houses straggled, all of them a little faded and stooping, as though they had been put there in haste and forgotten. On three sides the wooded slopes drew in, crowding closer every year, like shy people edging up to a conversation that did not concern them.

Toward the south the road ran out between the meadows and climbed to the notch. Whoever had named it Pleasant Notch had perhaps done so in irony, for the wind, funneled there in winter, sharpened itself on the bare ledges, and even on summer days the pass kept a remembrance of cold, as if hoarding a grudge against the short season that forced warmth upon it. Beyond the notch, people said, lay other towns—bigger, noisier, where the trains stopped as a matter of course and not as a condescension. The young in North Ashfield listened to such talk as to the accounts of a foreign country which one might some day be summoned to govern, but in which no one one knew had ever set foot.

On the morning when Ally Hatherley first thought the hills looked lower than they had the day before, the bell at the factory had already rung twice. The broad, stammering clang, meant to sound brisk and imperative, drifted over the roofs with an air of apology; but Ally heard it, and did not hasten. She was standing on the back step of the Hatherley house, her hands idle in the coarse apron that hung straight from her thin shoulders, and looking up at the gap of the notch as if she expected to see someone come through it.

The yard before her, baked to a gray powder and pricked at the edges with plantain and purslane, ran down into a pasture barred by a stone wall. Beyond that, the land broke into ledges and scrub-oak, then rose in earnest into the thick growth of the hillside. The air even at that early hour had a settled density, as if it had nowhere to go. There was a sweet, heavy smell of clover from the low field on the left, and from over the ridge came, faint and regular, the creak of the hay-rake on the Penfields’ lot.

“Ally!” a voice called from within, sharp yet tired, like a knife used too often on the wrong sort of work. “You stayin’ there till kingdom-come?”

Ally started, but not guiltily. It seemed to her that she had been interrupted not in idling but in some serious business which she could not account for to other people. She turned her head over her shoulder and looked back into the dim kitchen where her mother moved between stove and table with the dry, precise motions of one who has done the same things long enough to have worn grooves in the air.

“I was only lookin’,” Ally answered.

Her mother’s loosened gray hair had fallen low on her neck; she gave her head a little impatient shake, as though the girl’s words clung there like cobwebs.

“Lookin’ don’t get the dishes washed,” she said.

Ally glanced again at the notch before stepping in. The mountains did look lower; or else the sky had somehow risen.

author: H. Rider Haggard
title: Montezuma's Daughter
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of H. Rider Haggard's Montezuma's Daughter. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
Now that my hair is white as the foam upon the bar at the river’s mouth, and my hand shakes so that I can scarce guide the goose-quill across this paper, it comes into my mind that I should set down the story of those things which I saw and did in the hot youth of my body, lest they be buried with me and lost for ever from the knowledge of men. For I, John Redmayne, sometime gentleman-adventurer of Devon, have looked on lands whereof many in England never heard, and have trafficked in strange kingdoms beneath alien stars, and stood in the presence of kings whose very names were unknown in Christendom. 

It is not for my glory that I write, for God knows there is little enough in all my tale of which a Christian man has cause to boast. Blood lies thick upon its pages, my own and another’s, and more than once I went very near to the loss both of my soul and of that earthly life which most men cling to with such desperate fingers. Yet I have been urged to it by those who have heard somewhat of my wanderings, and chiefly by my good friend, Master Thomas Elyot, parson of our parish, who says that in an age given over to traffic in silks and spices, the young grow soft, and may profit by the record of a man who has met naked Fate with a bare sword. 

Also there is another reason which moves me, and that is the memory of a face. Even now, when the candle burns low and the wind rattles in the panes of my little chamber, it rises before me—dark as night, yet lit with such a fire as I have never seen in mortal eyes either before or since. A woman’s face it was, proud and patient and very royal, though no diadem ever pressed that brow in the fashion we know of crowns. For her sake I defied kings, fought battles where no quarter was asked nor given, passed through torments of sea and land, and came at last to a place of shadows from which I was never meant to return. Whether I sinned grievously or whether I was but a tool in the hand of Him who shapes the destinies of men, you who read may judge when I have done.

When first these matters began I was but a lad of one-and-twenty years, newly home from the wars in Flanders, and with no better fortune than the sword at my hip and the strength within my limbs. My father, Henry Redmayne, was already some years dead, having wasted a good estate in following our late Queen’s banner upon the seas, where he won much honour and small profit. My elder brother, Geoffrey, held the remnant of our lands, lying upon the windy coast not far from Dartmouth Haven, and I—being the younger son and so born, as it were, with my face seawards—must needs seek my bread where I could find it.

Thus it came about that, upon a certain autumn evening, when the brown leaves skated along the rutted lanes and the great tides beat heavily upon the shore, I first met the man who was to lead me, though I knew it not then, far beyond the borders of any map that ever was drawn in London town.

author: H. G. Wells
title: The Country of the Blind, and Other Stories
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of H. G. Wells's The Country of the Blind, and Other Stories. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
There are certain incidents, trifling enough in their inception, which yet leave a curious scar upon the mind—events that will not be laid quietly away with the common lumber of memory, but return again and again, changing their colour with each recollection. The thing I have to tell began, so far as any story has a beginning, with a sheet of foolscap and a telegram, and ended—if it has ended at all—among the damp weeds and whispering reeds of a neglected fen.

At the time I was lodging in a mean little street off the Holloway Road, furnishing reviews for the *Chronicle* and small paragraphs of scientific intelligence for whoever would pay for them. The house was one of those dreary, high-shouldered buildings which seem always out of breath. It had ceilings cracked like old china, windows that rattled to every passing van, and, in my particular room, a persistent odour of mice and paraffin. Yet I was not unhappy there. My writing table commanded a dingy prospect of chimney-pots and an oblique section of sky, useful enough for weather-wisdom, and the landlady, though noisy, had the domestic virtues.

It was an evening in late October, the air thick with an equinoctial drizzle that made halos round the street lamps, when the landlady’s youngest—an anaemic child with an unaccountable passion for the police—came up with the telegram. I remember that I had just finished a note on some new photographic method of recording stellar spectra, and was debating whether I could make three columns of it. The yellow envelope lay on the blotting-pad a full minute before I broke it open. Telegrams, in my experience, meant one of three things—unpleasant news, demanded copy, or the recall of a loan; none of them inducements to a hasty curiosity.

It contained but seven words.

“Observe marsh lights at once. Urgent.—Helbrom.”

I read it twice, as if something more might disclose itself between the lines. The name, I suppose, requires explanation. Professor Helbrom was at that time attached, in a vague and largely honorary manner, to one of the lesser colleges at Cambridge. He had a small private income, an immense forehead, and an almost childlike enthusiasm for other men’s problems. Ideas, like migratory birds, were always settling upon him for a night’s rest and then flying on, but he made them welcome while they stayed. I had met him over some controversy concerning Martian canals, and he had shown me a kindness out of proportion to the occasion, lending books and such influence as he possessed with certain editors.

Yet I had not seen him for nearly a year. He had withdrawn, so gossip had it, to some remote district in the east-country, where he might “pursue researches unvexed by academical impertinences.” That was the phrase I remembered. I must confess that, on first reading, his message struck me as no more than another of his whims. “Marsh lights,” I thought, would be will-o’-the-wisps, or some trick of putrefying gases magnified into marvels by frightened rustics.

It was only when I turned the paper over, meaning to cast it aside, that I noticed a faint pencilling below the printed form, as if added at the last moment in a trembling haste.

“Not atmospheric. Not electrical. Not—natural.”

The final word, underlined twice, had its effect. Outside, in the narrow street, the drizzle thickened to a soupy darkness, and somewhere a costermonger’s voice rose and broke, strangely forlorn. I rang the bell for the landlady, and asked her, with a composure I did not altogether feel, at what hour the first morning train for the east-coast towns might be taken from King’s Cross.

author: Jack London
title: Martin Eden
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Jack London's Martin Eden. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
He had come up out of the steerage like a man climbing from a mine, blinking and half-angered by the gentler light. The gangway throbbed beneath his boots, quivered to the slow thud of engines far below the waterline, and beyond the rail the city rose, gray and golden, tier on tier, as if it were something that had grown that way—like rock, or fungus—without will or forethought. Smoke drifted across the harbor, torn into streamers by the wind, and among the streamers the gulls hung and swung, white and idle, like bits of paper tossed and forgotten.

He stood a moment with his duffel-bag slung over one shoulder, shoulder bulged high, lean neck corded against the weight. The bag had the lumpy look of a thing long packed in haste, everything he possessed jammed into it without order. There were tar stains on the canvas, and the rope-lashings had been knotted and re-knotted until they were black with grease from a dozen decks. The whole of it, bag and man, belonged aft, among the shouts of bosuns and the smell of bilge and paint. Yet he had bought a collar for his shirt in Honolulu, and now it rasped at his throat like a lie.

“Step lively, sir!” a dock-hand called, half respectful, half impatient; the word “sir” rubbed wrong against the look of him. He grinned without offense and moved on, the planks drumming hollow under his feet. There was a strange balance to his walk: heavy and loose at once, as if the sea still swung under him so strongly that the firm land felt itself in danger.

The passengers ahead were all in good clothes. Even their baggage looked as though it had opinions of its own and would never consent to be thrown. Brass corners shone on leather trunks; labels of distant ports flamed in red and blue. He read the names as he passed, sounding them in his head the way he might roll strange words in the mouth, testing their shape and savor. Half the ports he had seen only from the fo’c’sle head, shabby and wet, while the fine folk had gone ashore with umbrellas and guides.

He did not envy them; envy was too small. It was more as if something in his chest rose up when he looked, like surf running shoreward, only to fall back foaming when it met the hard sand of what was. He was patched and sun-burned and rough over the knuckles. He had never owned more than one suit at a time. Yet he clamored toward these people in some fashion, not in the way of begging a crust, but as if he had a right to sit at their tables and hear the talk that went on there, to say his own say and make it count.

The idea angered him and drew him in the same breath. He could neither put it off nor wear it properly. It had come to him late, a sort of sickness that had taken hold between watches over the last year, when he had found a tattered book in a bunkhouse in Yokohama and broken himself to the task of spelling out its print by lamplight. Since then the words in books had been like distant lands upon charts, half-real, half-painted dream, promising coasts he had never landed on.

At the foot of the gangplank the crowd stalled. Carriages waited, their glossy horses steaming faintly in the morning chill. A fat man in a long coat bawled orders, directing trunks, cabmen, porters. The fat man’s voice had command in it; he would never have to lift his own bags. The sight struck the sailor with a kind of slow amusement. Here was a fellow who had likely never tarred down a stay in his life, yet the world rushed to obey the crook of his finger.

He shifted his duffel to the other shoulder and looked past the confusion to where the city streets opened like rivers between stone banks. They were canyons, he thought, and along the sides the windows winked in the slanting light. Somewhere in that broken glitter men sat at desks and read papers that told them what to think about the world, while other men bent their backs and never saw three lines of print in a day. He knew very well to which lot he belonged. His palms were tough enough to take rope-burn without flinching; his back knew the length of sweated hours. Yet the notion of it no longer seemed settled as it had once been. There was a crack beginning in the thing, a hairline only, but it ran deep.

“Cab, sir?” A boy with a pinched face and hungry eyes had darted up, hand on the strap of his cap. The title again—and again that little wrongness. 

“No cab,” the sailor answered. “I’ll walk.”

He said it simply, as if it were the easiest thing in the world, to pick up one’s life in a bag and carry it, on foot, into the heart of a city that did not yet know one’s name.
