prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of James Fenimore Cooper's The Spy. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
The evening of a mild day in the early autumn of 1780 was drawing to its close, as a solitary horseman pursued his way along one of those by-roads which, crossing from the great highway of the Hudson to the interior of the country, seemed rather the foot-path of the husbandman than the frequented passage of the traveller. The sun had already sunk behind the ragged line of hills that formed the western boundary of the vale, leaving in the upper heavens a belt of pale and fading glory, while the lower sky was darkened by those dusky masses of vapour which hover around the horizon at the decline of the year, and appear, in their fantastic and ever-changing forms, like the fragments of a mighty banner whose folds are slowly drooping upon the world.

The horseman paused a moment on the brow of a gentle eminence, where the narrow road, turning abruptly from the fields, plunged at once into the deep shadow of a wood, whose spreading oaks and tall chestnuts, retaining yet the greater part of their foliage, stretched their huge arms across the path, and seemed disposed to interdict all further progress. He cast a searching glance around the landscape that lay open behind him, as if doubtful whether he were not already become an object of observation to some unseen follower, and then, drawing his hat more closely over his brow, he suffered the bridle to fall loosely upon the neck of his steed, and moved slowly forward into the obscurity.

In that dim light which precedes the actual coming of night, the features of the stranger might not have been easily distinguished, even had there been any to examine them; yet, in his air and carriage, in the easy seat which he maintained in the saddle, and in the prompt, though quiet, manner in which he controlled the movements of his horse, there was enough to show that he was no stranger to the fatigues of a life of action, and that he who now threaded, almost at hazard, the intricacies of this retired path, was not unaccustomed to encounter dangers of a more formidable nature than the stumbling of his beast among the roots and stones that encumbered the way.

The stillness which reigned in the wood was only interrupted by the occasional rustling of a withered leaf, as it detached itself from the branch, and came eddying to the earth; or by the cry of some startled bird, which, roused from its early slumbers by the tread of the intruder, flitted, with a hurried and uncertain flight, from one dark recess to another. Far off, and mellowed by distance, came at intervals the faint sound of a water-fall, whose white sheet might, perhaps, have been discerned from the hill the traveller had just quitted, had not the growing obscurity already begun to draw its veil over the minuter features of the scene.

After proceeding thus for several minutes, during which the intricacies of the winding path and the deepening gloom demanded rather the care of the horse than that of his rider, the stranger suddenly checked his progress. The road, if such it might be called, here separated into two branches, each as little promising as the other, and both soon lost amid the thickening trees. No finger-post directed the perplexed traveller, nor did any vestige of wheel or hoof appear to have disturbed the thick carpet of moss and fallen leaves by which each was equally overspread.

The horseman bent forward in the saddle, and, placing his hand upon the neck of the patient animal, seemed, for a moment, to surrender his decision to that instinct by which a beast will sometimes guide with more certainty than the reason of man. The steed, however, wearied with the labours of the day, or unused, perhaps, to those forest paths, merely shook his head with a low and discontented neigh, and, planting his feet more firmly beneath him, appeared little inclined to decide upon a course which might prolong his toil.

“It seems, then,” muttered the rider, in a tone in which something like bitterness struggled with a settled composure, “that, in this thicket, as in the greater wilderness beyond, a man must be his own counsellor, and answer for his choice as he may.”  

With these words, and after casting yet another cautious glance around, as though the trees themselves might have ears for his discourse, he guided his horse into the branch of the path which inclined most sensibly towards the river; and the forest, closing behind him, resumed its former air of repose, as if no living thing had ever broken its solitude.

author: Bram Stoker
title: Famous Impostors
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Bram Stoker's Famous Impostors. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
There is, in the long record of human frailty, no manifestation more constant, more protean, nor more perversely attractive to the imagination than that of imposture. War alters frontiers, pestilence thins cities, philosophies wax and wane; yet the Impostor, in one disguise or another, keeps pace with all the centuries. He is the unbidden guest at every banquet of history, the masked figure that glides at the fringe of every solemn procession of kings, saints, and sages. Where there is credulity, there he builds his throne; where there is suffering, there he rears his market-stall.

It would be a grave error to suppose that imposture is the prerogative only of the low-born and the needy. The annals of courts and parliaments, of conventicles and academies, are pricked all over with the pin-marks of false prophets, counterfeit nobles, pretended martyrs, and spurious geniuses. If the tattered mountebank at the fair cheats a wench of her silver penny by a vagrant miracle, so too has the grave philosopher debauched the understanding of nations with a miracle of another order. The difference lies chiefly in the amplitude of the theatre and the magnificence of the costume.

Nor is it to be imagined that the appetite which sustains such characters is confined to the vulgar and unlettered. On the contrary, the eagerness with which the educated and the powerful have embraced imposture, even when it came with a clumsy mask and ill-adjusted cloak, is one of the most humiliating, and therefore most instructive, spectacles in history. Princes have sheltered forged princes; bishops have attested forged revelations; scholars have collated forged chronicles; and all with an earnestness which would be admirable, were it not misapplied. The wit of the trickster is, in many instances, far surpassed by the ardour of his dupes.

The reader who expects to find in these pages merely a gallery of low scoundrels, strutting for an hour upon the stage and then decently whipped from it, will therefore be disappointed. The figure that emerges most distinctly, as one turns over the dust-laden leaves of chronicles and state papers, is less that of the impostor than that of Man himself, unhappily credulous, magnificently self-deceived. The rogue is, for the most part, of mediocre imagination and threadbare invention; it is the world that lends him colour, the audience that furnishes the applause, and the victims who contribute the tragic interest of the play.

If there be any unity in the strange histories which I propose to recount, it will be found less in the methods of the deceivers—though these have their curious resemblances—than in the persistent weaknesses they exploit. Vanity is their first ally, fear their second, and the longing for the marvellous their last and most faithful friend. Whether the impostor comes with a forged patent of nobility, a miraculous relic, a new gospel, or a fabulous machine which shall annihilate time and space, he finds men ready not only to be deceived, but to assist him in perfecting the deception.

In presenting these narratives, drawn from many lands and several ages, I have endeavoured to set forth, as far as the broken evidence allows, not only what the impostors did, but what the world did for them; how suspicion slumbered, how doubt was rebuked as impiety or disloyalty, and how, when the bubble at last broke, the very persons who had most industriously swelled it with their breath were the readiest to trample on its fragments. If some of these characters move us to laughter, and others to indignation, there are a few who may even arrest our pity; for there are impostors who began by deceiving others and ended by deceiving themselves.

The nature of our inquiry, then, is not a mere antiquarian rummaging among discredited scandals. It is a dissection of a perennial malady of the human mind, of which the figures here chronicled are but the passing symptoms. Whether we trace the shadowy career of a supposititious prince, the comfortable miracles of a trading saint, or the elaborate inventions of a scientific charlatan, we shall find always the same old drama in progress—the masquerade of Falsehood in the borrowed garments of Truth, and the strange readiness of mankind to be dazzled by the tinsel when the gold is out of reach.

author: George Eliot
title: The Essays of "George Eliot": Complete
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of George Eliot's The Essays of "George Eliot": Complete. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
It is one of the easier habitudes of the human mind to suppose that its gravest perplexities have arisen in the present generation, and that our fathers, moving in a more contracted circle of circumstance, were preserved from those finer embarrassments of judgment which beset us on every side. We are apt to imagine that a certain rawness in our moral sensibilities, a certain undefined hesitation in pronouncing upon character and conduct, belongs almost exclusively to the intricate society of our own day, and that the men and women who looked out upon the world from beneath three-cornered hats and towering head-dresses were guided by a simpler daylight than that which struggles now through our thickened atmosphere of knowledge and opinion. Yet this persuasion, if we examine it, will be found to spring rather from our vanity than from any sagacious reading of history; for the complexity of life is not a late invention, and the pains of conscientious thought are not the special tax levied upon an age of railways and newspapers.

If we could summon hither some grave personage of the last century—some provincial scholar whose days were divided between his folios and the modest duties of his parish—he would assuredly tell us that, in his time also, there were beliefs which had lost their authority, institutions that commanded obedience without awakening reverence, loud voices abroad in the world demanding a reconstruction of society, and feeble voices within his own breast asking whether he too must cry "Make all things new," or cling, with such quiet tenacity as might be allowed him, to the forms and sanctions in which he had first learned the meanings of justice and piety. The same difficulty which presses upon us—that of discerning how far fidelity to the past is compatible with sincerity towards the present—pressed also upon him, if only he were of that rare order of minds which regard truth as something more than a reverberation of their own preferences.

Indeed, if there is any respect in which the past has an advantage over us, it lies perhaps in this—that men were then less prone to confound rapidity of conclusion with solidity of conviction. The habit of reading thoughts in their briefest form, broken into paragraphs and headed by titles, has encouraged a persuasion that opinions may be taken in at a glance, like the pattern of a ribbon on a shop-counter. We are in danger of forgetting that truth has dimensions not to be mastered by a single look; that it must be walked round and handled, tested now on this side and now on that, and even then will often refuse to accommodate itself entirely to the clumsy angles of our understanding. Our forefathers erred, as we do; but they were not yet inoculated with that impatience which turns from the slow processes of reflection as from some obsolete handicraft, and demands of the intellect the same mechanical speed and facility which it exacts from a steam-engine.

Yet we must not flatter ourselves that the mere recognition of this impatience is enough to exorcise it. The man who, in the midst of hurrying opinions, resolves that he will move slowly, is in danger of mistaking his inertness for deliberation. There is a kind of sluggish conservatism which imitates the outward behavior of thoughtful caution while it remains incapable of that inward travail by which alone a robust judgment is born. It is not less easy to pride oneself on deliberation than on progress; and there is perhaps no posture of the mind more perilous than that which combines an indolent love of old habitudes with a complacent consciousness of being exempt from the vulgar haste of the multitude.

The subject which I propose to consider in these pages is one that continually offers itself at the point where these various moral tempers cross and jostle each other: namely, the relation between our inherited rules of conduct and those novel exigencies of feeling and circumstance which seem, at first sight, to fall outside their jurisdiction. For whether we look at society in its broadest institutions or in the narrow precincts of a single household, we are met everywhere by the same question: how shall a being, whose life is rooted in the past, yet fruitfully accept the claims of the present?

author: Joseph Conrad
title: Tales of Unrest
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Joseph Conrad's Tales of Unrest. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
Every evening, as the extinguished day withdrew itself across the western marshes, the river took on a sulky glow, like a sheet of tarnished silver, begrudging the last of the light. From the veranda of the trading-house one could observe that reluctant dissolution of colour, as one might watch the features fade from the face of a dying man. The palms, reduced to inky strokes upon the glimmering sky, stood in a mournful immobility, guardians of a secret that was perhaps not worth knowing. Now and then, from somewhere in the thickening obscurity, there came the dull splash of a paddle or the cry of an unseen bird, shrill, unaccountable, and instantly regretted by the oppressive silence that closed in after it.

On such evenings I would sit alone, smoking the harsh tobacco of the natives, for there was no other, and contemplating, with that peculiar lassitude which the climate engenders, the slow estrangement of the world from its own shapes and colours. It was at that hour that the house, with its mildewed beams and worm-eaten floor, its rattling shutters and disjointed rafters, seemed most disposed to collapse inward, as though to bury at last the memories, the futile calculations, and the ignoble anxieties that had, for so many years, collected within its shadow.

To call it a trading-house was already a sort of indulgence. It was a remnant, a husk, that had once reflected in its ledger the profitable agitation of distant markets; now it persisted in a kind of obstinate indolence, maintained less by any expectation of gain than by the invisible inertia of habits formed in more energetic times. We trafficked in palm-oil, india-rubber, and, when the river was in humour, ivory that came down from regions where the mapmakers had wisely contented themselves with blank spaces and speculative names. The natives, a small and sullen people, brought their produce to us in leaky canoes, watched our scales with animal suspicion, and departed with such goods as our dwindling stock would permit us to bestow upon them at a profit.

If I say “we,” it is out of adherence to the style of speech I had acquired in the service. In truth there were only three white men at Kamba’s Reach: myself, appointed as agent at a salary that diminished in exact proportion to the remoteness of my post from civilisation; old Jacobsen, who was book-keeper, storeman, and invalid by turns; and a young fellow named Fielding, sent out from Europe with an air of fragile superiority that the first fever swiftly and efficiently reduced.

It was Jacobsen who first spoke to me of Da Silva.

“You observe, captain,” he said—for in that lonely station every superior title was generously distributed—“that the boats from up-river have ceased to come.”

He leaned on the veranda rail, his gaunt figure cut out against the feeble afterglow. The river before us, streaked with uneasy reflections, flowed past in the growing dark with that secretive haste which has, in those regions, something of the character of guilt.

“Three weeks,” he continued. “Not one canoe. It is not natural. The people up there are like the tide—always returning. When they do not return, there is a reason. And in this country”—he made a vague, contemptuous gesture toward the black line of the forest—“reasons are usually men. Or the devil. But here they say it is Da Silva.”

author: Willa Cather
title: The Troll Garden, and Selected Stories
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Willa Cather's The Troll Garden, and Selected Stories. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
When Paulina Harrow came back to Severance, after the ten years which in that small town were spoken of as “her foreign period,” the people who went up in the omnibus from the depot found little in her that answered to their recollections. They had equipped themselves with certain phrases in which they meant to receive her, phrases that had been matured in sewing-circles and on back porches; they had rehearsed the indulgent, neighborly wonder with which they would confront the girl who had presumed to go away and make a life of her own among strangers. But when she stepped into the coach behind the cinnamon-colored bays and took her seat without looking either to the right or the left, their phrases went lame and halted. They were, for the moment, abashed, like actors who find the play altered in rehearsal.

It was not that Paulina had grown handsome. Severance did not require beauty of those who distinguished themselves; success could be more safely pardoned if it were accompanied by plainness. She had, indeed, the same freckled, uneven skin they remembered, and the same hair that did not adapt itself graciously to any mode, but rose in obstinate little waves and ridges above her narrow forehead. The chief difference lay, perhaps, in her look of having done with explanations. The Paulina of the old days had been forever protesting, half in ardor, half in apology, telling people what she intended, what she was trying for, as though life were an examination and she must keep the examiner informed. The woman who came back had the air of having passed, or failed, long ago, and of being now quite indifferent to the result.

She was thin in the extreme, and wore a travelling suit of some soft, foreign stuff the women could not name, which contrived, without ornament, to suggest that it belonged to a climate where people walked quickly and did not sit much in parlors. Her gloves were shabby, and a corner of the veil she wore hung in a point where a Severance girl would have fastened it neatly; but these negligences only made more apparent that curious finish of manner, that confidence in the efficacy of the smallest gesture, which she had somehow acquired.

At the first turn of the dusty road that led up from the depot, the town broke upon them with its familiar, undistinguished completeness. The Harrow place, set back in a thinning grove of maples, showed white and malignant through the trees. The big, square house had the sullen, waiting look which empty houses somehow learn. Its blinds were shut except the two on the north side which old Burris, the man who “did for” the empty dwellings of Severance, always left open for “air.” The front porch, with its lean pillars and its wreck of a swing, had slightly canted forward in the center, so that the whole façade seemed to wear a frown, as though the place had been fretting for ten years and had at last settled into a scowl.

Paulina looked at the house once only. Her glance slid over it as one’s eye passes a person with whom one has long since broken, and to whom no new terms can ever be offered. What she saw, or failed to see, in that single moment might have been read in the sudden compression of her mouth; but the women who watched her were thinking how odd it was that she did not, like any reasonable creature, lean half out of the window and exclaim at the changes. It pleased them to decide at once that foreign parts had made her proud.

author: H. G. Wells
title: The Outline of History: Being a Plain History of Life and Mankind
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of H. G. Wells's The Outline of History: Being a Plain History of Life and Mankind. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
If one could look back upon the history of this planet from some remote and patient vantage in space, the affairs of men would appear at first as a very small and recent incident in a far vaster story. The hills that seem so permanent, the seas that have their ancient names, the cities whose stones we call old, are but of yesterday when set against the long, slow unfolding of the earth. Before any creature trod with a conscious purpose upon its surface, this world had already known ages of silence and change, of fire and water, of unrecorded experiment.

We are apt, in our ordinary thinking, to begin history with kings and battles, with the founding of towns or the writing of chronicles. Yet these are only the later chapters of a book whose earlier leaves are written, not in ink upon parchment, but in strata of rock, in the shapes of mountains, in the fossils embedded in cliff and quarry. To trace the story of mankind with any fairness or clarity, we must first understand that man is not an isolated miracle suddenly dropped into a vacant theatre. He is, rather, the latest and most intricate expression of forces that have been at work for hundreds of millions of years.

The earth, as science now conceives it, was once a molten and tumultuous globe, a burning fragment cast off in the making of the solar system. For ages it spun thus, its surface an inferno of lava and vapor. No plant could root itself there, no animal could breathe that choked and poisonous air. Only as it slowly cooled did the first crust harden; only as vapors condensed did the early seas gather in the hollows of the forming continents. These processes, which we compress into a sentence or two, consumed aeons of duration so prodigious that our human measures of time—years, centuries, dynasties—are pitifully inadequate to express them.

Upon this cooling sphere, under the unremitting play of sunlight, lightning, and the still-active fires within the earth, there occurred, at some unknown point, that most wonderful transition we call the beginning of life. How precisely this came about we do not yet know, and it may be that we shall never know it in detail. But we can perceive that the elemental substances of the planet—carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and their companions—entered into new and ever more complex combinations. Out of that blind chemistry there emerged, somewhere in the warm seas, the first trembling film that could grow, divide, and in some primitive sense inherit the form of its parent.

With that minute and obscure event, the true preface to human history begins. For from that time onward, the story of the earth ceases to be merely a chronicle of rocks and vapours, of upheavals and erosions. It acquires continuity of another kind: a living continuity, passing like a thin, persistent thread through countless forms and transformations. From the invisible beginnings of protoplasm to the appearance of man stretches an unbroken series of generations, each handing on some small modification to the next, each contending with its surroundings, each part of a vast experiment of adaptation.

To follow that experiment from its lowest stages up to the mind of man is the purpose of this book. We shall try to see our own kind, not as something apart and sacrosanct, but as one outcome—perhaps the strangest—of the same natural order that fashioned the coral reef and the dragonfly’s wing. And in so doing, we may gain, not a diminished, but an enlarged sense of what mankind is and may yet become.

author: Kate Chopin
title: At Fault
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Kate Chopin's At Fault. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.

The sun had not yet risen above the long, level line of cypress and gum that bordered the river; but a thin, pallid light, creeping stealthily over the flat landscape, disclosed here and there a patch of color,—a late rose clinging to the gallery railing, a strip of crimson calico on a clothes-line, a boy’s red cap moving slowly along the road that skirted the cane-field. The hush of the early hour brooded over the plantation. Only the monotonous clack of a distant pump, and the occasional stir of some waking bird among the orange-trees, broke the silence that lay between house and quarters, field and river.

David Lemaire sat on the edge of the broad front gallery, his bare feet resting on the lower step, his elbows upon his knees, his hands supporting his chin. He had been sitting thus for half an hour, motionless, with his gaze fastened upon the vague line of the river mist. It was not that the river offered anything new or unfamiliar to his view. He had known it in all its moods, from the swollen fury of spring, when it fretted and tore along its banks, to the low, shrunken sulkiness of late summer, when it shrank sullenly from the strip of sandy shore. But this morning there was in his gaze a stubborn intentness, a sort of inward reckoning, that had little to do with what his eyes encountered.

A faint fragrance of coffee floated from the rear of the house, mingling with the sharp, green odor of crushed cane and the heavier scent of damp earth. Somewhere in the kitchen an iron pot clattered against another, and a woman’s voice—Mélie’s voice—lifted itself in a fragment of song that broke off suddenly, as though checked by a thought or a look no one but herself could feel. David heard these familiar sounds as a man hears the ticking of a clock to which he has grown accustomed; they reached him without disturbing the course of his musing.

The long, rambling house behind him bore the impress of days when timber had been plentiful and men’s ideas of space extravagant. Wide verandas encircled it, their decayed floor-boards yielding with a soft complaint beneath an unwary step. The shutters hung crookedly, some half closed, some flung open like indolent arms. A clump of oleanders, unpruned and riotous, pressed their glossy leaves against the brick piers of the foundation. To a stranger’s eye the place might have suggested neglect; to David it suggested only habit and a sort of mute endurance. The house knew its own sagging deficiencies as he knew his, and held them, as he did, in a stubborn silence.

The boy with the red cap, having turned in at the gate, came up the shell walk with slow, reluctant steps. He carried a folded paper, grimed at the edges where his fingers had gripped it too tightly on the way. When he reached the foot of the steps, he halted, shifting his weight from one bare foot to the other, and stared at David with that frank, unembarrassed curiosity which the Negro youth of the plantation felt for their master when he forgot to clothe himself in the outer garments of command.

“Mo’ning, Mista David,” he ventured at last.

David roused himself as from a remote distance. “Morning, Ben,” he answered, with an effort at his habitual quietness of tone. “What you got there?”

“A letter, suh. Come on de boat las’ night. Cap’n he say to bring it soon’s I wake.”

The boy extended the paper. David took it without haste, though his fingers closed upon it with an unconscious firmness that left their print upon the soiled margin. He turned it once between thumb and forefinger, as if the mere exterior might yield up some foreknowledge of its contents, then broke the seal with a deliberate motion that ill accorded with the sudden quickening of his pulse.

As his eyes moved along the closely written lines, the world about him narrowed and withdrew. The clack of the pump, Mélie’s fragmentary song, the first incisive cry of a jay from the live-oak near the well, dropped away into a thin, meaningless murmur. Only once he lifted his head, and that was when the sun, surmounting at last the low, dark rim of trees, flung a keen shaft of light across the yard, and touched the paper in his hand with a white radiance that seemed, for an instant, almost insolent in its cheerfulness.

author: Mark Twain
title: Sketches New and Old
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Mark Twain's Sketches New and Old. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
I was born with a natural talent for minding other people’s business, and at an early age resolved to make a profession of it, having observed that the most prosperous persons in our village were engaged almost exclusively in that line. The minister attended to the Lord’s business, the constable attended to the law’s, and the grocer attended to the community’s stomach; but each of these, when not too severely occupied with his specialty, gave generous attention to his neighbors’ affairs, and was much the happier and more respected for it. This seemed to me strong evidence that Providence intended the human race to live on a mutual-inspection plan.

Our village—Mudcat Landing, on the Mississippi—was not set down on any map that aspired to accuracy, because it moved too often. In the spring, when the river was feeling active and enthusiastic, it shifted our front street two or three rods inland, and now and then annexed a back yard whole, with the woodpile and a portion of the fence, to the State of Missouri. In the dry months it relented, and gave back some of the real estate it had borrowed, though in a different shape and arranged in what you might call a more original style. Ours was the only town in the Union whose tax-list had to be kept on a slate.

The inhabitants were of a durable, amphibious breed, calculated to prosper equally on land, water, or litigation. The chief industries were drifting, whittling, and improving the truth. Almost every man, woman, and child owned a dog, three debts, and a grievance against the county commissioners, and considered these holdings sufficient capital for a contented life in a republic.

I began my professional career of observation at the age of ten, by taking notes upon the conduct of our leading citizen, Colonel Ephraim Bledsoe. The title was not of a military character, but was conferred on him by general consent, on account of his uniform courage in other people’s dangers. If an accident occurred, the Colonel was always on the spot, with advice. He never risked his person, but he risked it in a confident and outspoken manner, which answered all patriotic purposes at a much smaller expense.

Colonel Bledsoe kept the “Palace Saloon and Family Refreshment Emporium,” a structure of such imposing leanness that the wind blew straight through its architectural opinions and came out the other side disheartened. The building had three doors and two hinges, and when the Colonel shut up at night, it was a matter of sentiment rather than security; any really earnest man could have entered by merely presenting his argument to the back wall.

The Colonel loved progress, especially if somebody else paid for it. He was forever proposing improvements to the Board of Selectmen—of which body he was the loudest, if not the most legal, member. He advocated street lamps for streets which had mislaid themselves every flood; he demanded a public library for a population that could not read its own names without assistance; and he urged the building of a town hall big enough to hold all the voters at once, a measure which, if adopted, would have ruined the local excitement at election time and reduced three promising feuds to bankruptcy.

It was one of these grand enterprises of his that brought about the events which I undertake to set in order here; and if, in relating them, I seem to stray now and then from the strict, poor, meager path of fact into the more flowery regions of probability, I must plead the example of my elders in Mudcat Landing and the natural infirmities of my memory, which was never strong enough to carry a plain, unadorned truth more than a mile without stopping to rest.

author: Herman Melville
title: The Piazza Tales

gpt_opening:
Of a certain upland valley in New England, retired as a hermit's cell from the bustling turnpikes of trade, I purpose here to relate such observations as, in idle hours, I have made there, to my no small contentment of soul, if to no other man's profit. The valley hath no name upon our county maps; surveyors, running their chill, geometrical gaze athwart the ridges, leap it as a boy leaps a gutter. Yet, to my thinking, there is a sort of anonymous dignity in that—like those old sea-captains whose deeds were storms, and whose epitaph is the blank horizon.

I first lighted upon the place in an autumn singularly cool and elongated, as though the year, being loath to commit itself to winter's irrevocable whiteness, would compromise by a long penance of dun and russet. Business, which in these latter days is become the true tyrant of sober men, had thrust me, as with a bayonet, from city desk and ledger, and I, fleeing as any raw recruit from the first crack of musketry, had taken the northern stage, caring little whither, so it were but away.

Towards evening of the second day's dragging, the coach, an antediluvian structure that seemed to have rumbled out of some patriarchal pasture, broke a spring upon a stone, which lay there, no doubt, from the creation, with the malicious purpose of tripping modern invention. Our driver, a man whose leathern physiognomy testified to a long intimacy with dust and profanity, vowed the coach could crawl no farther that night. A small tavern, smoky and low, like a pipe laid upon the earth, invited us with its begrimed sign of a rampant fish, which, to my mind, promised more than the larder could hope to furnish.

My fellow-passengers dispersed after their fashions—one to the bar, one to bed, and one, a silent woman in a brown cloak, to some obscure domestic destiny in the kitchen; but for me, whom the confinement of the coach had made a sort of civilized bear, craving space and coolness, I turned aside into a cart-path that, dim as a memory of youth, strayed off behind the tavern and vanished into the woods.

Now, it has been remarked by certain speculative philosophers that all men, however house-bred, carry about in them some faint aboriginal instinct, like a dull spark under municipal ashes, which, upon the least breath of sylvan air, begins to glow. Be that as it may, I had not gone a hundred rods into the wood when a sense of unaccountable lightness possessed me, as though the invisible harness of clocks and contracts had slipped from my shoulders, leaving me my own man again—a circumstance so novel as to be almost disreputable.

The path, seldom honored by wheel or hoof, was matted with last year's leaves, those frail archivists of forgotten summers. Underfoot they muttered in a dry, papery way, like old men in conclave. The trees, mostly maple and beech, stood in patient council, their trunks taking on, in that indecisive hour before night, a certain spectral sobriety, as of pillars in some Gothic crypt. A bird, too late for his kindred's southern exodus, darted across my path with a twitter that sounded oddly like a reprimand.

So musing, and not yet suspecting that I had deserted the highway of my life, and was already upon the faint beginnings of another road altogether, I came suddenly to the brow of a slope, where the trees broke ranks, and, like soldiers respecting a general, stood aside. Below me, hushed and hollow as a cupped hand, lay the valley.

author: Charlotte Bronte
title: The Wise Mamma Goose
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Charlotte Bronte's The Wise Mamma Goose. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
In a lonely hollow of the moor, where the wind went gossiping all day among the heather, there stood a little gray cottage, not much bigger than a raven’s feather in the wide breast of the waste. The stones of its walls were rough, the thatch upon its roof was raggeder than a beggar’s cloak, and the one crooked chimney that grew out of it like a blackened finger seemed always pointing to the sky, as if it would say, “Look there, not here, for all your grand things.” Yet, for all its meanness, this cottage had a secret, and secrets, my dear little reader, do not measure themselves by the size of the houses they choose to dwell in.

Behind the cottage there was a pond, and round that pond there was a world. You may smile, perhaps, and whisper, “The world is larger than a pond.” It is so, indeed, for those who have stout boots and long roads and many coaches and curious ships. But for some small folk, who wear only feathers and paddle with webbed feet, a pond is sea and river, kingdom and colony, palace and prison, all in one.

On the edge of this water-world, under a tangle of rushes and dock-leaves, there lived a Goose—no common, blundering Goose, such as runs hissing after country boys, but a personage of dignity and understanding, whom I shall beg leave to name Madam Griselda Grayfeather. Her wings were as sober as a Quaker’s cloak; her neck shone with a sort of satiny gravity; and her eye—ah, that eye!—was so shrewd, so searching, so brimful of old-fashioned wisdom, that the ducks, the drakes, the hen with her brood, and even the tall, conceited gander, were half afraid to meet it.

She was called, all over the farm and part of the moor besides, the Wise Mamma Goose.

Now, you must not suppose that Griselda was born wise. No one, neither goose nor child, comes into the world with spectacles on nose and a library under wing. She had once been as yellow and giddy as any gosling that ever tumbled headlong into a puddle and thought it an ocean. She had made foolish speeches, taken foolish pecks, and followed foolish leaders, till mishap and mothering, rain-storm and fox-shadow, had beaten some prudence into her feathers.

Therefore, when she grew plump in years and broad in experience, she made it her solemn business to see that the young people of the pond did not dash their soft, silly heads against quite so many hard stones as she had done. Not that she spared them all knocks—for how then should they learn that stones were stones?—but she tried to cry “Take heed!” before the worst bump came.

It fell out, one bleak autumn evening, when the sky wore a face as gray as Griselda’s own breast, that the creatures of the farmyard crept close together, like thoughts in a troubled mind. The wind was in one of its moody fits, pushing at doors, rattling at windows, marching up the chimney and stamping there like a rude guest. The hens were murmuring tales of foxes; the pigeons were cooing about hawks; the very cat, who commonly pretended to despise the weather, had rolled herself into a furry ball and turned her back on the world.

Only by the pond there was a kind of stir; for on that very day a brood of new goslings had set their first timid webs upon the water. Their down was yet patchy, their legs too slight for their bodies, their minds—alas!—nothing but soft clay, waiting for the first idle foot that should choose to stamp upon it. They chattered; they disputed; they boasted of the narrow circle they had swum from rush to rush, and each related, with admirable importance, the wonders of his own small voyage.

Griselda stood a little apart, with one foot drawn up under her, as philosophers are wont to sit upon their own reflections. She listened, she shook her head, she preened a feather, she looked at the sky. At length, when the chatter had reached such a pitch that even the tadpoles paused to hear what was the matter, the Wise Mamma Goose cleared her throat with a grave “Honk!” that made the startled pond lie still as glass.

“My children,” said she—for she called all foolish things her children—“the world, it seems, has grown wonderfully large since yesterday; and since you are already great travellers and deep thinkers, I must begin, though early, to tell you how easily little voyagers are lost in not very large waters. Draw near, and do not splash; the story I have to relate cannot swim, it must be kept dry.”

author: George Eliot
title: The Mill on the Floss
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of George Eliot's The Mill on the Floss. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
A wide level of pasture, with the slow wind moving shadows along the grass, and the hedgerows cutting the distance into mild green squares, lies about the village of Hetherton, which lifts its grey church-tower and clustering thatch as if in a modest consciousness of being the centre of this homely world. On an autumn afternoon, when the low sun slopes across the meadows and throws a river-like brightness along every dewy furrow, Hetherton looks as if it had never known a sorrow deeper than a bad harvest or a child’s slow fever. Yet the very peace that seems to brood over the roofs, and to rest like a benediction on the brown ricks, is but a thin veil, such as the lightest wind of memory may lift, showing beneath it the hard lines of labour and the tangled threads of human passion.

Those who have never lived in a country parish, where the same faces are seen year after year along the same bit of road, and the same names are repeated Sunday after Sunday at the same pew-doors, can hardly understand how full of incident such a narrow life may become to the imagination that has no wider prospect. In towns we grow accustomed to seeing new countenances thrust upon us at every turn; we lose the habit of connecting a face with a history that our own childish ears have gathered in fragments, like dropped stitches in an old stocking. But in Hetherton every wrinkle is a letter of some volume that has lain long open to village eyes: every crooked gate and gnarled thorn-bush has looked on griefs and misdemeanours that have given it a sort of moral personality in the talk of the old women.

The road that comes over the low hill to the south enters Hetherton with a sober air, as if it had left its last bit of frolicsome dust behind it at the boundary stone, and meant henceforth to take shorter steps, in respect for the decencies of churchyard and market-place. On the left, just where the first cottage garden flings out a crimson dahlia to catch the eye of the passer, a narrow lane turns away between two hawthorn hedges, and winds with rustic secrecy towards the lower meadows and the brook. A casual stranger might pass it unheeding, for there is little to attract the eye beyond a swinging gate and a pollard willow that leans as if it were listening. Yet there are lanes in country life that have more to do with a man’s destiny than the great highways where he fancies himself choosing his own course; and this humble by-way to the brook has more than once been the silent witness of events which the parson would have shaken his head over, and the parish clerk would have mentioned with an air of reluctant knowledge.

If you follow the lane to-day, while the fallen leaves lie sodden under your feet and the air is full of that moist fragrance which belongs to late October, you will come in sight of the red-tiled roof of Brookfield House, showing itself in a shy, side-long manner, as if it were half ashamed of its pretensions to gentility among the thatched dwellings scattered nearer the village. It is a house that has grown, like a family character, by small additions and compromises. A projecting gable with a bow-window, thrust forward upon the old brown front, tells of an ambitious ancestor who had been to market-town and come back with enlarged notions of comfort; while the low, deep porch, over which the jessamine still hangs its thin yellow trumpets, speaks of a time when men were more careful to keep out the east wind than to let in the sunshine.

Here, on such an autumn afternoon many years ago, there was a gathering of sounds and movements which broke in upon the habitual quiet of Brookfield as the first gusts of a storm break in upon the heavy stillness of a sultry day. For there are crises in the history of a household that are felt more keenly in the kitchen than in the parlour, and the feet that go quickest to and fro upon the brick floor are often the first to measure the length and breadth of an approaching sorrow.

author: Wilkie Collins
title: Heart and Science: A Story of the Present Time
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Wilkie Collins's Heart and Science: A Story of the Present Time. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
On a spring evening, some years since, when the smoke of London hung low and thick, like a curtain let down by a weary hand, over streets that had forgotten the colour of the sky, two men took opposite directions at the same turning, and so walked out of each other’s lives. One went eastward, towards those populous quarters where trade chokes the pavement and the human tide flows blackest after business hours. The other went westward, choosing by instinct the broader thoroughfares, the whiter lamps, and the dwindling trees that hinted at squares and gardens beyond. They were of an age to remember their school-days together; they were near enough in height and build to have been mistaken in the dusk for twin shadows; and yet the chance separation of a crossing had parted them more surely than oceans part ships that sail under the same flag and never sight each other from port to port again.

You will be curious to know how this came about. You may even be inclined to ask whether the whim of an idle pen exaggerates the importance of a trivial street incident. It is well that you should distrust the narrator who asks for your attention. Believe nothing, in these pages, on my authority which your own observation of the present time does not support. If it has never happened in your experience that a word missed, a letter delayed, a visit forgotten, has altered the whole course of a human life, then close the book here. It is not written for you.

The man who went eastward walked rapidly, with a certain mechanical briskness of movement which betrayed rather the habit of punctuality than the presence of an urgent purpose. His name was Alban Grey. Professionally, he had the honour—or the misfortune—of calling himself a surgeon. Socially, he occupied one of those ambiguous positions so liberally provided in our improved civilisation, in which a man is too learned for his friends and too poor for his superiors. His father, a clergyman of the old school, had left him a taste for Greek, a distaste for cant, and an income that represented the value of both in ready money. On these modest foundations, our friend had built a character which some found stiff and some found shy, but which possessed at least the negative merit of being unintelligible to the prosperous.

The man who went westward answered to the name of Doctor Louis Bencombe, and to half a dozen titles more, bestowed upon him by grateful institutions and ungrateful newspapers. He was one of those fortunate persons who arrive at notoriety by the shortest road—that is to say, by offending nobody whom it is profitable to conciliate. The devout admired his punctual appearance at certain decorous churches; the scientific cited him in foot-notes; the timid world of fashion consulted him when it was dangerously near having a conviction. He had written a book on Nerves which had tranquillised many consciences, and not a few dividends, by demonstrating, with a wealth of quotation and an economy of meaning, that all moral derangements might be safely attributed to a minute portion of the human frame not visible to the naked eye. It was whispered in professional circles that he had once performed an operation; but this rumour, resting as it did on no printed evidence, obtained little credence.

Alban and Bencombe had met that afternoon in the consulting-room of a charitable hospital devoted to the relief of bodily ills and the encouragement of social ones. They had differed—courteously, of course—on a case which, in other times, would have been referred to a priest, and which, in ours, is submitted to a physician. The object of their debate was a young woman whose mind had given way under the slow torture of a domestic wrong so common that the law has not yet discovered it. Whether her wandering intellect should be soothed by prayer, by drugs, or by the more fashionable opiates of amusement and travel, was the question which had divided the committee, and which now divided the two men.

“It is a matter of nerves,” Bencombe had said, settling his wristband with his usual air of politely arranging the universe. “We must not inquire too curiously into causes which the patient herself is unable to appreciate. Tone the system; divert the attention; strengthen the heart by gentle excitements. I have obtained excellent results with tickets for the opera.”

“It is a matter of conscience,” Alban had answered, with that bluntness which passes for brutality when it is unaccompanied by success. “She has been taught to feel, and forbidden to think. Her mind is not diseased; it is bewildered. Give her truth instead of tonics, and a chance of justice instead of change of air.”

Between nerves and conscience, the majority had decided for the more fashionable complaint. The hospital did not exist to encourage dangerous ideas. It existed, like many other modern institutions, to reconcile the poor to the consequences of the virtues which the rich prescribe to them.

When they parted at the turning, Alban carried away with him the mortification of another lost cause; Bencombe, the mild serenity of a man who adds daily to his store of well-bred disappointments in others. Neither of them knew—as you and I may yet discover—that the girl whose fate they had discussed was destined, by that very controversy, to exert a power over both their lives which no medical report could have predicted, and no moralist have approved.

I

THE FOUND MANUSCRIPT

The document which I herewith give to the world came into my possession in a fashion so strange that I hesitate to relate it, knowing well that the sober-minded will smile, and that those who have outlived faith in all but machines and markets will dismiss it as one more dream. Yet the yellowed pages lie beside me as I write; the ink, faded to a rust-brown, still holds the cramped, urgent hand of the woman who traced it; and the seal of the Historical Commission of the Federated Communities stands upon the enclosing case.

By the technical calendar this is the Year of the Commons 287, though the older chronological reckoning, used in the manuscript itself, would call it 2239 A.D. That so small a span of time should divide the world we know from the world herein recorded will appear incredible to some, and, to those who read carelessly, a sufficient reason to pronounce the whole work apocryphal. But we, who have dug with our own hands in the ash-beds of the Last Cities and sifted the slag of fallen towers for their embedded records, have learned a certain humility before the stubbornness with which fact clings to matter.

The manuscript was found during the third excavation of the Northern Shelf ruins, embedded in what had once been the lower strong-room of an administrative vault. It lay, preserved by an accidental sealing of fused stone and glass, in a metal cylinder of primitive but cunning workmanship. The cylinder itself was corroded, though less than the tools and ornaments about it, and had split along one seam. Through this wound moisture had penetrated and destroyed perhaps a third of the pages. The remainder, however, is legible, if in places obscure where whole sentences have been eaten away.

As to authenticity, I must speak briefly and with the reserve proper to my office. The Commission has compared the internal references of the text with all other surviving records of that era—legal fragments, algorithmic logs, transmission scraps, personal diaries charred at the edges like autumn leaves touched by first frost. Wherever comparison is possible, the alignment is exact in essentials and often startling in detail. Names of known factions, dates of recorded uprisings, the peculiar jargon of security directives, the technical limitations of machinery long since rotted—all are set forth with a fidelity which no later romancer could have contrived.

Yet, for all that, we deal not with a chronicle but with a confession. The writer, who names herself Mara Elgin, was plainly no historian. She wrote as those write who are pressed by danger at their doors and can hear the iron tread of events upon the stair. There is haste in the strokes of the pen, a certain passion that is at times almost incoherent. She interrupts herself to explain, pauses to argue with some imagined doubter, and now and again breaks into bitter laughter which she sets down with a grim parenthetical—“(I laugh, that I may not sob).”

It is not for me to say whether Mara Elgin’s judgments on her contemporaries are sound. That belongs to another department of our labor. I give you her words as she left them, save where decay has compelled the supply of a syllable or two, marked faithfully by brackets. If the tone is partisan, remember that she wrote under a regime which has left upon the earth nothing but scars and slag, and that impartiality is a virtue seldom bred by the sight of one’s comrades flung from drones into the sea.

Let it be remembered, further, that the age of which she writes was the last flowering of that System which, for nearly a thousand years, enthroned Profit above Life and called it freedom. Its ruins are the black mounds we have long since turned into gardens. Its legends are the ghosts that still trouble many an earnest student. What follows is the tale, not of its slow decay—that is written already in every climate-ruined shore and poisoned well—but of its last convulsion and ferocious defense, as seen through the eyes of one insignificant woman who, by the accident of love and thought, stood very close to the heart that beat within the monster.

Here, then, begins the Testament of Mara Elgin, composed, as she herself avers in the opening line of the first surviving page, “on the seventeenth day of the Fourth Quarter, in the eightieth year of the Market Sovereignty, while the sirens still wail in the distance and the city burns beneath a gentle rain of ash.”

gpt_opening:
In the year 1869, at a period when the sciences of the earth and seas seemed to have reached the furthest boundaries assigned to human curiosity, an event occurred which threw the learned world into confusion, disturbed maritime trade on every ocean, and compelled governments themselves to consult their academies. This event—long discussed in saloons, docks, observatories, and ministerial cabinets—may be summed up in a few words: the seas of our globe appeared to have acquired a new inhabitant, as prodigious in its power as it was inexplicable in its nature.

At first, wary minds took it for one of those rumors which are born in taverns and die on the steps of serious institutions. Sailors are imaginative men; the horizon is a great blank page upon which they willingly inscribe marvels. Yet when the “Cumberland,” an English steamer belonging to the Royal Mail, reported that on the 4th of April it had sighted, in the North Atlantic, a moving mass which glowed with a pallid light and displaced the waves as though a reef had suddenly uprooted itself, the story provoked a certain interest. The officers of the ship, men of experience and moderation, signed the report jointly. It was filed, printed in the nautical bulletins, debated a few evenings in the clubs, then turned over to that compartment in men's memories devoted to singular anecdotes.

Two months had not passed when the “Ville-de-Rochefort,” of the French Transatlantic Company, steaming towards the Antilles, signaled by lantern a similar apparition. The captain related that, toward ten o’clock in the evening, under a cloudless sky, a broad luminous stripe rose from the depths and ran parallel to the ship for nearly half a league, keeping pace with her thirteen knots. It seemed to breathe; its glow waxed and waned with a regular rhythm, and the disturbed surface boiled as though stirred by the passage of some titanic body. The passengers, awakened by the commotion, crowded the deck. A dozen witnesses, whose professions did not predispose them to credulity—among them a magistrate, an engineer, and a Lutheran pastor—gave testimony in due form at the consulate of Pointe-à-Pitre. 

This time, denial was less easy. The journals seized upon the affair. Some spoke of a shoal of phosphorescent mollusks acting in concert—though nature knows no such conspiracies. Others invoked the aurora borealis, even though the vessel was far from polar regions and the phenomenon had appeared at the very surface of the sea. A few, less restrained, resurrected ancient legends: leviathans, krakens, and Biblical monsters awoke, summoned from their well-earned slumber to populate the columns of contemporary gazettes.

I confess that I, Professor Armand Delatour, lecturer at the Museum of Natural History in Paris and member-correspondent of several foreign societies, followed these accounts at first with the indifferent smile of the man accustomed to shipboard exaggerations. The oceans, whose fauna I had classified on paper more often than I had observed it in situ, seemed to me incapable of reserving great surprises. Our charts were full, our catalogs substantial, our treatises voluminous. If anything remained to be discovered, it would surely be some shy little crustacean, a worm to delight entomologists, not a colossus to trouble admirals.

Yet it is one of the humbling prerogatives of facts to overthrow even the most solidly built opinions. Before the year was out, I was compelled—not without some secret pleasure which every naturalist will pardon—to renounce my too hasty skepticism. For the mysterious inhabitant of the seas was soon to govern my thoughts, to draw me from my lecture hall, and to hurl me, despite myself, toward one of the most extraordinary voyages ever accomplished by man beneath the waters of the globe.

author: Willa Cather
title: My Ántonia

gpt_opening:
I first saw the country that was to shape my life from the rear platform of a west-bound day coach. I remember that I could not have been more than ten, and that my boots swung clear of the floor when I sat up straight on the worn seat-cushions. It was late afternoon in October, and the sun, already slanting, poured across miles and miles of land that seemed to rise only because the sky bent itself down upon it. I had been dozing, one cheek pressed against the veined window-glass, lulled by the breathing of the train, when a sudden stillness woke me. We had halted at a siding, and for the first time in many hours the world outside was not sliding past in a blur of color and line. It waited, as if for my examination.

The conductor, a broad man with a gray mustache that bristled like wire, came through the car and unlatched the door to the platform. A puff of colder air came in, smelling of iron, coal smoke, and something else—something sharp and thin that I came later to know was the clean odor of open fields. A handful of men got down, stamping their feet and swinging their arms. I followed them as far as the threshold and stood there, catching the rail with both hands.

Beyond the low cinders and the sloping ditch, the land reached out in long, gentle swells, dull gold where the grass had died, purplish in the hollows where shadows were gathering. There were no trees, except a crooked cottonwood far off, that leaned as if it had started for the horizon and wearied of the effort. Fences ran here and there, but they did not divide the country so much as emphasize its unbrokenness. The sky was so wide that it seemed to begin at my very shoulders; I had the impression that if I were to raise my eyes too quickly, I might fall into it.

Until that journey, my world had been made up of the narrow, brick-paved streets of a small river town, bound in by bluffs and sheltered under heavy foliage. There, the sun was an intruder that slipped in only at certain hours, angling through the maples and elms. One saw always bounded things: the end of a street where the hill rose, the turn of the river, the opposite bank. That afternoon, balanced between the warm, used air of the car and the thin wind outside, I felt for the first time the uneasy delight of something that had no edges.

A woman in black, who had been sitting opposite my grandmother, rose and joined me. She was a dark person with a widow’s net falling over her brow, and under it her eyes looked both tired and amused. She rested her gloved hand on my shoulder, light as a bird’s claw.

“So you are seeing it now,” she said. “It will not look like this to you again.”

I asked her why, though I was half-afraid of the answer, as children are afraid of all prophecies that seem to single them out.

“Because you are new to it,” she replied. “Things are only born once for each of us. After that, they are only remembered.”

The conductor called “All aboard!” and the men hurried up the steps, shaking the dust from their trouser legs. I hesitated, then backed away from the open space and slipped once more into the smell of plush and oil and human clothes. When I took my seat, the land outside had already begun to move again, folding and unfolding upon itself with the slow, solemn motion of waves on a windless day.

I did not know then that I should ever come to live on that land, or that it would enter into me so completely that years later, in a crowded street of a far city, the sight of a burnt field or a strip of sky drawn low and pale beyond the roofs would bring it back with a pang that was half longing and half gratitude. Yet even that first evening, watching the flat country darken and give itself up to the stars, I felt something in me turn toward it, as a plant turns toward light it cannot see but somehow expects.

author: P. G. Wodehouse
title: Love Among the Chickens

gpt_opening:
I am not, as a rule, a man who consults the stars. Experience has taught me that, whatever celestial influences may be at work, the immediate cause of one’s troubles is usually a fellow human being with a bright idea. The horoscope may say, “Avoid new ventures,” but it is generally a friend who says, “I’ve got it! We’ll be rich in six months!” and that, I have found, is where the real danger lies.

Had I realized this more clearly on that particular Thursday afternoon, I might have let the telephone ring itself into a ripe old age. As it was, I picked up the receiver, said “Hallo?” with my customary bonhomie, and thereby altered the whole course of my existence.

“Jasper, you old prehistoric relic!” yelled a voice that might have stripped bark from trees at thirty yards. “Why aren’t you dead?”

There is, I suppose, a convivial school of thought that regards this sort of opening as spirited badinage. I belong to the quieter, more retiring persuasion which likes to begin a conversation without being given instantly to understand that one is an oversight of Providence. However, the man at the other end of the wire was Hugo Penbury, and Hugo had been talking to me like that ever since the term we first met at school and he introduced himself by throwing my hat out of a third-floor window.

“Hugo,” I said, “I cling feebly to life in the hope that one day you will say something pleasant. It’s a long shot, but I’ve always admired optimism.”

“Rubbish,” said Hugo. “You’re preserved by a kind Providence for the carrying out of my schemes.”

“These wouldn’t happen to involve personal danger, social disgrace, or financial ruin?” I asked. “Only I’ve promised my aunt to cut down.”

“Nothing of the sort,” said Hugo. “Well, hardly anything. Listen. How do you feel about the country?”

“Deeply suspicious,” I said. “It has cows in it.”

“Nonsense. Cows are just armchairs that have mislaid the drawing room. I'm talking about God’s own acreage. Meadows! Orchards! A little river, burbling. You ought to hear this river burble, Jasper. It’s like an infant gargling champagne.”

“Are we nearing the point,” I said, “or merely circling it with a view to landing later?”

“The point,” said Hugo, “is that I have taken a house in the country.”

The world slipped sideways. “You,” I said, “have taken a house in the country.”

“Exactly. A little place. Modest. Twenty acres, give or take. Bit of garden. Couple of paddocks. Orchard. Small lake. Or possibly large pond. One must see it in a poetic spirit. And there is,” he concluded triumphantly, “a barn.”

“That,” I said, “completes the picture. I can see you now, framed in rustic simplicity, milking the hay and mowing the cows.”

“Don’t be a chump,” said Hugo. “I am not proposing to commune with nature for my soul’s uplift. I am going in for—” He dropped his voice to a rich, throaty stage whisper. “—ducks.”

“Ducks,” I repeated.

“Ducks,” said Hugo. “The white kind. Very superior birds. Lay eggs of a quality that will make the metropolis grovel. Jasper, old lad, we stand upon the threshold of an empire founded on omelettes. I shall be Napoleon, and you shall be… whoever it was who did the work for Napoleon.”

“Talleyrand?”

“That’s the bird. Pack a bag. I’m sending you a ticket. You arrive tomorrow.”

“I arrive—what?” I said.

But he had already rung off, leaving me with the receiver at my ear, my mouth open, and a gathering conviction that Aunt Agatha’s horoscope-writer was a good deal closer to the truth than the man who had invented Hugo Penbury.

gpt_opening:
Mr. Utterson—for so I shall name him, though the reader may know him by another style—was a man of spare habit and sober inclinations, whose chief luxury consisted in the orderly conduct of his days. He took his walks, as he took his meals and his clients, with a grave regularity that had grown about him like a kind of armour. His face, rugged rather than handsome, was marked by lines as straight and perpendicular as the ledgers in his office; and though his countenance had more of winter than of spring in its aspect, there was in his eye a look of quiet endurance which disposed men insensibly to confidence.

It was his custom, upon Sundays, when the labours of the week had been folded and laid away like worn garments, to traverse a portion of the city to which business seldom called him. The streets thereabout had preserved something of the older London, being narrow and ill-lit, with houses that leaned together in a sort of confidential decrepitude; but Utterson, who was no lover of novelty, found in these dingy ways a companionship beyond that of the new squares and too brilliant shop-fronts which had lately crept westward like a tide of glass.

On one such afternoon, in the melancholy fall of the year, as a raw mist hung over the river and the lamps went up early, Mr. Utterson passed along a by-street which he had not trodden for some considerable time. The paving-stones, being ill-laid and glossy with damp, betrayed under his careful step a kind of stealthy treachery; and the houses, which were for the most part let out in rooms to clerks and small tradespeople, showed a pattern of poverty and decency so monotonous that one shabby doorway might have been brother to another. Yet, in the midst of this dreary uniformity, there occurred a circumstance so strange to his eye that he stopped, as a man might stop who finds a word in a familiar page turned suddenly to cipher.

The house that arrested him stood at a corner where the street, like a tired thought, broke off abruptly and lost itself in a court. It had once been a commodious dwelling, perhaps the pride of some citizen in periwig and sword; but its glory had long since departed. The shutters of the lower windows were fastened, the paint had peeled away in melancholy scales, and above the door, whose brass fixtures showed only a greenish memory of polish, there remained the ghost of a coat of arms, as if respectability itself had been scraped out.

What rendered the place remarkable, however, was not so much this dilapidated dignity, which Utterson had seen matched in many decayed streets, as a certain air of watchful exclusion that seemed to emanate from its very stones. The neighbouring houses, though poor, gave signs of human occupancy; here a curtain twitched, there a kettle steamed at a half-closed casement; but the corner house preserved round itself a silence that was almost an attitude. No sound issued from within; no footmark smirched the grime upon the threshold; and yet the knocker on the door, which was of an unusual shape and size, had a brightness that told of recent service, being rubbed and burnished as if some jealous hand had but that morning caressed its metal.

Mr. Utterson, who had a lawyer’s fondness for anything that hinted at concealment, stood a little while contemplating this anomalous dwelling. He recalled vaguely to mind that he had heard its situation named in connexion with a case which had never come fully to his ears; but memory, like the mist about him, refused to take a settled shape. As he lingered thus, half amused by his own curiosity and half oppressed by a sensation for which he could find no respectable name, a figure advanced slowly up the street, and with its approach the lawyer’s idle observation hardened of a sudden into interest.

gpt_opening:
The ship had lain three days in the dry, cold glitter of the winter roads, with her tall spars pencilled against a sky of iron and a wind that went about the docks like a watchful magistrate, prying into every corner, nosing among coiled hawsers and under the tarpaulins of slumbering lighters. She had come from the north, from those coasts where the sea gnaws at the land with patient teeth, and was outward bound again; and already, about her great black hull and under her bulging bows, the uneasiness of departure had gathered like an invisible tide.

Beyond the sheds and cranes and piles of coal, the town rose, begrimed, innumerable, with its million windows reflecting here and there the pale smile of a reluctant sun. But near the water all things were of the sea and for the sea. The very cobblestones were polished by the shuffling of sea-boots; the tavern doors stood open like warm mouths, exhaling a strong breath of liquor, smoke, and damp clothing; and in the gutters guttered the refuse of ships—strands of oakum, splinters of crate-wood, orange-peel brought from a brighter world.

Between the tram-lines and the quay-edge the men of the forecastles of many oceans drifted and mingled, as though some strange current, setting along the warehouses, had washed them together. Dark men, with tattooed arms and golden rings in their ears, trudged sullenly beside broad-shouldered, red-necked giants with the slow blue eyes of the north; cabin-boys, small, shrill, important, darted in and out among the heavy legs; and now and then, emerging from the shabby gloom of a waterside lodging-house, there came a figure with a seaman’s swing in the shoulders and a landman’s hesitation in the feet, new to the calling or returning to it after a season of idleness ashore.

They were coming to our ship, to that long black hull moored by the grain elevator, where a white name, already blurred by many seas, glimmered faintly under the bending masts. The “Resolute” she was called—a sober, sturdy name, smelling of duty rather than adventure, like a word spoken low in the dark behind set teeth. She lay a little aloof from the noisy crowding of coasters and ferries, as if conscious of her deeper errands; and the ripple that tapped against her sides was a more distant, more deliberate voice than the chattering wash of the harbour craft.

Forward, in the forecastle that still stood empty and resonant, the air was heavy with the memory of other crews. From the beams hung bunches of drying clothes, forgotten by men whose names had long since been spat out from the ship’s log into oblivion. An old sea-chest, lid broken and lock rusted, lodged in a corner like a stranded thing. In its ill-sorted depths lay a knitted muffler, a cheap concertina, a Bible with swollen pages and a foreign printer’s name. Dust of years, shaken loose from the planks by a hundred tramplings, rested upon these relics, and sifted slowly, imperceptibly, into the interstices of charred tobacco and decayed rope-yarn.

Into that den—the lair and home of the men who work under the open sky—were to come presently the voices and faces of a new company, bearing with them their oddities, their vices, their silent fidelities, and the secret sorrows that cling to them like the smell of their damp clothes. The sea conscripts from all shores with impartial hand, and the forecastle is her roll-call.

It was on the morning of the fourth day, when the frost had drawn white flowers upon the tarred planks and the smoke from the galley rose straight as a mast, that the first of them came tramping up the gangway; and with his arrival the idling ship, so long inert among her hawsers, felt, as it were, the first heave of the voyage stir obscurely within her ribs.

author: Louisa May Alcott
title: Jack and Jill

gpt_opening:
Snow had fallen all the night before, and when the pale winter sun looked over the hills, it found village and valley buried up to the window-sills, and every tree dancing under its white burden, as if glad to be so finely dressed. Chimneys smoked, bells rang, and sleigh-bells tinkled as the good people bestirred themselves earlier than usual; for a snow-storm, though it does sadly interfere with errands and imposes much shovelling and sweeping, is still a sort of holiday in a quiet country town, and sets the young folks’ hearts to beating faster, no matter how old they may pretend to be.

In one small brown house, standing a little apart from its neighbors, there was a great bustle, because the children had waked with the first streak of daylight, and would not be persuaded to lie still, though it wanted a full hour to the time when the school-bell commonly rang.

“Do stop hopping, Ned; you shake the whole bed, and I can’t find my other stocking,” cried Dora, a slim, lively maiden of twelve, whose flaxen braids were all in a tangle about her shoulders, and whose blue eyes shone with a mixture of impatience and delight.

“I can’t help it. Just look out, Dora! It’s as deep as the fences. Oh, won’t there be splendid coasting!” answered Ned, a rosy boy of ten, who, being already half dressed, could allow himself the luxury of dancing up and down like a young bear just released from its den.

Dora left the stocking to its fate, and ran, slipperless and shivering, to press her eager face against the cold window-pane. A long-drawn “Oh!” of rapture escaped her lips; for the world outside looked to her like fairy-land. The bare lilac-bushes were trees of silver; the gate-posts wore caps more comical than any she had ever trimmed for the village fair; the very clothes-line made a graceful garland from apple-tree to shed, every sag and loop of it edged with glittering frost.

“How beautiful!” she whispered, forgetting her impatience in her honest admiration.

“I told you so. There’s the hill, Dora! Just see the track all drifted full. We can make a new one; and I mean to be the first boy down, if Tom Blake doesn’t get ahead of me,” said Ned, crowding beside her, till the cold glass took the print of two small noses instead of one.

“You’ll have to clear the path to the gate before you think of the hill,” called a brisk voice from the next room; and presently Mrs. Dean appeared, with her sleeves rolled up and a shawl pinned over her neat morning dress, for she had been at work since daybreak, as mothers often are, while lazy children lie dreaming of the pleasures they mean to have.

“There, now! You’ve no business to be barefoot on this floor, Dora. And, Ned, you’ll take off that clean shirt if you keep romping so,” she added, with a look that was half reproof and half pride; for, like the snow without, the frolic and freshness within were pleasant to see, though they did make some trouble.

“We were only looking at the drifts, Mother. They’re as high as the wall.”

“So much the more shovelling for us. Come, dress quickly; Father has begun on the path already, and will want help. The sooner the work is done, the sooner you can try your hill,” said Mrs. Dean, tying Dora’s braid with a practised hand, and giving Ned’s rumpled hair a smoothing pat, which he dodged with a laugh, for he considered combs and brushes his natural enemies.

If either child felt inclined to grumble at exchanging castles in the air for paths in the snow, the discontent did not last; for in that house it was well understood that play must wait till duty had been attended to. So, with many a glance from the scanty breakfast toward the tempting white world, the little pair made haste, and in a surprisingly short time might have been seen, like two industrious ants, helping their father clear a way from door to gate, very unconscious that this busy winter morning was to be the beginning of such changes and chances as neither they nor the reader could possibly imagine.

author: Wilkie Collins
title: The Haunted Hotel: A Mystery of Modern Venice
