gpt_opening:
In the autumn of the year 18—, when the summer visitors had fled from Venice as if the first breath of October had been the pestilence itself, a small English steamer came gliding, like a guilty creature, into the still and yellow waters of the lagoon.

The hour was near sunset. A dull red light—such a light as might have suited a city built for crimes and burials rather than for music and love—hung low in the heavy sky, and turned to tarnished copper the golden angels on the churches and the brazen horses of Saint Mark. Along the Riva degli Schiavoni a few idle gondoliers, muffled in their cloaks, watched the steamer with an indolent curiosity which did not prevent them from spitting meditatively into the canal. They had seen too many strangers come and go to take much interest in a single additional soul destined to lose its way in the crooked streets, to pay too much for bad beads, and to mistake indigestion for romance.

One man only on the quay looked with attention as the vessel slowly swung round, and the gangway was thrown across. He was tall and lean, dressed in black with an attempt at clerical propriety, and he wore his shabby respectability with the air of a man conscious that it was a disguise and determined that it should be respected. His long, colourless face—thin, like a page scarce written on—might have been passed over by the most observant eye, but for the strangely watchful expression of his light grey eyes. They rested—not on the passengers in general—but, with singular persistency, on one woman who stood a little back from the rail, with her veil down and her hands clasped on the handle of a small travelling-bag.

When the plank was fixed, the usual rush of the impatient broke out. Englishmen, who had grumbled at their own apathy all the way from Trieste, pushed forward with the fury of a newly-discovered energy; German philosophers, whose systems provided for every other contingency of human existence, forgot themselves and their systems alike in the struggle for precedence; an American, with his hat on one side and his mouth full of tobacco, trod on a Frenchman’s toes in a burst of republican brotherhood.

The veiled lady did not at first move. She waited with that unaccountable patience which belongs sometimes to very gentle natures, and sometimes to very desperate ones. When, at last, the way was clearer, she passed quietly along the plank. As she set her foot upon the stones of Venice, the man in black moved forward a step, then checked himself, and fell back again into the shadow of a column.

If any person present had taken the trouble to watch him, that person would have seen the thin lips twitch with something like a smile—rather of anticipation than of pleasure. The woman paused, looking about her with the irresolute air of a stranger who expects to be met. Nobody approached her. The porter of the hotel to which most of the English went that season shouldered his pile of luggage and marched off, unchallenged, at the head of his procession. The veiled lady stood alone.

The man in black stepped out once more, this time decisively.

“Madam,” he said, with a foreign accent which did not prevent his English from being remarkably pure, “you are looking, perhaps, for the messenger of your hotel. May I be permitted to offer my services instead?”

He lifted his hat. The lady raised her veil. Her face, pale and unmistakably beautiful, wore that look of fixed attention which betrays less surprise than fear suddenly controlled.

“You know me, then?” she asked.

He bent low, as if the avowal he was about to make were a homage.

“Madam,” he answered, “I have been waiting for you all day.”

On a gray morning of the year 1649, at that doubtful hour when the light does not yet know whether it should declare itself day or return modestly into the arms of night, a solitary rider followed, at an amble, the muddy road that led from Saint-Denis to Paris.

The man, enveloped in a cloak of dark brown cloth, rode a small, vigorous horse, of that indefatigable Norman breed which seems made less for shining in a tournament than for enduring a campaign. The animal, spattered with mire up to the withers, held his head low, less from fatigue, perhaps, than from that patient resignation in which experience soon instructs both men and beasts destined to travel long roads for small profit.

The rider went with bowed head, not, as might have been believed, to protect his face from the drizzle, for the hood of his cloak was pushed back, but with that pensive inclination of the neck which proclaims a soul occupied elsewhere than on the path it follows. One saw, framing a pale and somewhat thin face, black hair streaked prematurely with threads of silver; the mouth was firm, the chin obstinate, the nose aquiline, and, under eyebrows thick as a hedge, two eyes whose calm was more menacing than another's anger.

From time to time he raised those eyes towards Paris, which drew itself in a confused mass on the horizon, and each time that confused mass, half fog, half smoke, seemed to grow larger and to stir like a living thing.

"Paris is awake early," murmured the traveller. "Or rather Paris has not slept."

He pressed his knees lightly to his horse's flanks; the generous animal quickened his pace.

Those who have not seen Paris in those troubled years of the Fronde can scarcely imagine what sort of monstrous city it was: a vast inn where every party took its meal by the light of its own torches, a great chessboard on which the king, the queen, the princes and the people moved simultaneously, and often, it must be said, without knowing very clearly who was checking whom.

Our rider, whose cloak, though threadbare, had once been of rich cloth, and whose boots, in spite of the mud, betrayed a foot accustomed to the stirrup, did not have the air of a man come to seek fortune there. He resembled rather those old soldiers who, after having long borne the blows of fate, return to the capital to ask from it not gold, but a corner of pavement on which to die without being jostled.

Yet, as he approached the city, a singular change took place on his impassive face: the more the towers, roofs and steeples detached themselves from the mist, the more that countenance, at first gloomy, took on something like animation. One would have said that, under the ashes of the past, a spark still smouldered, and that this spark, feeling approaching breath, made an effort to revive.

The man was about to enter by the gate of Saint-Denis. There, beneath the arch blackened by the smoke of a hundred years, two soldiers of the city watch extended their halberds.

"Halt!" cried one of them. "Who goes there?"

"A Frenchman," replied the traveller simply.

"That is not enough," said the other. "In these times we must know not only of what country a man is, but to which party he belongs. Are you for the king, for the Parliament, or for the princes?"

The rider smiled, and this smile, which hardly softened his features, revealed a habit of irony as much as of resignation.

"My friend," said he, "I have had, in my life, the honor of being for men more considerable than all those you have just named; you may therefore let me pass, without fear that I shall compromise your gate by entering it."

The guards looked at one another. There was, in the tone with which these words were pronounced, such calm assurance that they hesitated to take offence at them.

"And what is your name?" resumed the first soldier, who was not without curiosity.

"My name?" repeated the traveller, as if that question, the simplest of all, were the most difficult to answer. "My name is of so little consequence today that it would be charity to let me forget it. Content yourself, my comrade, with knowing how I was once called. Formerly, when there were still kings in France and gentlemen in the provinces, I was called—"

He paused a second, as though listening to an echo which spoke only to him.

"I was called Monsieur d’Artagnan."

author: Edith Wharton
title: The Decoration of Houses
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Edith Wharton's The Decoration of Houses. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
In considering the present condition of domestic architecture, one is met at the outset by the curious fact that, while never before have so many people been in a position to build or alter houses, never has the resulting confusion of taste been more apparent. The multiplication of what are called “styles,” the continual shifting of fashion from one historical period to another, and the eager adoption of every novelty that commerce can exploit, have conspired to make the modern house less an expression of ordered intention than a repository of ill-assorted objects. That a dwelling should first of all be convenient and wholesome no one will deny; but convenience and wholesomeness, though the indispensable foundation of good building, do not of themselves produce it. Something more—something of proportion, selection, and restraint—is needed before a house becomes, in the finer sense, habitable.

It is perhaps natural that a society trained to regard the house as a background for social display rather than as the frame of daily existence should concern itself chiefly with such parts of it as are most open to public scrutiny. Hence the preoccupation with stair-halls and drawing-rooms, and the comparative neglect of those quieter spaces in which the real business of living is carried on. Yet the impression of a house—the sense it conveys of repose, dignity, or triviality—is more surely determined by the treatment of its modest rooms than by the splendour of its state apartments. No elaboration of carved panel or painted ceiling will compensate for a lack of order in the distribution, proportion, and fitting-up of the simpler living-rooms. It is in these that the character of the house finds its truest expression; and since character, in architecture as in people, is rarely the result of accident, it must be sought by deliberate means.

To seek it wisely presupposes some acquaintance with the principles that underlie all good building. By “principles” is not meant any rigid formula to be applied without regard to circumstance, but rather certain conclusions slowly reached by experience and confirmed by observation of the best examples of the past. The study of historical models, if pursued without intelligence, ends, indeed, in a sterile archaism; yet it is only by studying them that we escape the formlessness of mere whim. Those who object that the habits of another age cannot profitably guide our own forget that the fundamental needs of human life vary little from one century to another. Men must always eat, sleep, work and converse; they must be sheltered from cold and heat, and protected, so far as four walls can protect them, from intrusion and display. The problem of the house is, and must remain, the ordering of space to answer these needs with the least friction and the greatest grace.

If the following pages make any claim, it is not to offer an exhaustive treatise on architecture, but to indicate, for those about to build or rearrange a house, certain considerations which may help them to distinguish between the essentials of good planning and those adventitious ornaments with which trade is ever ready to overlay them. For only when the temptation to accept the ready-made is resisted, and when a clear conception of purpose precedes the choice of form, can the dwelling cease to be a warehouse of objects and become, instead, the visible expression of an inward order.

author: Herman Melville
title: White Jacket; Or, The World on a Man-of-War
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Herman Melville's White Jacket; Or, The World on a Man-of-War. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
Call me Gannet. It is a sea-bird’s name, and I have a sea-bird’s fortune, to be forever hovering over waters that never become my own. If, in the course of these pages, I swoop somewhat low, and dip my pinions into brine that has washed more illustrious hulls than ever bore me, impute it not altogether to presumption, but to that irresistible gravitation by which the ocean draws not only ships, but thoughts, and compels all meditative minds to sail upon its surface, whether or no they be provided with a proper commission.

The time of which I begin to speak was when I, a youngster not long weaned from the maternal shore, first found myself regularly enrolled upon the books of a ship-of-war: a mighty, many-mouthed monster of oak and iron, that ate the King’s stores with all the appetite of a Leviathan, and roared out his displeasure in thirty-two pound curses. To have lived merely upon land, and never to have trod a quarter-deck under the pennant of a man-of-war, is to have missed one of the chief commentaries upon that harsh text of human life—Discipline.

It was at dawn, in the chill gray, when the river and the sky seemed conjoined in conspiracy against all warm-blooded creatures, that I first climbed the side of His Majesty’s frigate Athalia. She lay at moorings like a guilty thought arrested between purpose and execution; silent, yet knit for action from spritsail to mizzen-peak. The long tiers of cannon-gaping ports were closed, as if she hugged her wrath under hatches; the rigging traced an infinite cobweb against the paling stars; and the pennant, a strip of colorless shadow, drooped damply from the main.

A boat had put me from the shore, manned by blue-jacketed fellows who pulled as though the river were some stubborn duty to be flogged into submission. I sat in the stern-sheets, my small sea-chest between my knees, my civility between fear and pride, and my heart divided among a hundred contradictory sensations. To part from land is always to be, for a moment, an orphan to the earth; and when the land is your native country, and the sea before you is not your own choosing, but your monarch’s, the orphanage is doubly felt.

“Hullo there! Give way, my hearties,” cried the coxswain, a scar-scribbled veteran whose face bore as many wrinkles as the sea has caprices. “The new chicken’s for the coop. The first luff’ll want him afore breakfast, I’ll warrant.”

There is nothing so prompt to hatch contempt in a man as to hear himself spoken of as poultry. It plucked my dignity to the last feather. Yet I held my peace; for though words are free as wind ashore, at the very smell of a man-of-war they grow dear commodities, to be coined out only when authority holds the mint.

We came under the frigate’s towering flank, black and silent as a premeditated secret. A side-ladder dangled like a doubtful invitation. The marine sentry, outlined above by a faint sky beginning to redden, stood motionless, his musket an exclamation of steel at the end of a mute sentence. As I put my hand upon the lowest rattling, it seemed I seized not merely a rope, but a new covenant with hardship. For the sea, when it takes a man under the Articles of War, baptizes him over again into an order where obedience is the mother, and individuality the sacrificed child.

So, with my chest hauled up before me like a small domestic past, I followed where so many bare-footed destinies had preceded, and set the first of my land-born footsteps upon a deck where I was to learn how small a unit a man may be made in the arithmetic of a man-of-war.

author: Thomas Hardy
title: Two on a Tower
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Thomas Hardy's Two on a Tower. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
On the eastern verge of a certain upland in Wessex, where the last fields fall away in a rash of furze and brier before they surrender to heath and sky, there stood, some years since, a solitary tower. It rose from the back of a hill that was neither sufficiently lofty to be called a down nor sufficiently humble to escape remark altogether, and might, by a stranger driving past in the valley road on a dim day, have been mistaken for a windmill robbed of its arms, or for the stump of some ecclesiastical ambition lopped short by time. To those, however, who had dwelt under its shadow from childhood—if shadow it can be called when the thing rather shared than stole the light—it was simply “the Tower,” as if no other had ever been reared in Christendom.

It was not a stronghold, though it feigned the plain solidity of one; nor a campanile, though the village church, modest and heavy, crouched at a decent distance below, much as a clerk might crouch below his master’s desk. It had in truth been built for a laudable but now forgotten purpose connected with the observation of the weather and the noting of terrestrial magnetisms, in the days when learned men thought to make a map of every breeze and a calendar of each caprice of the needle. The gentlemen who had raised the funds and signed their names to the brass plate at the base were long buried, the brass itself half worn into dumbness by rain and the fingerings of idle children; the instruments once crowning the summit had been dismantled or carried away in vans that arrived and departed mysteriously one autumn morning; and the fabric was left to stand, as many such fabrics stand in rural England, a monument less to human knowledge than to the transience of particular enthusiasms.

Around it the land rolled and folded in a manner that betrayed some ancient convulsion smoothed over by centuries of weather and plough. The eye, mounting from hedge to ridge, from ridge to further ridge, met always with some barrier which promised a view beyond, and, when gained, only revealed another line to be surmounted. Yet if one took the trouble of climbing to the Tower itself, and, having done so, of climbing again the spiral that wound within its thickness to the platform on the top, a prospect awaited such perseverance that might almost reconcile a man to his own insignificance. Far away to the west, where the hill-country lost patience at last and fell despairingly into a dim grey plain, the smoke of a distant town made a faint blur upon the horizon, like a thumb-stain upon a clean page. Northward the chalk downs gleamed and retired, their white scars showing pale through the turf wherever sheep had kept the green ragged and close. South and east the heath stretched, tinted here with lingering purples, there with the dun of exhausted growths, till it merged imperceptibly in that unfeatured remoteness which the minds of rustics inhabit as naturally as their bodies inhabit the soil.

From this eminence the village of Lanton could be discerned lying along the flank of the hill in a manner rather horizontal than perpendicular, as if fearful of committing itself decidedly either to elevation or to the vale. Its thatches were at this hour exhaling the day’s hoarded warmth in little wavering exhalations, the chimneys pointing very primly upward, having as yet scarcely begun their evening talk of smoke. A lane, narrow as scruple and twice as tortuous, came climbing from the lower road, slipped by the church like a whispered apology, brushed the outermost cottages with its dusty skirts, and then, as if ashamed of its presumption in aspiring so high, turned sidelong and descended again, leaving the Tower isolated upon its knoll, unvisited save by such feet as had some particular errand there, or such as were driven, by impulses unnamed and perhaps not wholly conscious, to seek altitudes when the plain of life grew too confined.

It was upon a late afternoon in September, when the year, like a woman of thirty, still carries bloom without the confidence of youth, that a figure might have been seen ascending this lane with an intention that, though not easy to define at the moment, will become apparent as we proceed.

author: Charles Dickens
title: The Pickwick Papers
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Charles Dickens's The Pickwick Papers. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
That eminent body of men, whose collective wisdom has so long illumined the dim by-ways of our national existence, and whose proceedings, though frequently misunderstood by the vulgar, have ever been directed to the loftiest and most philanthropic ends, was assembled, one moist November evening, in the upper room of the Blue Lion Inn, in the town of Mudbury, for the purpose of debating a question of transcendent interest to mankind in general, and to the members in particular. 

The society—for such indeed it was, although the modesty of its founders had imposed upon it the unpretending title of "The Amenity Improvement Association"—had been called into especial council by its president; and when it is added that the president was no other than Mr. Bartholomew Pottle, it will be immediately perceived by the enlightened reader that the affair was of magnitude and moment, and could by no possibility relate to anything so trivial as the mere payment of arrears, the settling of a tavern bill, or the discharge of a waiter who had dropped hot gravy upon the person of the treasurer. 

Far be it from these pages to liken Mr. Bartholomew Pottle—whose virtues will, in the fullness of time, shine with the mellow lustre of a well-rubbed brass doorknob—to any of those noisy and self-sufficient individuals who, in this too literal age, mistake vehemence for genius, and imprudence for philanthropy. Mr. Pottle was a man of thought. He was one of those great silent spirits who meditate profoundly at the corners of streets, who look through the small panes of coffee-room windows with an air of pensive investigation, and who have an impressive habit of shaking their heads slowly at nothing at all. When we add that his baldness was of that polished and extensive description which bespeaks either the habitual use of a hat several sizes too small, or the constant friction of an enlarged intelligence upon the inner surface of the cranium, we but imperfectly shadow forth the splendour of his externally unadorned, but inwardly luxuriant mind.

On the evening to which our narrative first—though most respectfully—introduces itself, a thick fog, of that particularly adhesive and insinuating quality which appears to have been expressly manufactured for the depression of the human spirit and the encouragement of colds, lay heavily upon the town of Mudbury. It entered the nose of the apothecary, and made him sneeze among his potions; it blunted the edge of the razor in the barber’s shop, where a melancholy clerk sat resignedly under the hands of persecution; it stole into the passage of the Blue Lion Inn, and took such liberties with the coats and hats hanging thereby as only the most intimate fogs ever presume to take.

Within the large room upstairs, however, the atmosphere, though by no means free from the external invasion, was cheerfully disputed by a blazing fire, several tallow candles of a meditative turn, and the vapours of sundry hot mixtures, which steamed and fumed upon the table in a manner highly gratifying to the senses and morals of the assembled members.

Around that hospitable board, on wooden chairs of unbending virtue, sat the flower of Mudburian philosophy. There was Mr. Pottle himself, in a bottle-green coat of ancient cut, which looked as if it had been made at a period when tailors, despairing of the human form, had resolved to consult only their own imaginations. There was Mr. Tobias Crumple, the secretary, whose thin hair strove in weak detachments to maintain a position upon a head that had evidently abandoned all hope of concealment. There, too, was Mr. Jonas Pigg, the treasurer—a gentleman of such rotund respectability, that he seemed expressly created by Providence to represent a substantial balance in hand.

It was upon the narrow but determined shoulders of these, and other scarcely less distinguished individuals, that the resolute task of improving the amenities of mankind had fallen; and as Mr. Pottle slowly rose to address the Association, placing his right hand beneath the lowest button of his waistcoat, and his left upon the lid of the steaming punch-bowl (to ensure, as he afterwards observed, the strictest impartiality in his sentiments), the whole company felt that a crisis, alike in their own history and that of the civilised world, was about to be nobly encountered.

author: H. Rider Haggard
title: Allan and the Holy Flower
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of H. Rider Haggard's Allan and the Holy Flower. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
It was on a certain mist-laden evening, now thirty years and more agone, that I sat alone in my room in the little house which I then occupied at Durban, turning over some yellowing memoranda of past journeys, and wondering, not without a touch of melancholy, how such a one as I could have lived to see so much and yet understand so little of the strange game which we call life. Outside, the sea was grumbling upon the bar, as it always seems to do when the wind sets in from the eastward, and the masts of the ships in the roadstead groaned and clanked together like men in fetters. A thin rain drifted slantwise across the window panes, and the lamp upon my writing-table made a faint halo in the tobacco smoke that hung beneath the ceiling.

I had been sorting my notes with a view to burning a portion of them. A man accumulates many useless things when he has been knocking about the world as long as I have—Allan Quatermain is my name, though there is small reason why it should be writ down—and if he does not from time to time make a clearance, he finds that, instead of possessing his papers, they possess him. So I was tying up this bundle and casting that one into the basket, when there slipped from between two dog-eared notebooks a folded letter, stained with damp and bearing upon the outer side, in a very fine and foreign hand, these words:

“To Mr. Allan Quatermain, sometime hunter of elephants, whose heart is as brave as his aim is true; to be delivered when he shall have grown weary of small adventures and be ready for a great one.”

I remember distinctly that I laughed aloud when I read this piece of extravagance. I had no recollection of the letter at all, nor of having received it, which did not surprise me, for I have been a careless man with papers, and at times weeks will pass in the veld with no opportunity to read a letter, much less to answer it. Yet the hand was not wholly strange to me; it stirred some memory that lay half-buried, like a wagon wheel in the dust of a long-deserted track.

I opened it and read. The ink had faded, but the words were still legible. As I mastered the contents, my amusement left me, and I confess that I sat up straighter in my chair.

The letter was from a certain Count de Marny—at least, that was the name he chose to sign—whom I had met some years before at a mission station on the Zambesi. He was one of your restless gentlemen of birth, such as Europe seems to breed at times when she has no wars on hand with which to occupy them. I remember him as a tall, pale man with a trick of looking past one, as though at something a thousand miles away, and with eyes that would light up most unpleasantly when the talk chanced upon danger or difficulty. We had shared a certain small business together among the Barotsi, which is another story and not to be told here, and I had seen enough of him to know that, for good or evil, he was not of the common run of men.

Now he wrote to remind me of a promise which, it appears, I had either made or suffered him to put into my mouth—that if ever he should light upon a quest worth the seeking, he might send for me, and I would at least consider the matter. He declared that the quest had come. Somewhere beyond the farthest limits of the known Portuguese territories, behind a line of blue mountains which the natives named, in their tongue, “The Place-Where-the-Sky-Falls,” there was—if their tales were to be believed—a hidden valley, very ancient and very holy. In this valley, he said, there grew a certain Tree—no common thing of bark and leaf, but a living relic of times before man trod the earth; a Tree that glowed faintly at night, like a coal upon a dark hearth, and bore upon its topmost branches blossoms of such whiteness that to look upon them too long was to grow faint and blind.

“This Tree,” he wrote—for I remember the very words—“is the last of its kind, the Mother of Flowers, worshipped as divine by the people of that valley, and guarded with a jealousy that has already cost more than one life. I have sworn an oath to behold it with my own eyes, and, if I may, to bring away one blossom from its crown. For such a journey, my friend, I know but one companion fit, and his name is Allan Quatermain.”

The rain hissed more sharply on the window. I laid down the letter and stared into the lamp-flame, thinking of many things—of the quiet grave where my poor wife slept under the red soil of the Transvaal, of comrades dead far up-country, of the white tusks piled in trading-houses that had once been wild bulls crashing through thorn. I was growing old; the bones ached somewhat in damp weather, and the world was changing fast about me, not for the better, as it seemed.

Yet, as I sat there, I became aware of a curious stirring at my heart, like the first faint beating of a drum very far away. It was the old call—that of the untrodden path and the blue line of hills beyond which lies one knows not what.

author: Nathaniel Hawthorne
title: Nathaniel Hawthorne
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Nathaniel Hawthorne's Nathaniel Hawthorne. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
In that dim and not altogether reputable quarter of our ancient New England seaport, where the tide, in sullen intimacy with slime and refuse, creeps twice in every four-and-twenty hours beneath worm-eaten wharves and along the mouldy foundations of rotting warehouses, there stood, until within the memory of men not yet gray, a house of such peculiar aspect that the common people, to whom wonder is as needful as bread, had long since endowed it with a baleful consciousness. They spoke of it, one and all, not as a dwelling, but as “the Old Watcher,” and would as soon have jested with a funeral procession as with that gloomy edifice, which, in its own grim fashion, seemed always listening, always remembering, and never, by any mortal solicitation, induced to forget.

It fronted a narrow and much-trodden lane, paved scantily with disjointed stones, between which the tough grass of many summers thrust itself like obstinate recollections that no prudence can entirely uproot. Above the lane, a tangled skein of clothes-lines, stretched from gable to gable, fluttered at all seasons with such garments as the poor contrive to interpose betwixt their nakedness and the world’s cold scrutiny. Children, too ragged to fear further tatters, played riotously there, though never so near the threshold of the Old Watcher but that the boldest among them would, by an instinct as old as humanity itself, abate his clamor and cast an uneasy glance toward its darkened windows.

These windows—small, square, deep-set—were not deficient in number, yet contrived, with a sort of malignant thrift, to emit far less light than any reasonable portion of glass and atmosphere might be supposed to afford. The panes, begrimed by many winters’ coal-smoke and by no summer’s careful hand, reflected the world without in broken and dingy fragments, as a sullied conscience will give back only a distorted and melancholy image of things once bright and innocent. The house had, in a former and more decorous age, been coated with a sober hue of yellow, but this, under the incessant discipline of east-wind, sea-salt, and neglect, had faded into a sickly pallor, streaked and blotched as if some contagious malady of the moral nature had crept over it, beginning, perhaps, in one guilty chamber and spreading thence, by slow degrees, to every beam and shingle.

Yet it must not be supposed that the Old Watcher owed its sinister celebrity merely to the ravages of time or the disadvantage of an ill-favored situation. Time, which mellows honest structures into venerable grayness, had here sharpened every angle and deepened every shadow. The very stoop, with its sunken steps, had a look of reservation and stealth, as if it knew more of the feet that had passed over it than it had any intention of revealing. The brass knocker—curiously wrought in the semblance of a hand tightly clenched upon an unseen scroll—had been, in that earlier day when merchants of substance and magistrates of authority frequented the lane, polished to a decent brightness; it now wore the dull and sullen aspect of an object accustomed to be avoided, rather than employed, by those who passed.

There were old wives, and not a few grave men moreover, who would aver, with a seriousness born less of superstition than of half-remembered facts, that the house had once, in the stern period of our Puritan ancestry, sheltered a magistrate whose zeal for righteousness outran, by many furlongs, his charity for the frailties of flesh and blood. Under his threshold, it was whispered, innocence and guilt had alike been made to tarry, while the scales of judgment, held in an unrelenting hand, inclined heavily—though, whether to the side of mercy or of doom, the dim traditions dared scarcely say. Certain it is that from his time, or near upon it, the Old Watcher began to gather, as a garment that none may shake off, that brooding air of conscience ill at ease, with which it confronted every idler’s gaze down to the afternoon—not so remote from our own—when a stranger, bearing with him the odors of distant seas and the weariness of farther-travelled thoughts, paused before its portal, as one to whom a destiny, long delayed yet never escaped, had at last distinctly spoken.

author: P. G. Wodehouse
title: The Man with Two Left Feet, and Other Stories
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of P. G. Wodehouse's The Man with Two Left Feet, and Other Stories. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
Of all the melancholy spectacles which this world of ours, in its leisure moments, contrives to exhibit, commend me to the sight of a young man in love who is also in lodgings. He is handicapped at both ends. Providence has designed him for chivalrous antics with moonlight and roses, but Fate has shackled him to the gas-meter.

Consider, if you will, the case of Clarence Tuttle.

Clarence, on that particular Tuesday morning when the curtain rises upon this veracious narrative, was seated at his breakfast-table in Bingley Street, W.1, contemplating a bloater with the eye of one who knows he is being imposed upon by the social system and is not going to stand it much longer. The bloater—thin, pallid, and bearing throughout the unmistakable imprint of Economy—gazed back at him with the detached resignation of one long used to struggling against impossible odds.

Between them, on a cloth which had aspirations to be white if life would only give it a chance, reposed a portion of butter that had plainly lost heart, a loaf of bread that appeared to have been on active service in the late war, and an egg.

The egg, to be fair, might have been worse. In the hierarchy of eggs it ranked perhaps above the criminal classes but was certainly no more than a respectable artisan.

Clarence, lighting a cigarette with the air of a man who has weighed life in the balance and found it extremely light in places, pushed his plate away and addressed the universe.

“Dash it,” said Clarence.

The universe, being used to these outbursts from Bingley Street and several adjacent thoroughfares, made no comment.

It should be mentioned at once, in justice to Clarence, that his language, though strong, was justified. He was in love, as has been hinted, and deeply; his landlady, a woman with the soul of a Prussian General Staff, had raised the rent; his tailor, a person of gloomy imagination, had begun to speak of “steps” which he would be reluctantly compelled to take; and that very morning the postman, with the brutal insensibility of his profession, had brought him a letter from Aunt Emily.

To the casual observer Aunt Emily’s handwriting was merely firm. To Clarence it always suggested one of those notices you see on wire fences, “Trespassers will be prosecuted.”

He slit the envelope and found his worst fears richly rewarded.

“CLARENCE,” ran the epistle, omitting all softening preliminaries, “I am returning to England next week and shall expect you to meet me at Victoria Station, Wednesday, 3.45. Your last letter was disgraceful. It was bad enough that you should waste your time in town, but that you should talk of ‘literary work’ is simply ridiculous. If there is one thing I object to more than another, it is literature. I have never known any good come of it. Your Uncle Frederick once wrote a sonnet and has been peculiar ever since.

“I shall discuss your future when I arrive.

“Your affectionate,
“EMILY TUTTLE.”

Clarence read this carefully through twice, as a man will with a document that has about it the chill suggestion of the scaffold.

“Discuss,” he repeated, in a stricken voice. “That means she’s brought the axe.”

He got up and walked to the window. Outside, Bingley Street sprawled under a pale, uninspired sun, looking—as Bingley Street always looked—like a bad attack of bricks. A costermonger was passing, urging on his barrow with the kind of rich, fruity vocabulary that auntly ears would undoubtedly have described as “low.” A piano-organ somewhere in the middle distance was rendering a popular melody in a manner that suggested it had never really approved of the tune and didn’t see why it should pretend.

To Clarence, gazing upon this panorama of metropolitan despair, there came suddenly the full realization of his position. On the one hand, Aunt Emily, approaching England like a punitive expedition. On the other, Angela Devereux, with her golden hair, her trusting blue eyes, and a father who regarded young men with small incomes as Nature’s one serious mistake.

“Something,” said Clarence to the gas-meter, “has got to be done.”

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
title: A Child's Garden of Verses
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Robert Louis Stevenson's A Child's Garden of Verses. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
When I was little—though I am not so very big now—I used to think the world began at our front gate and ended where the hill turned blue against the sky. All that lay between was my kingdom, and I was its only prince. I had a crooked stick for a sword, a rusty tin lid for a shield, and a cap with a button missing that served me very well for a crown. It is a strange thing to be a prince and still be told to wash your hands before tea.

Our house stood at the very edge of the town, where the stone pavement gave up trying and melted into ruts and dust. It had three chimneys like three old gentlemen in hats, and windows that blinked when the sun was strong. Behind it ran a narrow garden-path, and at the back of that a low, red wall, and beyond the wall the fields, and the hedge, and the sky that never quite sat still but went wandering off to other places.

Every morning, when Mother opened the curtains and the light scrambled in, I felt as if the day had been waiting all the night through, just for the pleasure of surprising me. The shadows still dozed under the chairs, and the clock ticked very loudly because the house was not yet fully awake. I would lie in my bed and name the things I meant to do before the sun went down: there were battles on the lawn, voyages in the rain-barrel, and terribly perilous expeditions under the dining-room table. For there are countries to be found in places where grown folk never think of looking.

To you, perhaps, a chair is only a chair, stout and wooden, with four straight legs and a seat that squeaks. To me it was the bravest sort of horse, that would carry me clattering across deserts of carpet and through jungles of table-cloth without ever once losing its temper. The great oak wardrobe in the corner of my room was not, as Father said, “just for clothes.” On the inside of its door, if you shut yourself in very quietly and held your breath, there came a faint, far-away smell of rain and boats and the sharp, green air of hills I had never climbed. The coats hung there like patient, friendly trees, and the black Sunday one was always the tallest and most gloomy of the forest.

I had a window that looked upon the garden and the little path that ran between the flower-beds. The path never seemed to be in a hurry, but strolled along, turning its corners as if it had all the time in the world. In the mornings the grass glittered with beads of wet, finer than Mother’s necklace; and the sparrows hopped about, each thinking himself very important and very busy. They quarrelled a great deal, which made them seem more like people we knew.

Beyond the garden was the red wall, and beyond the wall was Adventure.

The wall was not so high that a proper prince could not climb it; but it was just high enough that I could not see what lay on the other side unless I stood on tiptoe and made myself very tall indeed. On days when the sky was clear and the clouds sailed slowly like ships that had quite forgotten where they meant to go, I would watch the top of that wall and wonder if the world went on, or if it stopped there like a shelf with nothing beyond but air.

Sometimes, when the wind was in a talking mood, it brought over the wall the smell of wild things and wet earth, and once or twice the far-off bleating of a sheep, so small and thin it might have been a dream. Then I was sure that something was waiting for me out there, something grand and strange that did not yet know my name but would be pleased when it learned it.

This book is the story of how I climbed that wall, and of the fields, and the hedge, and the sky that never kept still; and of how the world, which I had thought began at our front gate and ended at the hill, turned out to be much larger, and much more like a picture-book that had lost its last page.

author: Willa Cather
title: Alexander's Bridge
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Willa Cather's Alexander's Bridge. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
It was on an autumn afternoon, clear and a little chill, that Henry Vardon first saw the town from the highroad. The air had that thin, bright quality which makes distances seem at once nearer and more mysterious. The river shone and flashed in long, level glints between the yellowing poplars, and beyond it the rows of grey warehouses and the clustered chimneys rose like a low, jagged wall before the pale horizon.

He had come to the curve of the hill where the road turns suddenly and drops toward the bridge. There he checked his horse and let him stand, blowing steam in faint, shredding wreaths. Henry sat looking down upon the town which, twenty years before, he had left behind him, meaning never to come back except, perhaps, as a name in some printed report. Now, in the candid afternoon light, it lay spread out under his eyes, not perceptibly changed, not visibly awaiting him, as calm and self-absorbed as if he were some casual traveller whose coming would leave no more trace than the passing of the thin October clouds.

That was the disconcerting thing about it, he reflected,—the way a place could take no account of one’s absence, of one’s successes and shames. You might go away and build tunnels in the Andes, steel skeletons over Western rivers, you might talk before committees in Washington, be quoted in the engineering journals of three continents, and still the little town where you had first learned your figures, and first told a lie, would sit and smoke by its river, patient, incurious, as if you had merely walked out of sight behind the mill and might at any moment emerge again, a boy with a slate under his arm.

From where he sat, Henry could see the old stone bridge that had been the pride of the county in his childhood. Its three arches, heavily buttressed and patched with many years of rough mending, threw crooked shadows upon the slow water. A wagon rattled over it, the sound coming up to him distinctly in the clear air. Near the farther end a flock of sheep was halted, their dingy backs massed in a soft, wavering heap, while a boy on the parapet flung pebbles and shouted hoarsely. The scene was as ordinary as the ticking of a familiar clock, and yet it stirred in Henry a faint, almost impersonal irritation, as if he had discovered some old suit of clothes, carefully brushed and folded, laid away in a drawer, waiting for him to put it on again.

He touched the horse lightly and went on down the hill. As the road sank, the town rose, losing that flattened aspect it had worn from above and taking on substance and detail. Roofs detached themselves, windows caught the light, thin pillars of blue smoke went up from scattered chimneys. Here and there new houses had been run up, bristling with sharp, unweathered angles. A wooden water-tower jutted above the freight yards like an awkward, unfinished thought. Yet for the most part the place had held jealously to its old outlines, as if time had passed with a kind of grudging respect.

When he reached the bridge, Henry drew rein again and leaned a little sideways in the saddle, looking down over the parapet. The water, clear and shallow just here, slipped noiselessly over a ribbed bed of sand and stone. He could see the oval shadows of the arches resting upon it, solid and unmoved, while the current slid through and was gone. He smiled, not altogether pleasantly. That was the trouble with good masonry: it remembered too much.

author: Alexandre Dumas
title: Celebrated Crimes (Complete)

gpt_opening:
Towards the close of the eighteenth century, when the throne of France, eaten away at its foundations, still preserved the gilded semblance of strength, there occurred in a quiet province an affair so obscure in its origin, so bloody in its development, and so disastrous in its consequences, that the people, who are not given to subtleties of jurisprudence, accepted the verdict of rumor as more infallible than the decisions of the courts, and inscribed another name upon that dismal roll where the condemned of opinion are often more numerous than the condemned of law.

It is this affair which we shall narrate.

We do not pretend to decide whether the man whose history we undertake was victim or monster. The tribunals spoke; God has judged; time, which effaces blood from the pavement, does not always efface it from the memory of man. Between these three powers—earthly justice, divine justice, and popular remembrance—we take no side. We are historian, not advocate. If sometimes indignation escapes our pen, if sometimes pity breaks the cold chain of narration, our reader will pardon us: the man who could remain always impartial before certain spectacles would be less than historian; he would be less than man.

The little town of Saint-Pardoux, seated like a brooding hen at the junction of three roads, possessed in that year 1787 but one notable house, and that house belonged to the de Varennes. The château—for so the peasants named it, more out of respect for the family than admiration for the building—was a long, grey structure, whose single tower, square and sullen, rose at the corner like a clenched fist. The moat had long since been filled in, the drawbridge replaced by a stone causeway, yet the great iron hooks which had once supported the chains still thrust their rusted jaws from the wall, as if regretting the age of feudal insolence.

The last of the de Varennes, Jean-Antoine, was a man of thirty-six or thirty-eight years, though his face, prematurely lined, might have added a decade to that count. He had neither the splendid vices of the great lords of the court, nor the rustic virtues of the peasants who bowed before his carriage; he stood, like his house, between two epochs, and belonged entirely to neither. His father, a colonel under Louis XV, had impoverished the estate by purchases of glory, which, like all commodities sold at Versailles, cost much and returned nothing. His mother, sprung from the petty nobility of the robe, had brought him that restless ambition which dreams of letters when it cannot attain power, and writes pamphlets when it cannot make laws.

Thus, when we meet him, Jean-Antoine reads Voltaire in the morning and hears mass in the evening, argues against the nobility in his library and defends its privileges in the parliament of Limoges. He is harsh to his tenants and generous to beggars; he lends money at usury to ruined gentlemen and gives dowries to peasant girls. One would search in vain for a single straight line in this broken character; everything in him is angle, contradiction, or compromise. In tranquil times, such men pass through the world without leaving more trace than a fish in water; but when the storm rises, they are struck by the first lightning.

On the 14th of May, 1787, at five o’clock in the morning, the stillness of Saint-Pardoux was broken by the furious gallop of a horse, which stopped short before the door of the château de Varennes. A moment later, a cry rang out within the house—a cry so sharp, so desperate, that the oldest servant crossed himself and muttered:

“Again! It is beginning again!”

What struck him, afterward, was that he had from the first been aware of something strained in the daylight, something unnaturally solicitous in the very air of the great room, as though the high windows and tall mirrors and deep-toned panels had conspired to present him with a scene rather than contain a simple human hour. He was late, of course, by his own measure, which had always been a little out of step with the punctual sequences of other people’s afternoons; and he came in with that odd sense—rare with him, and therefore the more marked—that delay had not merely altered the situation but had, in some obscure fashion, created it.  

The place had at once the amplitude and the hush that attended all old Parisian salons still inhabited by a remnant of faith in their original significance. Chairs had been arranged and promptly deserted, the tribes of shawls and gloves and ivory-handled fans were assembled as if for inspection, and people talked, it struck him, from positions expressly calculated to show their profiles in the long lunettes of the mirrors. He had been there before, returning at intervals decided less by desire than by a sort of reluctant curiosity—as one goes back to peer again at a familiar picture that has never quite disclosed its puzzle. Yet on this day the scene appeared unusually composed, expectant, as if he had entered on a pause so complete that any movement of his might be taken for an initiative.  

He stood, for an instant, just inside the door, noticing with his habitual slowness—he had often reproached it as the very agent of missed opportunities—the trellis of folded conversations, the sudden lapses into silence as a servant crossed the carpet, the way in which the little groups, breaking and recombining, traced the faint geometry of old allegiances and forgotten affronts. It was really only afterward, turning the afternoon over in the tedious mill of his review, that he recognized this whole apprehension of pattern as the first clear note of what the day was to become: the day, as he afterward named it, in which everything that had been merely felt as possible arranged itself as inevitable.  

She was not at once distinct to him; or rather, she presented herself at first less as a person than as the sudden consciousness that a person had taken up a place long vacant in his mind. It was as though a chair that had always been empty, and must, one had supposed, remain so by some law of the room, were discovered to be quietly occupied, without anyone’s having seen the newcomer enter. Yet when his glance, traveling with what he later thought an indecorous haste, settled and fixed, he recognized that he had, in the way of men who carry their past about them like an old coat to which they have ceased to attend, already made her acquaintance.  

The surprise lay not in her presence, but in the uncovering of his own remembrance. That he had seen her before, years earlier, on some other afternoon disposed with the same deliberate light and the same hovering of talk, came to him less as a recollection than as an accusation. It was not she, after all, who had been absent; it was he who, in the interval, had contrived so successfully to absent himself from his own experience. The effect of this discovery was so sharp that he changed his direction in the room without knowing that he did so, the instinct of flight and that of approach operating, as they sometimes perversely will, to bring him nearer to the very object he might, had he been frank with himself, have declared he wished to avoid.

author: P. G. Wodehouse
title: Right Ho, Jeeves
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of P. G. Wodehouse's Right Ho, Jeeves. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
I am not, as a rule, one of your brooding, introspective Johnnies. The world, in my opinion, is a decentish sort of show, provided one keeps it supplied with an adequate flow of breakfast and tobacco, and I have always felt that if one can totter to the window, perceive that the sun is more or less up and about, and ascertain that the old lungs are still drawing the regulation quantity of air, there is really nothing much to complain of.

Still, there come days in the life of every man when he is compelled to take a strap down Memory Lane and ask himself, in a quiet, reasonable spirit, how the blazes it all happened. I chanced upon one of these soul-searching occasions on a brightish Thursday in June, immediately after choking to death on my own toast.

It is extraordinarily difficult, I should imagine, to pass from this world with dignity while engaged in barking and gargling like an elderly hyena. Yet this, I discovered, was precisely what I was attempting to do somewhere between the marmalade and the second cup of coffee. Recollection grows hazy here, but my impression is that the eyes protruded, the ears went puce, and the general Wooster façade assumed the appearance of one who has just found a hippopotamus in the bath.

“Water—glug—water, Bingham!” I croaked.

“Certainly, sir,” said Bingham.

Bingham is my gentleman’s personal gentleman, valet, counsellor, and general keeper in the zoo of life. He drifted in, apparently from nowhere, carrying, not the life-restoring tumbler I had so reasonably requested, but the _Times_ on a salver, like one presenting a lady of title at Court.

“Don’t just stand—gurk—there!” I endeavoured to articulate, indicating by means of frantic semaphore that the lungs were staging their farewell performance.

“Very good, sir,” said Bingham, in that quiet undertaker’s tone of his, and handed me a napkin.

Only after my features had assumed a shade of mauve rather favoured by certain tropical fruits did he relent, produce the desired H₂O, and apply those mysterious remedies of his. A sharp tap between the shoulder-blades, a discreet tweak of the neck, and the next thing I knew I was reclining in my chair, eyes streaming, chest pumping, but otherwise as nearly restored to the old joie de vivre as any man could be with half-chewed toast distributed about his interior.

“I took the liberty of removing the offending crumb, sir,” said Bingham, adjusting the morning coat at the shoulders. “I trust you are now comfortable?”

“As comfortable,” I said feebly, “as a man who has just seen his entire youth flash before his eyes can be. Bingham, if it had not been for you, the Wooster saga would have been cut short in its prime.”

“You are too generous, sir.”

“I am justly appreciative. A moment more and I should have passed to the Great Beyond, where the wicked cease from troubling and the good are forever being asked to play squash with one’s Aunt Agatha.”

“Indeed, sir,” said Bingham, not unkindly. “In that case, you will perhaps be relieved to learn that a letter has arrived from Mrs. Gregson.”

I fell back.

“From Aunt Agatha?”

“Yes, sir. It came by the early post. I took the liberty of placing it aside until after you had breakfasted. I apprehended that its contents might, to employ a vulgarism, put you off your feed.”

“Read it, Bingham,” I said, in a faint, martyred sort of voice. “If Fate has decided to tickle the Wooster ribs, let us get the worst over.”

“Very good, sir,” said Bingham, and began to read in the measured tones of one announcing the fall of a government.

author: Elizabeth Gaskell
title: Wives and Daughters
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Elizabeth Gaskell's Wives and Daughters. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
The little town of Hunningham lay, as it had lain for generations past, folded in a hollow of the low green hills that sloped gently down to the river Vare. A stranger, coming upon it suddenly from the turn of the high road, might almost have fancied it slept, so still and self-contained did it appear, with its grey church-tower rising modestly from amongst a crowd of red-tiled roofs, and the thin blue line of smoke that hung about them on calm mornings. Yet, for all its quietness, Hunningham was as much alive in its own fashion as any place of double its size; only its life went on with so regular and settled an order, that those who had been bred up in it could scarcely conceive of any other manner of existence.

The first house that attracted notice, as one descended the slope into the town, was the vicarage; and it was as proper and decorous a vicarage as the most fastidious patron could have desired. Its white front, with the dark ivy clinging closely round the parlour windows, looked towards the churchyard; while at the back there was a well-kept garden, not large, but filled with such flowers as are the peculiar joy of country clergymen—more remarkable for their sweet and wholesome familiarity than for any pretension to rarity or elegance. There was a trim border of thrift and London pride; there were bushes of sweet-brier under the study-window, which made the whole room fragrant in June; there was a straight walk leading to an arbour of clipped yew, under which one could sit in the warmest weather without so much as freckling the skin.

In this house, and under its modest roof, lived Mr. Latham, the vicar of Hunningham. He had been there five-and-twenty years, long enough to see little children grow up into men and women, to baptize, marry, and bury within the same families, and to feel that peculiar sense of quiet proprietorship in the joys and sorrows of his parish which comes to no man who is perpetually changing his flock. He was a grave, still man, with a certain gentle obstinacy of character, which made him cling, perhaps, somewhat too fondly to old ways when newer and better presented themselves; but his heart was tender and kindly, and he loved his people after his own fashion, even when he thought himself bound, for their soul’s good, to thwart their wishes.

His only child, Clara, had never known any other home than this spotless vicarage, nor any other world than that small one which lay within a morning’s walk of it. To say that all her interests were bounded by Hunningham would be unjust to the little maiden’s lively imagination; for, like many whose bodily steps are confined within narrow limits, her fancy had learnt to make voyages of discovery on its own account. But her affections at least were all there—rooted in every grey stone of the church, every turn of the river, every cottage where she had carried broth and jelly, under her father’s direction, to old women who blessed her with trembling voices and watery eyes.

It is very certain that Clara, at sixteen, would have been pronounced by any fashionable critic to be a most unformed, unsatisfactory girl. She knew nothing of accomplishments, save such as she could pick up, in a desultory way, from an old spinet in the parlour, and from a pile of volumes which had come down to her mother from a musical aunt who had once nearly sung herself into a London success, and had quite sung herself into a decline instead. Clara drew ill and read eagerly, talked more than was quite becoming in one so young, and had a trick of looking straight into people’s faces when they spoke to her, which discomposed those who had anything to conceal, and was not altogether approved even by the innocent. Yet there was a sweetness in her manner, and a frank, affectionate sincerity in her speech, that went far towards reconciling most persons to her little defects; and if she was not a model young lady, she was at least a very loving and loveable girl.

On a certain soft April afternoon, when our story may be said to begin, Clara stood at the vicarage gate, shading her eyes from the low westering sun, and looking down the road which led to the high moor-country beyond the hills, with an eagerness that sat oddly upon one whose life was, for the most part, made up of very quiet expectations.

author: Edith Nesbit
title: Beautiful Stories from Shakespeare
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Edith Nesbit's Beautiful Stories from Shakespeare. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
When you have read stories of fairies and magicians, of talking beasts, and of kings who went disguised in poor men’s clothes, perhaps you have been told that such things are not true, and could never have happened in this sensible, every–day world of ours. Yet there once lived in England a man who, without wand or spell–book, could call up more wonderful things than all the wizards that ever were written of. This man was William Shakespeare.

He did not, so far as we know, turn pumpkins into coaches, nor lizards into footmen; he never sailed in an enchanted ship, nor dined at a table that spread itself; but with his pen and a few drops of ink he could build palaces bright as sunlight, and dark forests where strange creatures lurked, and stormy seas, and moonlit gardens, and battlefields where brave men fell. And, what is more marvellous than any of these, he could fill those palaces and forests and sea–coasts and battlefields with men and women and children who speak and feel and hope and fear just as we do, though they wore other clothes and lived in other days.

You may have seen an old mirror in some quiet house where the same glass has hung for a hundred years and more. If you look into it, you see your own face and the room behind you; but if you could command it, as queens in fairy–tales command magic mirrors, perhaps it would show you also the faces of those who looked into it long ago—ladies in brocades, and gentlemen with ruffles and swords, and little boys in jackets with rows of buttons down the front, and little girls with stiff, flowered gowns and long mittens, all looking into the same mirror in the self–same place, though so many years divide their time from yours. Shakespeare’s plays are like such a mirror. When you look in, you see yourself and your own happiness and troubles; but if you look again, with more patience and more love, you will see, besides yourself, those princes and beggars, wise men and fools, noble ladies and wicked queens, who have looked into that mirror of his since first he polished it with his thoughts and words.

Now, when Shakespeare wrote his plays, he did not think of little boys and girls reading them in printed books. They were meant to be spoken and acted upon a stage, with music and dancing, with bright garments and high–plumed hats, with trumpets and drums, and with such scenery as his time could give. So sometimes the story is hidden among long speeches, as a ripe nut is hidden in its rough shell; and because he wrote in the speech of his own day, some of his words have grown strange to us now, as old coins grow worn and dim.

Therefore these stories are written here in plain, simple English, that you may know what those plays are about; that you may follow the fortunes of the kings and queens and lovers and clowns whom Shakespeare set before the eyes of men; and that, when you are older and come to read his very words, you may find them, not like a dark forest where one loses the path, but like a familiar garden, where each tree and flower is already your friend.

author: Arthur Conan Doyle
title: Tales of Terror and Mystery

gpt_opening:
It was in the melancholy winter of ’99 that chance first led me to the acquaintance of Mr. Alaric Vane. I have known many curious men in the course of a somewhat wandering professional life—eccentrics, fanatics, and those touched, delicately or otherwise, by madness—but never one whose personality so subtly disquieted me, nor whose history, bit by bit extracted, has haunted so obstinately my sober, medical reason.

Until that season my practice had lain chiefly among the lowlier quarters of the town, where illness is common and payment exceptional. A modest inheritance, however, enabled me to remove to more reputable rooms in W—— Street, facing the river, and to cultivate a clientèle better suited, as my aunt was wont to say, to my “birth and faculties.” It was in these rooms, on a bleak and dripping afternoon, that Vane first presented himself.

I recall the hour distinctly. A premature darkness had settled upon the city, the lamps in the street showing dim and spectral through the ceaseless drizzle. The fire in my grate burned with a dull, resentful glow that gave more smoke than heat, and a curious lethargy—half weariness, half depression—had fallen upon me, so that I had almost determined to close my surgery for the day when the sharp, metallic jangle of the bell startled me from my chair.

“Show him in, Mrs. Partridge,” I called, when my landlady’s shuffling step announced an arrival. “Whoever comes now has earned his consultation.”

The door opened, and the man who entered seemed, in some indefinable manner, to bring the outer darkness with him. He was tall and very slight, his figure held with that languid rigidity which one sees sometimes in those long afflicted by some wasting disease. The light from the gas-jet fell upon a face extraordinarily pale, with cheek-bones sharply defined and eyes of a gray so light as to appear almost colourless. They were restless eyes, that glanced quickly to every corner of the room before settling upon me with a look of cold appraisal.

“You are Dr. Jonathan Hales?” he inquired, in a voice low yet perfectly distinct, every syllable shaped with fastidious precision.

“I am. Pray be seated, Mr.—?”

“Vane. Alaric Vane.” He laid a card upon the table with a movement that betrayed the tremor of his hand. “I am told that you are a man of discretion, doctor. It is on that account that I have ventured to seek your advice.”

I bowed without speaking. It has been my habit to allow my patients to approach the heart of their trouble in their own fashion. Yet there was something in this man’s manner—a mingling of nervous haste and proud reluctance—that piqued my curiosity more than I cared to admit.

He sat for a moment, his long fingers pressed together, his gaze fixed upon the fire. Then, as if with an effort of will, he turned those pale eyes upon me.

“You must indulge me,” he said abruptly, “if I begin at a point which seems to you remote from any medical complaint. What I suffer, Dr. Hales, has its roots less in the body than—elsewhere. And if you are to aid me at all, you must hear, from my own lips, the history of the thing that has followed me these fifteen years.”

“The—thing?” I repeated, involuntarily.

He smiled, a thin, mirthless contraction of the lips.

“You shall call it what name you please when I have done. For myself, I have exhausted the language of men upon it. It remains only… the Companion.”

He uttered the last word in so peculiar a tone—half mockery, half shuddering loathing—that, much as I pride myself upon my common sense, I confess I felt a faint stirring of uneasiness, as though some invisible draught had crept across the room and chilled the very marrow of my bones.

author: Herman Melville
title: Bartleby, the Scrivener: A Story of Wall-Street
prompt: Write the first passage of a book in the style of Herman Melville's Bartleby, the Scrivener: A Story of Wall-Street. Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
I am one of those men—if man I may be accounted—who, having long trafficked in the safer commodities of life, have insensibly contracted a taste for the quieter catastrophes of the soul. Shipwrecks, insurrections, bankruptcies, and all that noisy artillery of Providence, I leave willingly to the newspapers. The obscure foundering of a single heart, unheralded and unwept, has ever seemed to me more tragical than the blowing up of an armada. Perhaps this tendency in me, which others might call morbid, is but the natural result of certain professional habitudes, wherewith it may be well to acquaint the reader before proceeding to narrate the history of a man who, without ever raising his voice or his hand, overturned the peace of my office, and (what is more curious) unsettled, in some small degree, my established notions of the mercantile universe.

For upward of thirty years I have occupied a suite of chambers in the upper part of Cedar Street, looking obliquely, as it were, upon that great stone cataract of business which pours itself daily down Broadway. Mine is no splendid establishment. The ceilings are low; the windows stingily meted out by some architect of a thrifty turn; the stairway narrow and industrious of splinters. Yet there is a sobriety in those begrimed walls, an obstinate, smoky decorum in the very panes, that has always chimed agreeably with my disposition. I have never been a man for the flamboyant cornice or the marble step. Give me a sufficiently durable floor, a tolerable amount of daylight, and such four-legged furniture as will bear a not immoderate load of papers, and I am content.

In these rooms I prosecute what is commonly called a "safe" branch of the legal vocation. My clients are chiefly merchants of the more substantial kind; men whose forefathers trafficked in whale-oil and cod, and who themselves have descended, by gradual and decorous stages, into the trade of stocks, warehouses, and insurances. They are not imaginative men. They prefer bonds to poems and mortgages to metaphysics. Their consciences are, on the whole, no worse than their ledgers, which is to say they sometimes omit an entry, but rarely falsify a column.

With such persons I have dealt so long, conducting for them such portions of their affairs as can be transacted with ink instead of oaths, that I, too, have perhaps become safely inked over; not so much a man as a receptacle of documents, a sort of ambulatory pigeon-hole. This may appear an overstatement, but I am persuaded that whoever passes his days arranging the world into labeled packets will, in the end, begin to label himself and put himself away.

At the period to which I refer, my staff—if so dignified a term may be accorded to those faithful assistants who, like grand pianos in respectable parlors, are used chiefly for show and occasional company—consisted of three clerks and a lad. Of the former I might relate many small peculiarities, and of the latter, various boyish misdemeanors; but these would detain us from the more singular personage whose advent among us I am now desirous to chronicle. Let it suffice that the machinery of the office went on, if not harmoniously, at least with that regular, cough-laden wheeze which is the nearest approach to music attainable by business.

It was on a damp October morning—one of those mornings when the sky, like an overworked debtor, seems to have signed away all its brightness—that there came to my door a man whose quiet presence has ever since remained with me like a question to which I have not yet framed an answer.

The morning-room of Mrs. Fairfax-Langley’s town house in Grosvenor Square. A large window opening upon a balcony, through which an entirely respectable London light struggles optimistically against lace curtains. Comfortable, well-bred furniture that has never been surprised in its life. Tea-table. An air of cultivated idleness.  

REGINALD FAIRFAX-LANGLEY is discovered at the tea-table, not making tea with great deliberation. He is reading a letter and smiling with the industrious malice of a man who has nothing to do and does it extremely well.  

Enter LADY CECILIA ARBUTHNOT, beautifully dressed for the purpose of appearing as if she has been nowhere in particular.

REGINALD:  
My dear Cecilia, you are ten minutes late. I have already forgiven you. Please don’t spoil the effect by apologising.

LADY CECILIA:  
I could not apologise if I tried, Reginald. It always gives the impression that one might have done otherwise, and I cannot permit such a slander upon my character.

REGINALD:  
That is what I admire about you. Your remorse is entirely theoretical. Will you have some tea? I am brewing a perfectly undrinkable pot. It is the only domestic accomplishment that society allows a man, and it has naturally ruined my taste.

LADY CECILIA:  
No, thank you. I dislike tea on principle. The only people who ought to drink it are those who have nothing to say, and I would never rob them of their only excuse for remaining in the room.

REGINALD:  
You are all heart. Do sit down. You look delightfully agitated. It is such a relief. One gets so tired of seeing one’s friends perfectly composed; it suggests they have been rehearsing.

LADY CECILIA (sitting):  
I am not agitated, Reginald. I am interested. It is much more serious. Agitation implies feeling; interest only implies curiosity, and curiosity is the first duty of a civilised woman.

REGINALD:  
And the last virtue of a civilised man. What are you interested in?

LADY CECILIA:  
You, of course.

REGINALD:  
Then you must be in want of occupation.

LADY CECILIA:  
On the contrary, I am overwhelmed with engagements. That is why I have come to see you: I needed a rest. Besides, your mother told me you have done something disgraceful. It seemed only polite to call at once.

REGINALD:  
My mother has a most touching confidence in my capacity for wrongdoing. It is the only point on which she has ever encouraged me. What did she say?

LADY CECILIA:  
She said you have become—(with delicate horror)—*useful*.

REGINALD:  
Useful! My dear Cecilia, that is a monstrous exaggeration. I have merely become engaged.

LADY CECILIA:  
To be married?

REGINALD:  
I am afraid so. Society would never forgive me if I were engaged to be anything else. The modern world has reluctantly accepted electricity and democracy, but it still insists that an engagement must lead to a wedding or a scandal. It dislikes vague endings.

LADY CECILIA:  
You might at least have consulted me. I always regarded your bachelorhood as one of my few solid possessions. Who is the unfortunate girl?

REGINALD:  
Miss Honoria Blythe. You do not know her. She is entirely respectable; that is why I chose her. Respectability is the most daring form of eccentricity left to us.

LADY CECILIA:  
I have heard of her. She has principles.

REGINALD:  
Only temporarily, I hope. Marriage is the proper treatment for principles. It domesticates them.  

LADY CECILIA:  
And when is this calamity to take place?

REGINALD:  
As soon as Honoria has finished improving me. She believes in forming a man’s character before marriage. She says it saves time afterwards.

LADY CECILIA:  
How industrious of her. I have never believed in forming a man’s character; one is expected to live with it, not complete it.
