In the year when the new steamers first began to nose their way, with a sort of clumsy assurance, into the brown waters of the outer roadstead, I found myself, by one of those intricate arrangements of chance and indolence that pass among seafaring men for destiny, detained for many weeks in the port of San Miguel. It was a place of crumbling warehouses and glaring white dwellings set in a hollow of the barren coast, like chalky bones left uncovered by some ancient retreat of the sea. All day long a dry wind came downhill, hot and perfumed faintly with the acrid dust of the interior; all night long the tide crept up with stealthy perseverance, lapping against the rotten piles of the jetty where the cockroaches held obstinate possession.

The Company had its offices at the corner of the Plaza, in a house painted the colour of faded blood. There was a balcony staring stupidly on the square, and below, a flight of uneven steps at which the idle population assembled each afternoon to contemplate with equal apathy the passing of foreign sailors and the ceremonial progress of starving dogs. The manager, a hollow-eyed man whose neck seemed always on the point of escaping from his collar, received me there with an impatient civility, the resource of those whose minds are too occupied with profit or fear to find time for simple indignation.

“You may be here a fortnight,” he told me, drumming his fingers on the warped table. “You may be here two months. The lighter is smashed; the captain is drunk; the customs official has a fever, or pretends to have one. It is all the same. Nothing moves.”

He spoke with that peculiar air of disavowing responsibility which is the last privilege of authority in out-of-the-way places. I had come ashore from the barque *Leonora* with a letter for him in my pocket and a vague expectation of prompt dispatch in my mind. The *Leonora*, with her patched canvas and her damp-smelling hold, lay out there beyond the sand-bar, rolling to the long heave of the ocean, and I confess that, looking at the little town, its blind windows and blistered doors, I would have been glad to return on board that same evening. But I remained—because the manager shrugged his shoulders, because the tide was already on the ebb, and because the world, which is so large when one contemplates it from the height of youth, can become incredibly small under the shadow of obligation.

I took a room at the Casa Europa, an inn kept by an Italian whose white, heavily-ringed hands had never known any more arduous labour than the manipulation of a coffee-pot. The shutters of my room looked upon a narrow lane where a solitary palm leaned, as if in discouragement, over a ditch choked with refuse. Toward sunset, when the heat settled down in a visible haze upon the roofs and the voices in the street sank to an indolent murmur, I would sit by that shutter and watch the thin strip of sky grow gradually darker, wondering with a sort of resentful curiosity what power had flung me upon that indolent shore.

It was on one of those evenings, when the last light lingered behind the hill like a reluctant farewell, that I first saw Captain Riever.

Use approximately 500 words. Even if the first passage is in your training data, make sure not to copy it exactly; write a similarly-styled first passage yourself.
gpt_opening:
The history of humanity, when observed not through the spectacles of scholars but with the unarmed eye of the conscience, presents itself as an unceasing struggle between what people recognize as good and what, in fact, they do. From the earliest chronicles down to our own newspapers, we see the same sorrowful spectacle: men know the law of love, extol it in their temples, teach it to their children, inscribe it on their banners—and yet, in their daily dealings and in the solemn affairs of nations, they live according to a law of violence and deceit.

In no age has this contradiction been justified with such subtlety, and at the same time felt with such pain, as in our own. Never have the words of Christ been so widely disseminated; never have they been interpreted with so much learning; never have there been so many societies bearing His name. Yet never, perhaps, has the life of Christian peoples been so obviously in opposition to the very teaching they profess to revere. In every Christian land, power is maintained by institutions that rest avowedly on the threat and practice of physical coercion; fortune is accumulated by means that presume constant rivalry; education trains the young not for the fulfillment of the law of love, but for the contest of all against all; and, what is more grievous than all, this entire order of existence is held to be not only inevitable, but compatible with Christianity, and even demanded by it.

The ordinary man, oppressed by labor, taxes, and the perpetual anxiety of an insecure life, and seeing around him courts, prisons, factories, armies, and the glittering comfort of those who command him, experiences in the depth of his heart a confused protest. He has heard the words about the Father who is in heaven, about the brothers who are on earth, about the blessedness of the meek and of the peacemakers. He dimly feels that the life he is forced to lead, and the life he sees led by those above him, cannot be the life intended by that teaching. Yet when he turns for guidance to those who are supposed to interpret the doctrine of Christ—the priests, the pastors, the theologians—he is told that the order under which he suffers is ordained by Providence, that authority comes from God, that the inequalities and violences of the social structure are not merely tolerable for a time, but form part of the mysterious plan of redemption.

This contradiction between the teaching of love and the practice of violence is so glaring that it might seem it could not be endured for a single generation, much less for centuries. But it is endured because men have learned to consider Christianity, not as a law of life binding here and now upon every conscience, but as a collection of incomprehensible dogmas, promises, consolations, and ceremonies—helpful perhaps for the soul in another world, but not intended to govern the arrangement of this one. Thus they have been able, without renouncing the name of Christ, to fashion a civilization that rests on principles precisely contrary to His.

The purpose of the following pages is to show that this separation of Christian faith from human life is neither necessary nor innocent; that it arises, not from the nature of the teaching itself, but from deliberate distortions of it; and that, when the doctrine of Christ is taken in its plain, rational meaning, it presents itself, not as a mystical rite nor as a consolatory metaphysic, but as a new and strictly obligatory theory of life—one which demands a complete change in the foundations of individual and social existence.

It was upon the evening of the 3rd of November, in the year 1893, that the first shadow fell across my quiet and well-ordered life. I date the matter thus precisely, for from that hour the current of my days—hitherto smooth enough, if somewhat monotonous—was diverted into so strange and troubled a channel that I cannot recall it without a certain wonder, not unmingled with a vague dread.

At the time I was junior partner in the firm of Harcourt & Vale, Solicitors, of Lincoln’s Inn Fields. My habits were settled, my prospects good, my ambitions restrained within those narrow bounds which, though they do not stir the imagination, make for comfort and worldly success. My professional duties lay chiefly among leases and settlements, with such occasional incursion into the more thorny ground of Chancery practice as might fall to a younger man with patience and a clear hand. Of romance, mystery, or any of those more perilous interests that tincture some men’s careers, I had known nothing beyond the ordinary gossip of the town and the sensational paragraphs of the evening papers.

It was therefore with no expectation of aught uncommon that I sat that evening in my private room, finishing the draft of a will for a retired ship-chandler of Rotherhithe. The gas had been lit an hour, and made a hissing halo above my head, while outside, in the square, a dull November fog hung in heavy folds among the lamp-posts and about the bare limbs of the plane-trees. The clerks had gone some time before, and the outer offices lay in that peculiar silence which belongs to places of business after work is done—a silence that seems to retain, half-stifled in its dusty corners, the faint echo of many footsteps and many voices.

I remember that, as I wrote the final clause, a curious reluctance took hold upon me. There was nothing in the document itself to occasion it; yet my hand went slower, and my pen scratched uneasily along the page, as though the steel had grown suddenly dull. Once or twice I laid it down, looking about the room with a kind of impatient attention, as though I expected to discover there the cause of my momentary uneasiness. The green shade over the gas, the heaps of papers, the bookcase with its calf-bound volumes, the safe in the corner with its iron door ajar—these were as usual. Yet I felt, I know not why, that some unseen change had fallen on the familiar scene.

It was while I still hesitated, pen in hand, that I became aware of a sound which, at first so faint as to be scarcely more than a vibration in the air, gradually resolved itself into the measured tread of footsteps on the flags of the outer passage. They paused at the glass door of my room; I heard the handle turn slowly, as though the hand that moved it was either very feeble or very deliberate. Then the door opened, and a man entered.

He was, I judged, between fifty and sixty years of age, tall and singularly spare, with a stoop of the shoulders that gave him at first the appearance of great weakness; yet as he advanced the impression of feebleness passed, leaving in its place one of nervous energy, held in check rather than exhausted. The gas-light, falling full upon his face, showed a complexion of that sallow pallor which comes less from sickness than from long confinement and intense application. His hair, iron-grey and plentiful, was brushed back from a high, narrow forehead furrowed by many vertical wrinkles; his eyes, deep-set and of so light a grey as to seem almost colourless, had a fixed, unwavering look that I found, even in that first instant, strangely disquieting.

He closed the door behind him with care, and stood for a moment with his back against it, as though listening. Then, with a quick glance round the room, he advanced to my table and spoke my name:

“Mr. Arthur Vale?” he said.

title: The Mystery of the Sea

gpt_opening:
It was in the summer of my thirty-second year that, through a concurrence of small events such as might have passed unnoticed at any other time, I found myself drawn into that strange coil of circumstance which has ever since affected my life as the moon affects the tides. Looking back now, through the mists of memory and the darker vapours of what followed, I can see that there was nothing in those opening days to forewarn me of the shadow whose fringe was already stealing across my path. The sea was blue and benign; the air, soft with the breath of June, hung lightly over land and water; the very world seemed to rest, as though tired of its own turmoil and content for a little space to be quiet.

I had gone north, more in obedience to a whim than from settled purpose, to that iron coast where the shoulder of Scotland is thrust out against the North Sea in a sort of grim defiance. I had known the place but slightly in former years—no more than a passing traveller knows a wayside inn—yet, from the first, something in its aspect had taken hold upon my imagination. The grey cliffs, scarred with old convulsions; the long, resounding caverns wherein the waters mutter and boom with a voice older than speech; the black reef, which at low tide shows its cruel teeth above the surf—all these had, as it were, whispered to me of hidden things. There are localities which, apart from all association, have a personality; and this coast, stern and homely by turns, had sunk deep into my regard.

The little town of Kilmoray, huddled like a flock of sea-birds about its harbour, was at that time almost untouched by the newer commerce which has crept northward with the rail. True, a single line of steel, recently driven, linked it with the greater world; but the old spirit still held. The men were fishermen for the most part, brown and taciturn, and the women bore the look of those who have watched too often for boats that did not return. From the windows of the inn where I had taken up my quarters—a low, white house, with thick walls and deep-set casements—I could see the mouth of the harbour, and, beyond, the long, swinging line of horizon where sea and sky met in an alliance that seemed now tender, now treacherous.

I had come there, as I say, with no intention more serious than to breathe sea-air and to forget for a time the crowding cares of a London practice that had begun to weigh somewhat heavily upon me. My life hitherto had been ordered enough. Born the younger son of a country parson, bred in decent poverty and honest work, I had found in medicine a career which satisfied the practical side of my nature and left but scant leisure for indulgence of the romantic. Yet there had always been in me, hidden perhaps from my acquaintances and even, in part, from myself, a certain hunger for the unusual—for those borderlands of experience where the solid earth of fact shelves away into the dimmer regions of conjecture and half-light. It was this latent appetite which, when once the first steps had been taken, urged me on into paths from which a prudent man might well have turned back.

On the evening of which I now speak—the first of my stay in Kilmoray—I stood by the low wall that fenced the inn-garden from the cliff, and watched the dusk draw its veil over the sea. The tide was running out, and from far below there came up the sucking hiss of the retreating waters among the rocks, like the long indrawn breaths of some great creature that slept but ill. Here and there a late boat crept shoreward, her brown sail taking a last faint glow from the west; and on the height above the harbour the lamp in the little lighthouse sprang suddenly to life, a single, steadfast eye of yellow fire looking seaward.

As I leaned upon the stone and filled my lungs with the keen salt of the wind, I became gradually aware that I was not alone. There is a sense, quickened perhaps by solitude and the open air, by which we come to know of another’s presence before sound or sight has certified it. I felt, rather than perceived, that some one was standing a little space to my left, also gazing out upon the darkening water. When at length I turned, it was to see a figure which, though in no way remarkable at first glance, I was destined to remember with a clarity that time has done nothing to impair.

author: Oscar Wilde
title: The Happy Prince, and Other Tales

gpt_opening:
In a little city that had once been proud of its chimneys, but had grown ashamed of them when the fashion for smoke went out of use, there stood a clock-tower which had forgotten the time.

It was built of pale stone, the colour of old gloves, and in its youth it had worn a cap of copper that flashed like a mermaid’s tail whenever the sun looked at it. Now the copper was green and melancholy, as though the sea had abandoned it, and the stone had acquired that tired dignity which comes to people and buildings who have been admired too often and too long.

Around its four faces the tower carried great round clocks, one for each wind. The North clock, which had once been celebrated for its punctuality, now lagged three hours behind, because it thought it more aristocratic to be late. The South clock, on the contrary, had such a passion for arriving before anything was expected of it that it had already struck next Tuesday. The East and West disagreed with one another out of principle, and had not spoken in years. The hands trembled with important gestures, but they never pointed to the same thing at the same moment, which gave the townspeople a very modern notion of truth.

Children, who are cruel in their love of astonishment, adored the tower, because it told them that bedtime had already gone or had not yet arrived, according to their needs and moral courage. But sober merchants complained that they could not conduct trade with a clock that believed in imagination, and pious ladies whispered that it was scandalous for bells to ring in the middle of sermons merely because they were bored.

“It is a disgrace,” said the Mayor, who wore his chain of office even when he was in bed, for fear that his dreams would not recognise him. “No respectable city can go about not knowing whether it is breakfast or Judgment Day.”

He said this from the balcony of the Town Hall, which, not wishing to quarrel with the tower, modestly pretended to be falling down. The square below him was full of citizens, who were not at all certain whether they had been summoned to celebrate something or to protest against it, but had come out, as they always did, to see if other people would arrive.

Now, although the clock-tower had fallen into such shocking habits, there was once a time when it had been the pride of the whole city and believed firmly that moments were as precious as pearls. In those days it had counted the seconds like a miser, and its deep bronze bell spoke with such grave sincerity that even the sparrows paused to consider their conduct.

But that was when the Time-Keeper still lived inside it.

The Time-Keeper had not always been old, but he had always been thin, because he lived between minutes and could only eat what slipped through from one to another. How he came to dwell in the tower, why he loved the hours, and how the city lost him, is a story which must be told very carefully, for it concerns the one treasure that poor men and rich men possess in equal poverty, and in which they are all prodigal together.

The hamlet of Larkfield, lying as it did in a dip of the hills, might have passed for a place which had sunk there tired, and chosen never to rise again. Its roofs, a scattered handful only, showed like a cluster of old brown mushrooms from the distance of the turnpike-road above; and the smoke of its chimneys, when there was any, went up with a hesitating air, as though doubtful whether the world beyond the hollow would care to see it.

It was an autumn afternoon, when the sky, after long weeks of blue that had shrunk the rills to threads and burnt the meadows to a tired pallor, had at last bethought itself of tears. There had been rain in the morning—squalid, persevering rain that made every thatch in the village look as if it had been crying all night—and now the weather sat moodsome and undecided, like a woman between laughter and reproach. Grey clouds lay about the horizon in companies, and above them thinner veils were drawn across by the leisurely hand of the west wind. Here and there a rent in these draperies showed a dim argent, as of a tarnished silver disk rather than a sun.

Seen from the crest of Clay Barrow, which rose on the northern side like a rough rampart set there by Nature to keep out too much knowledge of the nineteenth century, Larkfield wore a look at once secret and self-sufficient. The elms that ringed it in, gaunt yet leafy still, were already touched toward their summits with a smokiness of yellow, as though the green there had been breathed upon by time and taken the sickly tint of old paper. Between the trunks, at irregular intervals, the white glimmer of a cottage-wall, the black gape of an open hovel-door, or the slant of a grey roof could be spied, like scraps of an old engraving half-effaced by thumb and weather.

At the lower extremity of the village, where the lane to Marsh Farm went curving away over a little bridge of three low arches, there stood the church of Larkfield, with its tower huddled down upon its nave as if in resignation to the smallness of its lot. Its stone had taken a lichenous complexion which varied from sooty olive on the north to a warm, though dirty, gold upon the south. At this temperate hour of the afternoon, the paling light slid along the churchyard-stones in such a way that the letters cut thereon—names of men and women who had once walked barefoot through the very mire now lying upon the ground—came into momentary legibility, and then went back again into the same general dumbness which is the final commentary upon local fame.

Close by the church, so close indeed that upon still evenings the rustle of the surplice as the parson went up into the pulpit might be heard by one standing at the inn-door, loomed the Lark and Plough. The inn was thatched, and the thatch advanced so far beyond the face of the upper story that the whole front of the house appeared a sort of shadow out of which windows and door looked shyly forth. Signs of a modest prosperity clung about the place as sparrows about the eaves: a row of bright pewter mugs within, that somehow contrived to flash themselves into notice at every opening of the door; a bench without, polished to a satin by the sliding and settling of rustic trousers; and an apple-tree, badly pruned but prodigal of fruit, which leaned affectionately across the low garden-hedge, dropping windfalls as largesse for such children as were nimble of hand and conscience.

On this afternoon the bench before the Lark and Plough was occupied by three men, their figures silhouetted against the dun radiance of the sky after rain. They were engaged, not in such talk as might have been overheard with profit by any passing legislator—for legislators seldom come that way—but in that slow, desultory interchange of remark upon weather, cattle, and character which, being the staple of country converse, shapes more destinies than it appears to touch.

To say that no one else in Larkfield was abroad would be to wrong both the curiosity and the necessities of its inhabitants. Smoke rose from one or two chimneys with a livelier gesture, signifying that tea was in the making; a child’s thin cry came and went like the squeak of a gate that does not quite shut; and somewhere in the obscurer part of the hamlet a dog barked with a conscientious regularity which hinted that a stranger had at last turned into the lane from the high-road.

There are, in this busy and bustling world of ours, certain persons who, by an odd conjunction of circumstances, are born, as it were, with a small travelling trunk already packed and labelled for misfortune; so that, whether they will or no, they are no sooner fairly ushered into existence, and have scarce had time to cry lustily, before Fate, that indefatigable chambermaid of human affairs, whisks them off, bag and baggage, upon some long, crooked, ill-arranged journey, with half the proper stages omitted, and none of the comfortable inns by the way. Such a person was Mr. Barnaby Rudgelet, and if the reader complain that this is a harsh beginning, and argues little for the consolations of the volume which it commences, the writer can only reply that he is but a humble reporter of facts, and that Providence—who, it must be admitted, can on occasions be as whimsical as the best of us—chose to write Mr. Rudgelet’s first chapter in a very crabbed and intricate hand.

Mr. Barnaby Rudgelet was born in the year—never mind what year, for if it be of consequence at all, the curious are politely referred to the parish register of St. Bartholomew-in-the-Wicket, where it may still be read (ink, like some reputations, fading in parts, but legible upon the whole). It was a period when the science of government was chiefly exercised in discovering how much could be taken from the people in taxes without actually stripping them bare in the streets; when the dignity of trade consisted in giving the smallest possible article at the largest possible profit; and when benevolence, which has since put on so many smooth faces and velvet garments, commonly walked abroad in a very threadbare coat, with elbows much in want of darning, and an empty stomach that might have advantageously changed places with many a full one.

His father, Mr. Jonas Rudgelet, was a man who deemed it the height of human wisdom to owe everybody, in order that everybody might be equally interested in his welfare; and who held, as an article of private faith, that a man who paid his way lost, at one stroke, his just influence over society. His mother, on the other hand, was a lady who considered gentility the beginning and end of all things here below: who would have cheerfully gone without a dinner to secure a new ribbon for Sunday; and thought herself amply repaid for a week’s fasting by an opportunity of remarking, in a genteel whisper, that beef was so very coarse a food, she could not imagine how people reconciled it to their feelings to see it upon their tables.

In such a household, where the creditors came as regularly as the milkman, and stayed at least as long; where the parlour chairs were always ready to be sent to the pawnbroker at the first approach of a quarter’s rent; and where the family pride, like a distressed gentlewoman, contrived to look respectable upon the shabbiest of gowns; in such a household, I say, was our hero cradled, rocked, and very imperfectly fed.

The house itself stood in a lane which, without being precisely a thoroughfare, was, like certain modest reputations, so much used by back way that it might fairly have complained of being taken advantage of. It had a melancholy front of battered brick, a knocker that looked as if it had been many times arrested for debt, and windows which, from constant habit of being stared out of by curious neighbours, had acquired a furtive, half-ashamed expression, as though they had witnessed things (and they had) which it would not be delicate to mention.

It is no disparagement to Mr. Barnaby Rudgelet that he first opened his wondering eyes upon these objects; for there are very respectable people who begin life in worse places, and contrive, in spite of every disadvantage, to end it in considerably better ones. Whether he was destined to be of that fortunate number, these pages will, in due time and season, endeavour to relate.
